amazon-athena.text:
• Serverless interactive query service to analyze data in Amazon S3 using standard SQL
• No infrastructure to provision or manage — pay per query
• Built on Presto and Apache Spark engines
• Supports CSV, JSON, ORC, Avro, Parquet formats
• Pricing: $5 per TB of data scanned (columnar formats reduce cost dramatically)

• Use columnar formats (Parquet, ORC) — reduce data scanned by up to 87%
• Compress data: gzip, snappy, zstd, lzo — reduces storage and scan cost
• Partition datasets in S3 by date, region, or other dimensions — Athena skips irrelevant partitions
• Use larger files (>128 MB) to minimize overhead from S3 request overhead on small files
• Partition projection: define partitions in table properties instead of scanning S3 for them — faster queries
• Use CTAS (CREATE TABLE AS SELECT) to store query results as Parquet for future reuse

• Federated Query: query data in sources outside S3 using Lambda data source connectors
  - Sources: RDS, Aurora, DynamoDB, Redshift, CloudWatch Logs, on-premises DBs, HBase on EMR
• Results always stored in S3 (output location required)
• Integrates with AWS Glue Data Catalog as the metadata/schema store
• Integrates with QuickSight for visualization of query results
• Athena for Apache Spark: run Spark notebooks serverlessly without provisioning EMR

• Scenario: analyze VPC Flow Logs, CloudTrail logs, ALB access logs stored in S3 → Athena (no ETL needed)
• Scenario: one-time ad-hoc query on large S3 dataset → Athena (serverless, no cluster to spin up)
• Scenario: reduce cost of Athena queries → convert to Parquet + partition by date
• Scenario: query data across RDS + S3 in a single SQL statement → Athena Federated Query
• Scenario: business analyst needs to query S3 data without coding → Athena + QuickSight

• Max query timeout: 30 minutes
• Max concurrent queries: 20 per account (soft limit, can be increased)
• Query results retained in S3 output bucket — you must specify one
• TRAP: Athena queries S3 directly — it does NOT load data into a database first
• TRAP: Athena is NOT suitable for OLTP or frequent small queries — use RDS/Aurora for that
• TRAP: Athena charges per TB scanned even if query fails — always partition + compress
• TRAP: Glue Data Catalog must be set up to use Athena with structured tables

aws-data-exchange.text:
• Service to find, subscribe to, and use third-party data in the cloud
• Data providers publish datasets; subscribers access them directly via S3
• Integrated with AWS Marketplace for billing and licensing
• Supports data files, S3 datasets, Amazon Redshift datasets, and API datasets
• Eliminates manual data procurement, licensing agreements, and delivery pipelines

• Data products are published by providers and versioned as Revisions
• Subscribers receive new data automatically when providers publish revisions (auto-export to S3)
• Data can be exported to S3 then queried with Athena, loaded into Redshift, or processed by EMR/Glue
• Providers can restrict data by region, subscriber type, or usage agreement
• Supports: financial data, geospatial, weather, healthcare, demographic datasets from 300+ providers

• Scenario: company needs up-to-date financial market data without building ingestion pipelines → AWS Data Exchange
• Scenario: monetize proprietary dataset to external AWS customers → publish via Data Exchange on Marketplace
• Scenario: automatically receive updated third-party datasets into S3 daily → subscribe + auto-export to S3

• TRAP: Data Exchange is for THIRD-PARTY data — not for sharing data between your own AWS accounts (use RAM or S3 replication for that)
• TRAP: Data Exchange does NOT process or transform data — it only delivers it; use Glue/EMR after delivery
• TRAP: Billing goes through AWS Marketplace, not directly — providers set pricing terms

amazon-data-firehose.text:
• Fully managed service for loading streaming data into data stores — no administration required
• Near real-time delivery: buffers data before delivering (NOT real-time like Kinesis Data Streams)
• Auto-scales to match throughput — no shards to manage
• Formerly called Kinesis Data Firehose (renamed to Amazon Data Firehose in 2023)

• Supported destinations: Amazon S3, Amazon Redshift, Amazon OpenSearch Service, Splunk, Datadog, New Relic, Dynatrace, Coralogix, MongoDB Cloud, HTTP endpoints
• CANNOT write directly to DynamoDB, SQS, or SNS
• Lambda transformation: invoke Lambda to transform/filter records before delivery
• Format conversion: convert JSON to Parquet or ORC automatically (using Glue Data Catalog schema)
• Dynamic Partitioning: partition S3 output by custom keys extracted from records (e.g. by customerId, date)

• Buffer size: 1–128 MB (flush when reached)
• Buffer interval: 60–900 seconds (flush when reached — whichever comes first)
• Compression: gzip, snappy, zip, hadoop-snappy before S3 storage
• Encryption: KMS encryption before S3 delivery
• S3 backup: optionally send ALL raw records to a backup S3 bucket (even if transformation fails)
• Firehose delivery failure: failed records go to an S3 error prefix

• Scenario: stream clickstream data to S3 for later analysis → Firehose → S3 (serverless, near real-time)
• Scenario: ingest IoT data and store in Redshift for analytics → Firehose → S3 → Redshift COPY (or direct Redshift destination)
• Scenario: enrich streaming records before storage → Firehose + Lambda transformation
• Scenario: stream logs to OpenSearch for real-time dashboards → Firehose → OpenSearch
• Typical pipeline: IoT devices → Kinesis Data Streams → Firehose → S3 → Athena

• TRAP: Firehose is NEAR real-time (seconds of latency due to buffering) — NOT real-time; use Kinesis Data Streams for real-time consumers
• TRAP: Firehose does NOT support replay — once delivered, data is gone from Firehose (unlike KDS which retains data)
• TRAP: Firehose to Redshift: it actually delivers to S3 first, then runs COPY to Redshift — intermediate S3 bucket required
• TRAP: cannot write Firehose output directly to SQS, DynamoDB, or Lambda as a destination (Lambda is only for transformation)
• No storage layer — Firehose is a delivery pipe, not a buffer store

amazon-emr.text:
• Elastic MapReduce — managed cluster platform for big data frameworks
• Supports: Apache Hadoop, Apache Spark, HBase, Presto, Flink, Hive, Pig
• Runs on EC2 instances, EKS pods, or AWS Outposts
• Use case: large-scale ETL, log analysis, ML model training, genomics, financial risk modeling
• EMR Serverless: run Spark and Hive jobs without managing clusters

• Master node (Leader node): coordinates cluster, tracks job status — must always be running
• Core nodes: run tasks AND store data in HDFS — losing a core node risks data loss
• Task nodes: run tasks only — NO HDFS storage; safe to use Spot Instances here
• Single-node cluster: master node also acts as core node (dev/test only)
• HDFS: ephemeral — data lost when cluster terminates
• EMRFS: use S3 as durable persistent storage — data survives cluster termination (recommended for production)

• HDFS (local): fast, ephemeral — data lives on EC2 instance storage
• EMRFS (S3): durable, decoupled from cluster lifecycle — store input/output here
• Local file system: instance store — used for temporary buffers
• EBS: attach EBS volumes to nodes for additional HDFS capacity
• Best practice: read input from S3 (EMRFS), write output to S3, use HDFS only for intermediate shuffle data

• Use Spot Instances for Task nodes — they only run compute, no HDFS data at risk
• Use On-Demand for Master and Core nodes — to avoid data loss and job failure
• Auto Scaling: scale Core and Task node count based on YARN metrics
• Transient cluster: terminate cluster after job completes → save cost (vs long-running cluster)
• Instance Fleet vs Instance Group: Fleet allows mixing Spot + On-Demand with multiple instance types

• Scenario: process petabytes of log data with Apache Spark → EMR
• Scenario: reduce EMR cost for batch jobs → Spot Instances for task nodes + transient cluster + output to S3
• Scenario: run Hadoop jobs without managing cluster infrastructure → EMR Serverless
• Scenario: share data between EMR cluster and other services → store in S3 via EMRFS
• Scenario: real-time stream processing at scale → EMR with Apache Flink or Spark Streaming

• TRAP: HDFS data is LOST when cluster terminates — always persist to S3
• TRAP: Spot Instances on Core nodes = risk of data loss if interrupted — only use Spot on Task nodes
• TRAP: EMR is NOT serverless by default — EMR Serverless is the serverless option
• TRAP: EMR ≠ Glue — EMR = you define the Spark/Hadoop code; Glue = managed ETL with auto-generated code
• Master node failure = cluster failure — cannot recover without restarting the cluster

aws-glue.text:
• Fully managed serverless ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) service
• Automatically discovers and catalogs data schema via Glue Crawlers
• Generates ETL code in Python or Scala — can also write custom scripts
• Glue Data Catalog: central metadata repository used by Athena, Redshift Spectrum, EMR, Lake Formation
• Charged per DPU-hour (Data Processing Unit) for ETL jobs and crawlers

• Glue Crawler: scans data sources (S3, RDS, DynamoDB, JDBC, MongoDB) and populates the Data Catalog with table definitions and schemas
• Glue Data Catalog: acts as the Hive Metastore — one catalog per AWS account per region
• Glue ETL Jobs: PySpark or Scala Spark jobs that transform data between sources and targets
• Glue Job Bookmarks: tracks previously processed data to avoid reprocessing on re-runs
• Glue Triggers: start jobs on schedule, on-demand, or triggered by another job's completion
• Glue Workflows: orchestrate multi-step ETL pipelines with dependencies

• Glue DataBrew: visual no-code data preparation tool — 250+ transformations, no Spark knowledge required
• Glue Elastic Views: combine and replicate data across multiple stores using SQL (materialized views)
• Glue Streaming ETL: process real-time data from Kinesis Data Streams or Kafka — runs on Apache Spark Structured Streaming
• Glue Studio: visual drag-and-drop ETL job builder
• FindMatches ML Transform: de-duplicate records using ML without writing matching rules
• Dynamic Frames: Glue-native data abstraction similar to Spark DataFrames but schema-flexible

• Scenario: discover schema of data in S3 and make queryable with Athena → Glue Crawler → Data Catalog → Athena
• Scenario: convert CSV files in S3 to Parquet for cheaper Athena queries → Glue ETL Job
• Scenario: schedule nightly ETL to load S3 data into Redshift → Glue ETL Job with Redshift target
• Scenario: avoid reprocessing already-transformed files → enable Glue Job Bookmarks
• Scenario: business analysts need to clean data without coding → Glue DataBrew
• Scenario: stream data from Kinesis and transform before storing → Glue Streaming ETL

• TRAP: Glue Crawler does NOT modify source data — it only reads schema and writes to Data Catalog
• TRAP: Glue ETL ≠ EMR — Glue is serverless managed ETL; EMR gives you full cluster control
• TRAP: Glue Data Catalog is REGIONAL — one catalog per region per account
• TRAP: Glue does not natively support real-time sub-second processing — use Kinesis Data Streams for that
• Max DPU per job: 100 by default (soft limit)
• Job timeout default: 48 hours (configurable)

amazon-kinesis.text:
• Platform for real-time streaming data collection, processing, and analysis
• Four services:
  1. Kinesis Data Streams (KDS): real-time custom streaming
  2. Amazon Data Firehose: near real-time managed delivery to destinations
  3. Kinesis Data Analytics: SQL or Flink on streaming data
  4. Kinesis Video Streams: streaming video ingestion and processing

• Real-time streaming with millisecond latency
• Data retained: 1 day (default), up to 365 days (extended)
• Composed of Shards:
  - Write: 1 MB/s or 1,000 records/s per shard
  - Read: 2 MB/s per shard (shared across all consumers)
• Partition Key: determines which shard a record goes to (use high-cardinality keys to avoid hot shards)
• Sequence Number: unique ID per record within a shard

• Producers: AWS SDK (PutRecord/PutRecords), Kinesis Producer Library (KPL — batching, compression, retry), Kinesis Agent (log file collection)
• Consumers:
  - Standard (pull): 2 MB/s shared across all consumers per shard; polls every 1s
  - Enhanced Fan-Out (push): 2 MB/s per consumer per shard; uses SubscribeToShard (HTTP/2 push); higher cost
• Consumer types: Kinesis Client Library (KCL), AWS Lambda, Kinesis Data Analytics, Firehose
• KCL checkpointing: uses DynamoDB to track shard reading progress

• Provisioned Mode: manually set shard count; pay per shard-hour; scale by splitting/merging shards
• On-Demand Mode: auto-scales up to 200 MB/s write and 400 MB/s read; pay per GB ingested and retrieved; no shard management
• Use On-Demand when traffic is unpredictable; Provisioned when load is predictable and cost optimization is needed

• Run SQL queries or Apache Flink apps on real-time streaming data
• For SQL: input from KDS or Firehose; output to KDS, Firehose, or Lambda
• For Apache Flink: input from KDS or MSK; stateful stream processing; supports Java, Python, Scala
• Fully managed — auto-provisions Flink infrastructure
• Use case: real-time dashboards, anomaly detection, time-series analytics, ETL on streams
• Kinesis Data Analytics for SQL is being deprecated — Apache Flink is the recommended path

• Scenario: ingest millions of IoT events per second and process in real-time → Kinesis Data Streams
• Scenario: multiple independent apps consuming same stream with separate read rates → Enhanced Fan-Out
• Scenario: store streaming data in S3 with no custom code → Kinesis Data Streams → Firehose → S3
• Scenario: detect fraud in real-time from transaction stream → KDS → Kinesis Data Analytics (Flink)
• Scenario: log files from thousands of servers → Kinesis Agent → KDS → Lambda or Firehose
• Scenario: traffic is unpredictable, don't want to manage shards → KDS On-Demand mode

• Max record size: 1 MB per record
• Retention: 24h default, up to 365 days (extended retention costs extra)
• TRAP: Hot shard = bad partition key (e.g. using a low-cardinality key like device type) — use UUID or high-cardinality keys
• TRAP: Standard consumers share 2 MB/s per shard — if 5 consumers read same shard they each get ~400 KB/s; use Enhanced Fan-Out to give each consumer 2 MB/s
• TRAP: KDS does NOT auto-scale in Provisioned mode — you must split shards manually (or use On-Demand)
• TRAP: KDS ≠ SQS — KDS preserves order within a shard and supports replay; SQS Standard does not guarantee order
• TRAP: once data expires from KDS it cannot be replayed — retention period is key

aws-lake-formation.text:
• Service to build, secure, and manage a data lake in days
• Built on top of S3 (storage) and AWS Glue (cataloging and ETL)
• Centralizes data access control with fine-grained permissions
• Replaces complex combination of S3 bucket policies + IAM policies for data lake governance
• Free service — pay only for underlying S3, Glue, and other resources used

• Data Lake: centralized repository in S3 registered with Lake Formation
• Data Catalog: inherits Glue Data Catalog — stores table/database metadata
• Blueprints: workflow templates to ingest data from databases, log files into the lake automatically
• LF-Tags (Lake Formation Tags): attribute-based access control at scale — assign tags to databases/tables/columns, grant permissions by tag
• Data Filters: row-level and cell-level security filters applied to table access

• Grant permissions at: Database → Table → Column → Row → Cell level
• GRANT/REVOKE model: Lake Formation superimposes permissions on top of IAM
• Both IAM AND Lake Formation permissions must allow access — most restrictive wins
• Column-level security: restrict access to specific columns per user/role
• Row-level security: filter rows based on user identity (via Data Filters)
• Cross-account sharing: share Data Catalog databases/tables with other AWS accounts via RAM
• Governed Tables: ACID transactions and automatic data compaction (for streaming ingestion)

• Scenario: data lake on S3 where analysts can only see certain columns → Lake Formation column-level security
• Scenario: different teams need access to different subsets of rows in same table → Lake Formation row-level security
• Scenario: share curated data lake tables with partner AWS account → Lake Formation cross-account sharing
• Scenario: automate ingestion from RDS into S3 data lake → Lake Formation Blueprint
• Scenario: centralize access control for Athena + Redshift Spectrum + EMR on same S3 data → Lake Formation

• TRAP: Lake Formation does NOT replace S3 — it governs access TO the data in S3
• TRAP: IAM permissions are still required — Lake Formation adds another permission layer on top, not instead
• TRAP: Lake Formation permissions only apply to services that are integrated (Athena, Redshift Spectrum, Glue, EMR with EMR Record Server) — direct S3 access bypasses Lake Formation controls
• TRAP: Glue Crawlers must be granted Lake Formation permissions to catalog data in registered locations

amazon-managed-streaming-for-apache-kafk.text:
• Fully managed Apache Kafka service on AWS
• No need to manage Kafka brokers, ZooKeeper nodes, or upgrades
• Deploy Kafka clusters in your VPC across multiple AZs (Multi-AZ by default)
• Fully compatible with Apache Kafka APIs — no code changes needed for migration
• Data stored on EBS volumes with configurable retention

• Brokers: managed EC2 instances running Kafka; you choose broker instance type and count
• Topics: partitioned, ordered log of messages
• Producers → Topics → Consumer Groups
• ZooKeeper: managed by AWS (hidden); Kafka 3.x supports KRaft mode (ZooKeeper-less)
• MSK Serverless: run Kafka without managing broker capacity — auto-scales throughput and storage
• MSK Connect: deploy Kafka Connect workers fully managed — stream data to/from S3, Redshift, OpenSearch

• Message size: MSK default 1 MB, configurable up to 10 MB; KDS max 1 MB (hard limit)
• Retention: MSK — configurable, up to unlimited (disk-based); KDS — max 365 days
• Ordering: both preserve order per partition/shard
• Consumers: MSK uses Kafka consumer group protocol; KDS uses KCL or SubscribeToShard
• Protocols: MSK = Kafka protocol (open source); KDS = proprietary AWS API
• Migration: if you have existing Kafka workloads → MSK; greenfield → either works
• MSK requires VPC and broker management decisions; KDS is more fully abstracted

• Consumers: Lambda (event source mapping), Glue Streaming ETL, Kinesis Data Analytics (Flink), custom apps via Kafka SDK
• MSK Connect: S3 Sink, Redshift Sink, OpenSearch Sink connectors
• MSK Replicator: replicate topics between MSK clusters cross-region for DR
• Monitoring: CloudWatch metrics + MSK's open-source monitoring (Prometheus, Grafana via Open Monitoring)
• Authentication: IAM (recommended), SASL/SCRAM, TLS mutual auth, unauthenticated (dev only)

• Scenario: migrate existing on-premises Kafka workload to AWS → Amazon MSK
• Scenario: event streaming for microservices using Kafka APIs → Amazon MSK
• Scenario: stream Kafka topics to S3 without custom code → MSK Connect with S3 Sink connector
• Scenario: need Kafka but don't want to manage broker scaling → MSK Serverless
• Scenario: real-time stream processing on Kafka topics → MSK + Kinesis Data Analytics (Flink)

• Default max message size: 1 MB (vs KDS: 1 MB hard limit)
• Max message size (configured): 10 MB
• TRAP: MSK is NOT serverless by default — choose MSK Serverless for serverless Kafka
• TRAP: MSK runs in your VPC — you must manage security groups, subnets, and broker instance types (unless using Serverless)
• TRAP: MSK ≠ Kinesis — they are different protocols; you cannot use Kafka SDK against KDS
• TRAP: ZooKeeper is managed by AWS — you do NOT have direct ZooKeeper access in MSK

amazon-opensearch-service.text:
• Managed service for deploying, operating, and scaling OpenSearch clusters
• Successor to Amazon Elasticsearch Service (same service, renamed)
• Used for: full-text search, log analytics, real-time application monitoring, clickstream analytics
• Supports OpenSearch Dashboards (formerly Kibana) for visualization
• Automatically provisions, patches, backs up, and monitors clusters

• Domains: an OpenSearch cluster with its configuration, storage, and access policies
• Instance types: data nodes, dedicated master nodes, UltraWarm nodes, cold storage nodes
• Dedicated master nodes: improve cluster stability — handle cluster state management only
• UltraWarm: S3-backed warm tier for infrequently queried data — lower cost than hot nodes
• Cold storage: lowest cost tier — store and search infrequently accessed indices
• Multi-AZ: deploy across 2–3 AZs for high availability

• Kinesis Data Firehose → OpenSearch (built-in destination, managed delivery)
• CloudWatch Logs → Subscription Filter → Lambda → OpenSearch
• DynamoDB Streams → Lambda → OpenSearch (add search capability to DynamoDB)
• Logstash → OpenSearch (from on-premises log aggregators)
• S3 → Lambda → OpenSearch (batch index historical data)
• Direct SDK/API writes from application code

• VPC support: deploy within VPC for private access (no public endpoint)
• Fine-grained access control: IAM, HTTP basic auth, SAML, Amazon Cognito integration for dashboards
• Encryption: at rest (KMS) and in transit (TLS)
• Index-level, field-level, and document-level security
• Resource-based policies: control who can call the OpenSearch API

• Scenario: add full-text search capability to a DynamoDB-backed app → DynamoDB Streams → Lambda → OpenSearch
• Scenario: centralized log analytics for hundreds of EC2 instances → CloudWatch Logs → Firehose → OpenSearch
• Scenario: real-time analytics on clickstream data → Kinesis Data Streams → Firehose → OpenSearch
• Scenario: need Kibana-style dashboards for log data → OpenSearch Dashboards
• Scenario: search across large product catalog with complex queries → OpenSearch (vs DynamoDB which doesn't support full-text search)

• TRAP: OpenSearch is NOT a replacement for relational databases — it's an analytics and search engine
• TRAP: OpenSearch ≠ Athena — Athena queries S3 with SQL; OpenSearch indexes data and provides full-text search
• TRAP: data in OpenSearch is NOT durable by default like S3 — plan backup strategy using automated snapshots to S3
• TRAP: you cannot directly query S3 with OpenSearch — data must be ingested/indexed first
• Automated snapshots: daily to S3 — retained for 14 days

amazon-quicksuite.text:
• Serverless cloud-native BI (Business Intelligence) and visualization service
• Create interactive dashboards, graphs, and insights from multiple data sources
• Pay-per-session pricing: users pay only when they access dashboards (cost-effective for large orgs)
• No infrastructure to provision or manage
• ML-powered insights built in

• SPICE: Super-fast, Parallel, In-memory Calculation Engine
• Caches imported data in memory for sub-second query performance
• Each author gets 10 GB of SPICE capacity (additional capacity purchasable)
• Data can be imported into SPICE (from S3, RDS, etc.) or queried directly (live connection to Athena, Redshift, etc.)
• SPICE auto-refreshes on a schedule to keep data current
• TRAP: SPICE has a 500 MB limit per dataset import from some sources — check docs for limits

• Native AWS sources: S3, Athena, Redshift, Aurora, RDS, DynamoDB, OpenSearch, IoT Analytics
• SaaS sources: Salesforce, GitHub, Twitter, Adobe Analytics, ServiceNow
• On-premises: SQL Server, MySQL, PostgreSQL via direct connect or VPC
• File uploads: CSV, TSV, XLSX, JSON, ELF, CLF
• QuickSight Q: natural language queries — ask questions in plain English, get visual answers

• Users/Groups: manage at account level; Standard vs Enterprise edition
• Row-level security (RLS): define which rows each user/group can see using a dataset rule
• Column-level security (CLS): hide specific columns per user/group (Enterprise only)
• VPC connectivity: use QuickSight VPC connection to access private RDS/Redshift inside a VPC
• Private VPC access: QuickSight can reach private data sources via ENI in your VPC
• IAM integration: use IAM for QuickSight service permissions (S3, Athena, Redshift access)

• Scenario: business analysts need dashboards from S3 data lake → Athena → QuickSight
• Scenario: executives need real-time sales dashboards from Redshift → QuickSight (live query or SPICE import)
• Scenario: 10,000 users need to view dashboards but only 100 are active at a time → QuickSight pay-per-session pricing
• Scenario: allow different teams to only see their own region's data → QuickSight Row-Level Security
• Scenario: ask data questions in natural language → QuickSight Q

• TRAP: QuickSight does NOT store data — it connects to existing sources or imports into SPICE
• TRAP: QuickSight Standard vs Enterprise — Enterprise supports VPC connectivity, RLS, CLS, and Active Directory integration
• TRAP: QuickSight is NOT an ETL tool — use Glue for ETL before QuickSight visualization
• TRAP: SPICE data must be refreshed to reflect source changes — it is not a live view unless using direct query mode

amazon-redshift.text:
• Fully managed petabyte-scale data warehouse (OLAP — Online Analytical Processing)
• Columnar storage — optimized for aggregations and analytical queries
• Based on PostgreSQL — but NOT for OLTP (row-level transactions)
• Cluster architecture: 1 Leader node (query planning, coordination) + 1–128 Compute nodes (store and query data)
• Node types: dc2 (compute-dense, SSD), ra3 (managed storage, S3-backed — decouple compute from storage)

• Columnar storage + data compression: dramatically reduces I/O for analytical queries
• Massively Parallel Processing (MPP): distributes queries across all compute nodes simultaneously
• Result caching: cache query results on the leader node — identical queries return instantly
• AQUA (Advanced Query Accelerator): hardware-accelerated distributed cache — pushes compute closer to storage (ra3 clusters)
• Distribution styles: AUTO, EVEN (round-robin), KEY (by column value), ALL (replicate small tables to all nodes)
• Sort keys: COMPOUND or INTERLEAVED — pre-sort data on disk for faster range queries

• Concurrency Scaling: automatically add read cluster capacity during traffic spikes (pay per second of usage)
• Elastic Resize: quickly add/remove nodes (minutes); cluster briefly unavailable
• Classic Resize: change node type or large node count changes (hours/days); cluster is read-only during resize
• Multi-AZ (ra3): deploy across 2 AZs for HA — automatic failover
• Automated snapshots: every 8 hours or 5 GB of changes; retained 1 day (default, up to 35 days)
• Manual snapshots: retained indefinitely until deleted; can copy cross-region for DR

• Redshift Spectrum: query data directly in S3 without loading into Redshift — uses Glue Data Catalog for schema
• Spectrum scales compute independently of the cluster (separate Spectrum compute layer)
• Redshift Serverless: run analytics without provisioning a cluster — auto-scales RPUs (Redshift Processing Units)
• Use Serverless for: intermittent workloads, dev/test, unpredictable query volumes

• COPY command: most efficient bulk load method — loads from S3, DynamoDB, EMR, SSH
• COPY from S3 via Enhanced VPC Routing forces traffic through your VPC (required for VPC-only S3 access)
• UNLOAD command: export query results to S3 (Parquet or CSV)
• Kinesis Data Firehose: stream data directly into Redshift (via S3 intermediate step)
• Redshift Federated Query: query live data in RDS PostgreSQL/MySQL and Aurora without copying it in
• Redshift ML: CREATE MODEL in SQL → trains SageMaker model → invoke predictions in SQL queries

• Scenario: analyze terabytes of sales data with complex SQL aggregations → Redshift
• Scenario: query S3 data lake without ETL into Redshift → Redshift Spectrum
• Scenario: Redshift performance degrades at peak times → Concurrency Scaling
• Scenario: keep Redshift data in sync with RDS without moving data → Federated Query
• Scenario: run analytics but workload is intermittent → Redshift Serverless
• Scenario: replicate Redshift snapshots to another region for DR → cross-region snapshot copy

• Max nodes per cluster: 128
• TRAP: Redshift is OLAP — do NOT use for OLTP (high-concurrency row-level transactions) — use RDS/Aurora instead
• TRAP: Redshift is single-AZ by default (ra3 Multi-AZ is a newer feature — verify if question specifies ra3)
• TRAP: Redshift Spectrum requires a Glue Data Catalog or Redshift external schema — not automatic
• TRAP: COPY command requires proper IAM role attached to the Redshift cluster to read from S3
• TRAP: Redshift does NOT support full-text search — use OpenSearch for that

amazon-eventbridge.text:
• Serverless event bus for building event-driven architectures
• Successor to CloudWatch Events — same underlying service, same API, extended capabilities
• Decouples event producers from event consumers
• Three event bus types:
  1. Default: receives events from AWS services automatically
  2. Custom: receives events from your own applications
  3. Partner: receives events from SaaS providers (Zendesk, Datadog, Shopify, etc.)

• Rules: match incoming events using event patterns and route to one or more targets
• Event pattern matching: filter by source, detail-type, account, region, or any JSON field value
• Up to 5 targets per rule
• Targets include: Lambda, SQS, SNS, Kinesis Data Streams, Firehose, Step Functions, ECS tasks, API Gateway, CodePipeline, EC2 actions, EventBridge buses (cross-account), CloudWatch Logs, Batch, and more
• Schedule rules: run targets on a cron or rate schedule (replaces CloudWatch Events scheduled rules)

• Schema Registry: automatically discovers and stores event schemas from your buses; generate code bindings in Java, Python, TypeScript
• Event Replay: archive all events on a bus, replay historical events to a target for debugging or reprocessing
• Pipes: point-to-point integrations (source → optional filter → optional enrichment → target) with built-in batching and filtering
  - Sources: SQS, Kinesis, DynamoDB Streams, Kafka, MQ
  - Enrichment: Lambda, Step Functions, API Gateway, API Destination
  - Targets: same as rule targets
• API Destinations: send events to external SaaS APIs via HTTP with OAuth/API key auth
• Cross-account event routing: send events from one account's bus to another account's bus

• EventBridge: event-driven routing with content-based filtering; best for complex routing, SaaS integration, AWS service events
• SNS: pub/sub fan-out; simpler; push to multiple subscribers; best for notifications and fan-out to SQS/Lambda
• SQS: message queue with decoupling and buffering; consumers pull; best for load leveling and work distribution
• EventBridge is the ONLY service that integrates with SaaS partner event sources directly
• EventBridge supports more target types than SNS
• EventBridge has schema discovery; SNS does not

• Scenario: trigger Lambda when an EC2 instance changes state to 'stopped' → EventBridge rule on EC2 State Change event
• Scenario: react to Zendesk ticket creation in AWS → EventBridge Partner Event Bus (Zendesk)
• Scenario: schedule a Lambda to run every Monday at 8am UTC → EventBridge Schedule rule (cron expression)
• Scenario: route events to different targets based on event content → EventBridge content-based filtering rules
• Scenario: replay all events from last week to a new Lambda for debugging → EventBridge Event Replay
• Scenario: decouple microservices with content-based routing → EventBridge Custom Bus with rules

• Event size limit: 256 KB per event
• Max targets per rule: 5
• TRAP: EventBridge is NOT a message queue — it does not retain events for consumers to pull; use SQS if you need buffering
• TRAP: EventBridge default bus captures AWS service events but you CANNOT delete or modify it
• TRAP: EventBridge Scheduler is separate from EventBridge — it's for creating millions of scheduled tasks at scale
• TRAP: CloudWatch Events = EventBridge default bus — the same API, not a separate service
• Delivery guarantee: at-least-once (may rarely deliver duplicates)

amazon-simple-notification-service.text:
• Fully managed pub/sub (publish-subscribe) messaging and mobile notification service
• Publishers send messages to a Topic; all subscribers receive a copy of every message (fan-out)
• Push-based delivery — subscribers do not need to poll
• Highly durable: messages stored redundantly across multiple AZs before delivery
• Limits: 100,000 topics per account; 12.5 million subscriptions per topic

• HTTP/HTTPS: deliver to web endpoints
• Email / Email-JSON: send to email addresses (human notification)
• SMS: send text messages to phone numbers (mobile push)
• SQS: fan-out to one or more queues (most common architecture pattern)
• Lambda: trigger Lambda function directly
• Mobile Push: Apple (APNS), Google (GCM/FCM), Amazon (ADM), Baidu, Windows (WNS, MPNS)
• Kinesis Data Firehose: stream SNS messages into Firehose delivery streams

• Message Filtering: subscribers can attach a filter policy — only receive messages matching JSON attributes
  - Example: only receive messages where 'event_type' = 'order_placed'
• SNS + SQS Fan-Out Pattern: SNS topic → multiple SQS queues — each queue processes independently
  - Use case: same order event triggers inventory update, billing, and notification in parallel
  - Fully decoupled, durable (SQS provides buffering), and scalable
• Cross-region delivery: SNS can deliver to SQS queues in other regions

• Strict ordering of messages (like SQS FIFO)
• Deduplication: content-based or message deduplication ID
• Only SQS FIFO queues can subscribe to SNS FIFO topics
• Lower throughput than Standard topics: 300 msg/s (3,000 with batching)
• Use case: financial transactions, inventory management where order and deduplication matter

• SNS is NOT a queue — messages not delivered are NOT retained (no DLQ for HTTP subscribers by default)
• Dead Letter Queue (DLQ): attach an SQS DLQ to a subscription for failed deliveries — captures undeliverable messages
• Delivery retry policy: exponential backoff retry for HTTP/HTTPS endpoints (up to 23 retries over ~24 hours)
• Message size: up to 256 KB
• Raw message delivery: for SQS/Lambda/Kinesis — delivers raw message body without SNS JSON wrapper

• Scenario: send same order event to fulfillment, billing, and analytics simultaneously → SNS → 3 SQS queues (fan-out)
• Scenario: send app alerts via email + SMS + push notification at once → SNS topic with multiple protocol subscriptions
• Scenario: only send shipping notifications to subscribers interested in 'shipped' events → SNS message filtering
• Scenario: capture failed SNS deliveries to HTTP endpoint → DLQ (SQS) on the subscription
• Scenario: notify multiple teams when CloudWatch alarm triggers → CloudWatch Alarm → SNS topic → email subscribers

• Max message size: 256 KB
• Max subscriptions per topic: 12.5 million
• TRAP: SNS Standard does NOT guarantee ordering — use SNS FIFO + SQS FIFO if ordering is required
• TRAP: SNS is push-based — it does NOT store messages; if a subscriber is down, messages are lost (unless SQS is in between)
• TRAP: SNS cannot directly subscribe to SQS FIFO with a Standard topic — must use SNS FIFO topic
• TRAP: SNS is NOT for large payloads — for payloads >256KB, store in S3 and send the S3 URL via SNS
• TRAP: SNS + SQS fan-out requires SQS queue access policy to allow SNS to write to it

amazon-simple-queue-service.text:
• Fully managed message queuing service — oldest AWS service
• Decouples and scales microservices, distributed systems, and serverless applications
• Two queue types:
  1. Standard Queue: unlimited throughput, at-least-once delivery, best-effort ordering
  2. FIFO Queue: exactly-once processing, strict FIFO ordering, up to 300 msg/s (3,000 with batching)
• Producers send messages → SQS stores them → Consumers poll and process

• Message size: up to 256 KB per message
  - Extended Client Library: for larger payloads, stores body in S3 and sends S3 pointer in queue message
• Message retention: 1 minute to 14 days (default: 4 days)
• Visibility Timeout: after a consumer receives a message, it's hidden from other consumers for this duration
  - Default: 30 seconds; Max: 12 hours
  - If consumer doesn't delete message before timeout, it becomes visible again (retry)
  - ChangeMessageVisibility API: extend timeout while processing
• In-flight messages: Standard — up to 120,000; FIFO — up to 20,000

• Short Polling (default): returns immediately, even if no messages — many empty responses = higher cost
• Long Polling: waits up to 20 seconds for messages before returning — reduces empty responses and API call cost
  - Always prefer Long Polling in production
  - Set at queue level (ReceiveMessageWaitTimeSeconds) or per request (WaitTimeSeconds)
• Message batch: receive up to 10 messages per API call (ReceiveMessage)
• Delete batch: delete up to 10 messages per DeleteMessageBatch call

• Delay Queue: postpone delivery of new messages — 0 to 15 minutes delay (entire queue or per message)
  - Per-message delay (Message Timer): only on Standard queues, not FIFO
• Dead Letter Queue (DLQ): receives messages that fail processing after maxReceiveCount attempts
  - Isolates problematic messages for debugging
  - DLQ must be same type as source (Standard DLQ for Standard, FIFO DLQ for FIFO)
  - DLQ Redrive: send messages back to source queue after fixing the issue

• Exactly-once processing: deduplication using MessageDeduplicationId or content-based deduplication hash
• Deduplication interval: 5 minutes — duplicates within 5 minutes are discarded
• Message Group ID: messages in same group delivered in order; different groups can be processed in parallel
• Throughput: 300 msg/s without batching; 3,000 msg/s with batching (10 messages per batch)
• FIFO queue names must end with .fifo suffix

• Lambda event source mapping: Lambda polls SQS (up to 5 parallel pollers per event source mapping)
  - Standard: Lambda scales concurrency up to 1,000 Lambda functions
  - FIFO: Lambda scales per Message Group ID — preserves per-group ordering
  - Failed batch: Lambda returns failed items; SQS retries or moves to DLQ after maxReceiveCount
• EC2 Auto Scaling integration: scale EC2 ASG based on ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible CloudWatch metric

• Scenario: decouple web tier from backend processing — handle traffic spikes → SQS (buffer/leveling)
• Scenario: process financial transactions in strict order → SQS FIFO queue
• Scenario: dead messages are appearing in queue → SQS DLQ to capture and analyze failures
• Scenario: messages larger than 256 KB → SQS Extended Client Library (store in S3)
• Scenario: consumer takes longer than 30s to process → increase Visibility Timeout or call ChangeMessageVisibility
• Scenario: reduce cost of empty polling responses → enable Long Polling

• Max message size: 256 KB (use Extended Client + S3 for larger)
• Visibility Timeout: 0–43,200 seconds (12 hours)
• Retention: 60 seconds – 1,209,600 seconds (14 days)
• Delay Queue: 0–900 seconds (15 minutes)
• Long Polling wait: up to 20 seconds
• TRAP: SQS Standard does NOT guarantee ordering or exactly-once — use FIFO for those requirements
• TRAP: SQS is pull-based — consumers must poll; SNS is push-based
• TRAP: visibility timeout too short → same message processed by multiple consumers → duplicates
• TRAP: SQS DLQ maxReceiveCount: if set to 1, message goes to DLQ after first failure — may be too aggressive
• TRAP: FIFO throughput limit (300 msg/s) can be a bottleneck for high-volume workloads

aws-step-functions.text:
• Serverless visual workflow orchestration service using state machines
• Coordinate multiple AWS services into serverless workflows
• Written in Amazon States Language (ASL) — JSON-based DSL
• Two workflow types:
  1. Standard: exactly-once execution, up to 1 year duration, full execution history
  2. Express: at-least-once, up to 5 minutes, higher throughput (100,000 per second), lower cost

• Task: perform work using Lambda, ECS, DynamoDB, SNS, SQS, Glue, EMR, SageMaker, Bedrock, HTTP APIs, etc.
• Choice: conditional branching based on input
• Wait: pause for a fixed time or until a timestamp
• Parallel: execute multiple branches simultaneously
• Map: iterate over an array and process each element (inline or distributed)
• Pass: pass input to output with optional transformation (no work done)
• Succeed: stop execution successfully
• Fail: stop execution with error and cause

• Request-Response (default): call a service and immediately proceed — fire-and-forget
• Synchronous (.sync): wait for the called service to complete before moving to next state (e.g., ECS RunTask.sync)
• Wait for Callback (.waitForTaskToken): pause execution until an external system sends back a task token
  - Use case: human approval steps, third-party API callback, async workflows
  - Pass taskToken to SQS, SNS, Lambda, EventBridge, etc.; resume with SendTaskSuccess/Failure
• Activity Tasks: poll-based tasks for on-premises or legacy workers — worker polls for tasks using GetActivityTask

• Retry: automatic retries with configurable IntervalSeconds, MaxAttempts, BackoffRate per error type
• Catch: route to a fallback state on error — send to error handling branch
• Error types: States.TaskFailed, States.Timeout, States.HeartbeatTimeout, Lambda.ServiceException, custom errors
• Timeout: set per-state HeartbeatSeconds and TimeoutSeconds to prevent stuck executions

• Standard: exactly-once; max 1 year; full audit history in console; charged per state transition; best for long-running business processes, human approvals
• Express Synchronous: at-least-once; max 5 min; waits for completion; charged per execution duration; best for orchestrating microservices calls
• Express Asynchronous: at-least-once; max 5 min; fire-and-forget; logs to CloudWatch; best for high-volume event processing
• Standard cost: $0.025 per 1,000 state transitions
• Express cost: per duration (GB-second) — similar to Lambda pricing

• Scenario: orchestrate multi-step ML pipeline (data prep → train → evaluate → deploy) → Step Functions
• Scenario: order processing workflow that requires human approval before shipping → Step Functions + waitForTaskToken
• Scenario: retry failed Lambda invocations with exponential backoff → Step Functions Retry with BackoffRate
• Scenario: process 10,000 records in S3 in parallel → Step Functions Map state (Distributed Map)
• Scenario: long-running workflow that coordinates ECS tasks, Lambda, and DynamoDB over hours → Step Functions Standard
• Scenario: high-volume event processing (100k/s) with short steps → Express Workflows

• Standard max duration: 1 year
• Express max duration: 5 minutes
• Standard max state transitions: unlimited (charged per transition)
• TRAP: Express Workflows are at-least-once — NOT suitable for exactly-once financial transactions; use Standard
• TRAP: Step Functions does NOT retry by default — you must explicitly configure Retry in the state definition
• TRAP: Step Functions is NOT a scheduler — use EventBridge Scheduler or Step Functions with EventBridge for scheduling
• TRAP: connecting Lambda directly to Lambda (chaining) instead of Step Functions leads to poor error handling and no visibility — always use Step Functions for multi-step orchestration
• Max input/output size per state: 256 KB (use S3 for larger data, pass S3 reference)

aws-budgets.text:
• Set custom cost and usage budgets with proactive alerts before or when thresholds are breached
• Four budget types: Cost, Usage, Savings Plans utilization/coverage, Reservation utilization/coverage
• Alerts via: SNS topic or email
• Up to 5 SNS notifications and 10 email addresses per budget
• Free tier: first 2 budgets free; $0.02/day per active budget after that

• Alert types: Actual (already spent) or Forecasted (projected to exceed)
• Alert thresholds: absolute dollar amounts or percentage of budget
• Budget Actions: automatically apply controls when a threshold is hit
  - Apply IAM policy to a user/group/role (restrict permissions to prevent further spend)
  - Apply Service Control Policy (SCP) to an OU or account
  - Target EC2 or RDS instances: stop instances to stop incurring charges
• Budget Actions require IAM permissions for Budgets to take actions on your behalf
• Action states: PENDING, STANDBY, EXECUTION_IN_PROGRESS, EXECUTION_SUCCESS, EXECUTION_FAILURE

• Filters: filter budgets by service, linked account (Organizations), tag, AZ, purchase type, instance type, region, API operation
• Budget Reports: send daily, weekly, or monthly email reports with CSV attachment — up to 50 budgets per report
• AWS Organizations: create budgets for individual member accounts or the entire organization from management account
• Cost allocation tags: ensure resources are tagged for accurate budget tracking per team/project

• Scenario: automatically stop dev EC2 instances when monthly bill exceeds $500 → Budget with Budget Action targeting EC2
• Scenario: alert finance team when spend is projected to exceed budget → Forecasted alert via SNS → email
• Scenario: prevent IAM users from launching new resources when budget exceeded → Budget Action applying a restrictive IAM policy
• Scenario: give each team a spending limit for their AWS account → AWS Organizations + per-account Budgets
• Scenario: track Savings Plans coverage and alert when below 80% → Savings Plans coverage budget

• TRAP: Budgets ≠ Cost Explorer — Budgets = proactive alerts + automated actions; Cost Explorer = historical analysis + recommendations
• TRAP: Budgets do NOT prevent spending by default — they only alert; use Budget Actions to actually restrict
• TRAP: Forecasted alerts use ML-based projections — they may not be 100% accurate
• TRAP: Budget Actions are NOT instant — there is a short delay between threshold breach and action execution
• TRAP: Reserved Instance/Savings Plans budgets track utilization or coverage, NOT raw spend

aws-cost-and-usage-report.text:
• Most granular and comprehensive AWS billing report available
• Delivers detailed line-item data for every AWS service, resource, tag, and pricing dimension
• Delivered to an S3 bucket — updated multiple times per day (configurable: hourly or daily granularity)
• Available formats: CSV (gzip/zip compressed) or Parquet (columnar — recommended for Athena)
• Free to generate — only pay for S3 storage

• Line items include: service name, usage type, resource ID, usage quantity, blended/unblended cost, pricing details, Savings Plan/RI discounts applied
• Time granularity: hourly, daily, or monthly
• Versioning: CUR can overwrite or create new files for each billing period update
• Resource-level data: see cost per individual EC2 instance, S3 bucket, RDS instance, etc. (enable 'Include Resource IDs')
• Split cost allocation: attribute shared costs (e.g., support, shared S3) across linked accounts
• Cost allocation tags appear as columns in the report

• Amazon Athena: query CUR stored in S3 as Parquet using SQL — most common analysis method
  - AWS provides a CloudFormation template to set up Athena + Glue Crawler for CUR automatically
• Amazon Redshift: COPY CUR CSV data into Redshift for complex warehouse-level analysis
• Amazon QuickSight: visualize Athena or Redshift CUR data in dashboards
• AWS Cost Intelligence Dashboard (CID): pre-built QuickSight dashboard on top of CUR
• Third-party tools: Apptio, CloudHealth, Cloudability integrate directly with CUR S3 bucket

• Scenario: identify exact cost per EC2 instance per hour → CUR with hourly granularity + resource IDs
• Scenario: analyze billing data across 50 accounts for chargeback to business units → CUR in management account with Organizations enabled
• Scenario: build custom cost dashboards → CUR → Athena → QuickSight
• Scenario: audit how Savings Plans discounts were applied → CUR shows SP discount per line item
• Scenario: most detailed billing data for finance team → CUR (not Cost Explorer, which is summarized)

• TRAP: CUR is NOT real-time — it's updated multiple times per day; not for live cost monitoring
• TRAP: CUR requires S3 bucket in same region as your billing home region (us-east-1 for most accounts)
• TRAP: CUR ≠ Cost Explorer — CUR is raw data for custom analysis; Cost Explorer is a managed console/API for exploration
• TRAP: resource-level data must be explicitly enabled — not on by default; enabling increases report size significantly
• TRAP: CUR in Parquet format is required for Athena integration — CSV is harder to query efficiently

aws-cost-explorer.text:
• Visualize, understand, and manage AWS costs and usage over time
• Historical data: up to 12 months of past usage
• Forecasting: up to 12 months into the future (ML-based projection)
• Filterable by: service, linked account, region, AZ, instance type, tag, purchase option, etc.
• Free access to the console; API access costs $0.01 per request

• Pre-built reports: monthly costs by service, daily costs, RI utilization, Savings Plans utilization, EC2 usage
• Custom date ranges and filter combinations
• Granularity: monthly (free), daily (free), hourly ($0.01/request via API, visible in console for recent data)
• Group by: service, linked account, tag, AZ, instance type, purchase type, region, API operation
• Save custom reports for recurring analysis

• Rightsizing Recommendations: identify underutilized or idle EC2 instances and recommend smaller instance types
  - Looks at CloudWatch CPU/memory metrics over 14 days
  - Shows estimated monthly savings
• Reserved Instance Recommendations: recommend RI purchases based on your On-Demand usage patterns; shows payback period and savings
• Savings Plans Recommendations: Compute Savings Plans, EC2 Instance Savings Plans, SageMaker Savings Plans recommendations
• RI Purchase Recommendations: 1-year or 3-year, all/partial/no upfront options

• ML-powered service that continuously monitors your spend and detects unusual cost spikes
• Monitor types: individual AWS service, linked account, cost category, cost allocation tag
• Alerting: send anomaly alerts via SNS → email or Slack/PagerDuty integration
• Shows root cause: which service/region/account drove the anomaly
• Free to use (anomaly detection itself is free)

• Scenario: visualize which services are driving cost increase this month → Cost Explorer monthly view grouped by service
• Scenario: identify idle EC2 instances to terminate or downsize → Cost Explorer Rightsizing Recommendations
• Scenario: determine optimal RI purchase commitment → Cost Explorer RI Recommendations
• Scenario: get alerted when cost spikes unexpectedly → Cost Anomaly Detection
• Scenario: forecast next month's AWS bill → Cost Explorer forecast view
• Scenario: build custom cost reports for finance → Cost Explorer custom report (or CUR for more detail)

• TRAP: Cost Explorer ≠ CUR — Explorer is for visualization/exploration; CUR is for raw programmatic analysis
• TRAP: Cost Explorer ≠ Budgets — Explorer shows what happened; Budgets alert and act proactively
• TRAP: Rightsizing recommendations from Cost Explorer are based on CloudWatch metrics — ensure detailed monitoring is enabled for accuracy
• TRAP: Cost Explorer API charges $0.01 per request — can add up in automated systems; cache results
• TRAP: Hourly granularity in Cost Explorer is only available for the most recent 14 days in the console; full hourly history requires CUR

amazon-ec2.text:
• Elastic Compute Cloud — virtual servers (instances) in the cloud (IaaS)
• Configuration choices: OS (Linux, Windows, macOS), CPU, RAM, storage, network card, security groups
• EC2 User Data: bootstrap script executed once at first launch, runs as root user
• Security Groups: stateful virtual firewall at instance level — allow rules only, no deny rules
• Key Pairs: used for SSH (Linux port 22) or RDP password decryption (Windows port 3389)

• Naming convention: [family][generation].[size] — e.g. m5.2xlarge
• General Purpose (t, m): balanced CPU/memory/network — web servers, code repos, small DBs
• Compute Optimized (c): high-performance processors — batch jobs, HPC, media transcoding, gaming servers
• Memory Optimized (r, x, z): large RAM — in-memory DBs (Redis), real-time big data, SAP HANA
• Storage Optimized (i, d, h): high sequential read/write, low latency IOPS — distributed file systems, data warehousing, OLTP
• Accelerated Computing (p, g, trn, inf): GPU/custom chips — ML training, inference, graphics rendering
• T-series Burstable: accumulate CPU credits when idle, spend during bursts — T2 unlimited: no credits limit but extra charges

• On-Demand: pay by second (Linux/Windows) or hour; highest cost, most flexible; no commitment
• Reserved Instances (RI): 1 or 3 year commitment; up to 72% savings vs On-Demand
  - Standard RI: specific instance type/region — cannot change; highest discount
  - Convertible RI: can change instance type, OS, tenancy — lower discount (~54%)
  - Scheduled RI: deprecated — use On-Demand Capacity Reservations + Savings Plans instead
• Savings Plans: commitment to $/hour spend for 1 or 3 years; more flexible than RIs
  - Compute Savings Plans: any EC2 instance family, region, OS, tenancy + Fargate + Lambda
  - EC2 Instance Savings Plans: specific family + region; highest discount
• Spot Instances: up to 90% discount; AWS can terminate with 2-minute warning when capacity needed
  - Spot Block: reserve for 1–6 hours without interruption (deprecated in new usage)
• Dedicated Host: physical server dedicated to you; needed for BYOL (Bring Your Own License) software
• Dedicated Instance: instance runs on hardware dedicated to you; may share host with other same-account instances
• Capacity Reservations: reserve On-Demand capacity in a specific AZ; no billing discount; always billed

• EBS (Elastic Block Store): persistent network-attached block storage; survives stop/start; AZ-specific
• Instance Store: physically attached storage; ephemeral — data LOST on stop/terminate; highest IOPS
• EFS (Elastic File System): shared NFS storage; multi-instance, multi-AZ; Linux only
• S3: object storage via SDK; not block storage; not mounted like a filesystem
• Root volume: default EBS; can configure DeleteOnTermination (on by default for root, off for additional volumes)

• ENI (Elastic Network Interface): virtual NIC; can have multiple ENIs per instance; movable between instances in same AZ
  - Primary ENI: eth0 — primary private IP + optional secondary IPs + Elastic IP
  - Use case: dual-homed instances, separate management/data traffic, low-budget HA
• Elastic IP: static public IPv4 address; attached to instance or ENI; costs money when NOT attached to running instance
• Placement Groups:
  - Cluster: same rack, same AZ — ultra-low latency, high throughput (HPC, HFT) — risk: single rack failure
  - Spread: different racks, different AZs — max 7 instances per AZ per group — fault isolation
  - Partition: different partitions (racks) within AZ; up to 7 partitions per AZ; for Hadoop, Kafka, Cassandra

• Hibernate: saves RAM state to encrypted EBS root volume; instance resumes fast (RAM not lost)
  - Supported: C, M, R, T families; EBS root volume must be encrypted; max 60 days hibernation
  - Use case: long-running processes, save RAM state between sessions
• AMI (Amazon Machine Image): customized EC2 image; region-specific; can copy cross-region
  - Backed by EBS snapshots or instance store
  - Process: launch → customize → create AMI (creates EBS snapshots)
• EC2 Image Builder: automate building, testing, and distributing AMIs on schedule

• Scenario: run a steady-state workload 24/7 for 3 years at lowest cost → 3-year Standard Reserved Instance (all upfront)
• Scenario: unpredictable workload that can tolerate interruptions → Spot Instances
• Scenario: compliance requires physical server isolation + run BYOL Windows SQL Server → Dedicated Host
• Scenario: HPC job requiring low-latency network between instances → Placement Group Cluster
• Scenario: instance needs to remain running if one rack fails, up to 7 instances → Placement Group Spread
• Scenario: migrate on-premises license-bound software to AWS → Dedicated Host
• Scenario: fastest local storage for a database with data replicated elsewhere → Instance Store

• Max EBS volume size: 64 TiB (io2 Block Express)
• TRAP: Security Groups are STATEFUL — return traffic automatically allowed; NACLs are STATELESS
• TRAP: Dedicated Instance ≠ Dedicated Host — Dedicated Host gives you visibility/control of physical server; Dedicated Instance just ensures hardware isolation
• TRAP: Elastic IP attached to a stopped instance = still costs money
• TRAP: On-Demand Capacity Reservation does NOT give a discount — it reserves capacity only
• TRAP: Convertible RIs cannot be sold on the Reserved Instance Marketplace
• TRAP: Spot Instances are NOT suitable for databases or stateful workloads that cannot handle interruption

aws-app-runner.text:
• Fully managed service to deploy containerized web apps and APIs from source code or container image
• No infrastructure management — no EC2, ECS, or load balancer configuration needed
• Automatic builds (from GitHub or ECR), deployments, scaling, and TLS certificates
• Auto-scales from zero to handle traffic spikes; scales back to zero when idle (pay only for usage)
• Built-in load balancing and HTTPS endpoints

• Source types: Container image (ECR) or Source code (GitHub — App Runner builds automatically)
• Supported runtimes for source code: Python, Node.js, Java, .NET, Go, PHP, Ruby
• VPC Connector: optionally connect App Runner service to your VPC to access private resources (RDS, ElastiCache)
• Auto Scaling configuration: set min/max concurrency and min/max instances
• Health checks: HTTP health check endpoint; unhealthy instances replaced automatically

• Scenario: deploy a REST API quickly without managing servers or containers → App Runner
• Scenario: startup wants CI/CD from GitHub to production with auto-scaling → App Runner (source code deployment)
• Scenario: containerized app needs access to private RDS → App Runner + VPC Connector
• Exam distinction: App Runner vs Elastic Beanstalk → App Runner is simpler and container-native; Beanstalk is for more traditional app deployment with more config options

• TRAP: App Runner does NOT give you control over the underlying EC2 instances — fully abstracted
• TRAP: App Runner is NOT free tier eligible for the service itself — compute costs apply
• TRAP: App Runner does NOT support stateful workloads or long-running background processes — use ECS/EKS for those
• TRAP: Without VPC Connector, App Runner cannot reach private resources in your VPC

aws-batch.text:
• Fully managed batch computing service at any scale
• Dynamically provisions the optimal compute resources (EC2, Spot, Fargate) based on volume and requirements
• No need to install or manage batch computing software or clusters
• Jobs are defined as Docker container images — any language, any runtime
• Charged only for underlying EC2/Fargate resources used

• Job: unit of work submitted to Batch; defined by a Job Definition
• Job Definition: blueprint specifying Docker image, vCPU, memory, IAM role, env variables, volumes, retry strategy
• Job Queue: jobs are submitted to a queue; queues have priority and are associated with Compute Environments
• Compute Environment:
  - Managed: AWS provisions and scales EC2/Spot/Fargate instances automatically
  - Unmanaged: you provision and manage EC2 instances; more control
• Job Dependencies: define DAG of job dependencies (Job A must complete before Job B starts)

• EC2 Managed: choose instance types, On-Demand or Spot; Batch selects optimal instance
• Spot Instances: cost-optimized batch jobs — Batch handles Spot interruptions and re-queues jobs
• Fargate: serverless compute for batch — no instance management; best for smaller batch jobs (<16 vCPU, <120 GiB RAM)
• Multi-node Parallel Jobs: tightly coupled HPC jobs across multiple EC2 instances using MPI
• Array Jobs: submit one job definition, run N copies in parallel (e.g., process 1000 files simultaneously)

• Scenario: process 100,000 genomics samples nightly at lowest cost → Batch with Spot Instances + Managed Compute Environment
• Scenario: run distributed HPC simulation requiring MPI across many nodes → Batch Multi-node Parallel Job
• Scenario: batch job needs to run containerized Python script with no server management → Batch with Fargate
• Scenario: ETL job processes files as they arrive in S3 → S3 Event → Lambda → submit Batch job
• Scenario: job B should only run after jobs A completes → Batch Job Dependencies

• TRAP: Batch ≠ Lambda — Lambda max 15 min; Batch = unlimited runtime; Batch = Docker; Lambda = managed runtime
• TRAP: Batch ≠ Glue — Batch is general-purpose compute; Glue is for ETL/data catalog specifically
• TRAP: Batch is NOT serverless by default — it provisions EC2 instances unless you choose Fargate
• TRAP: Batch does NOT schedule jobs on a time basis — use EventBridge Scheduler or CloudWatch Events to trigger Batch jobs on a schedule
• TRAP: Spot interruption in Batch — managed environments automatically re-queue jobs on different instances

aws-elastic-beanstalk.text:
• Platform as a Service (PaaS) — deploy and manage web applications without managing infrastructure
• Developer uploads code; Beanstalk handles: load balancing, auto scaling, health monitoring, deployments, patching
• Supported platforms: Java, .NET, PHP, Node.js, Python, Ruby, Go, Docker (single/multi-container)
• You retain FULL control over the underlying AWS resources (EC2, ELB, ASG, RDS)
• Free to use — pay only for provisioned resources

• Application: logical collection of Beanstalk components (environments, versions, configurations)
• Application Version: specific labeled iteration of deployable code (stored in S3)
• Environment: running version of an application — runs on EC2 instances + supporting infrastructure
• Environment Tiers:
  - Web Server tier: handles HTTP requests via ELB + EC2 Auto Scaling Group
  - Worker tier: processes background tasks from SQS queue; auto-scales based on queue depth
• Environment URL: Beanstalk provides a CNAME (e.g. myapp.us-east-1.elasticbeanstalk.com)
• .ebextensions: YAML/JSON config files in .ebextensions/ directory of source bundle — customize environment (install packages, set env vars, configure nginx)

• All at Once: deploy to all instances simultaneously — fastest; downtime during deployment; no additional cost
• Rolling: deploy to a batch at a time — some capacity reduction; no additional cost; slow rollback
• Rolling with Additional Batch: launch extra instances before deploying — full capacity maintained; slight cost increase
• Immutable: launch completely new instances → deploy → swap → terminate old — safest; doubles capacity temporarily; easy rollback
• Traffic Splitting (Canary): deploy to new instances and split traffic percentage — A/B testing
• Blue/Green: deploy to separate environment → swap CNAMEs → zero downtime; rollback by swapping back
  - NOT a built-in Beanstalk deployment type — done manually by cloning environment + Route 53/CNAME swap

• Inside Beanstalk environment: RDS created within Beanstalk — tied to environment lifecycle; deleted if environment deleted (DEV ONLY)
• Outside Beanstalk environment (PRODUCTION): create RDS separately; pass connection string as environment variable
  - Benefits: RDS survives environment deletion; can connect multiple environments to same DB
• Beanstalk decoupling steps: create snapshot of internal RDS → create new standalone RDS → update CNAME → delete internal RDS

• Scenario: deploy a web app without managing load balancers, ASGs, or EC2 config → Elastic Beanstalk
• Scenario: zero-downtime deployment with easy rollback → Immutable deployment
• Scenario: background image processing jobs from S3 uploads → Beanstalk Worker Tier + SQS
• Scenario: test new version of app before full rollout → Blue/Green deployment (clone environment)
• Scenario: RDS database should survive environment deletion → create RDS outside Beanstalk

• TRAP: Beanstalk is NOT for complex microservices or fine-grained infrastructure control — use ECS/EKS for that
• TRAP: RDS inside Beanstalk environment is deleted when environment is deleted — never use for production data
• TRAP: .ebextensions files must be in the root of the source bundle in a directory called .ebextensions
• TRAP: All at Once deployment causes downtime — not suitable for production without maintenance window
• TRAP: Rolling deployment reduces capacity temporarily — use Rolling with Additional Batch to maintain full capacity
• TRAP: Beanstalk does NOT support Windows Server Docker containers natively on all platforms

aws-lambda.text:
• Serverless Functions as a Service (FaaS) — run code without provisioning or managing servers
• Supported runtimes: Node.js, Python, Java, C# (.NET), Go, Ruby, custom runtime (via Lambda Layers using Runtime API)
• Max execution timeout: 900 seconds (15 minutes)
• Memory: 128 MB to 10,240 MB (1 vCPU per 1,769 MB of memory allocated)
• Ephemeral storage (/tmp): 512 MB to 10,240 MB
• Stateless: no persistent state between invocations (use DynamoDB/ElastiCache for state)

• Free tier: 1 million requests/month + 400,000 GB-seconds compute/month
• Charges: per invocation + per GB-second of duration
• Concurrency: number of simultaneous Lambda executions
  - Account default limit: 1,000 concurrent executions per region (soft limit, can increase)
  - Reserved Concurrency: guarantees max concurrency for a function AND throttles function at that limit
  - Provisioned Concurrency: pre-warms execution environments to eliminate cold starts (costs extra)
• Scaling: Lambda scales automatically; 1,000 concurrent burst limit initially, then +500/minute

• Synchronous: caller waits for response; errors returned to caller — API Gateway, SDK, ALB, Lambda URL
• Asynchronous: Lambda queues event internally; caller doesn't wait; retries 2x on failure → DLQ (SQS/SNS) or EventBridge on failure
  - Sources: S3, SNS, EventBridge, SES, CloudFormation, Config, CodeCommit, CodePipeline
• Polling (Event Source Mapping): Lambda polls the source and invokes synchronously
  - Sources: SQS, Kinesis Data Streams, DynamoDB Streams, Kafka (MSK/Self-managed)
  - SQS: if Lambda fails to process, message returns to queue → eventually goes to DLQ
  - Kinesis/DynamoDB Streams: Lambda retries the entire batch until success or expiry → Bisect on Error, DLQ, Destination for failures

• By default: Lambda runs in AWS-managed VPC — can access internet but NOT private VPC resources
• Lambda in VPC: attach Lambda to VPC subnets + security groups → can access RDS, ElastiCache, etc. in VPC
  - Lambda uses ENIs (Elastic Network Interfaces) — provision in private subnets
  - For internet access from VPC Lambda → must use NAT Gateway (Lambda in private subnet → NAT GW → Internet GW)
• Lambda URL: built-in HTTPS endpoint for invoking Lambda without API Gateway
• VPC-attached Lambda cold starts: historically slower due to ENI creation — now pre-warmed (Hyperplane ENIs)

• Environment variables: store config; use KMS SecureString for sensitive values
• Layers: share common code/libraries across functions — up to 5 layers per function; max 250 MB unzipped total
• Aliases: named pointers to specific versions; used for traffic shifting (blue/green, canary)
• Versions: immutable snapshots of function code + config; $LATEST is mutable
• Lambda SnapStart (Java): snapshot and restore pre-initialized execution environment — reduces cold start by up to 90%
• Lambda@Edge: run Lambda at CloudFront edge locations for Viewer/Origin Request/Response events
• CloudFront Functions: lighter JS functions at edge — request/response header manipulation only; <1ms
• Container images: package Lambda as Docker image up to 10 GB (instead of zip deployment)

• Scenario: process S3 file uploads automatically → S3 Event Notification → Lambda (async)
• Scenario: REST API backend without servers → API Gateway → Lambda
• Scenario: eliminate cold starts for latency-sensitive function → Provisioned Concurrency
• Scenario: Lambda needs to access RDS in private subnet → Lambda in VPC, private subnet, security group allows RDS access
• Scenario: Lambda in VPC can't reach internet → add NAT Gateway in public subnet; Lambda in private subnet routes via NAT
• Scenario: share pandas/numpy library across 5 Lambda functions → Lambda Layer
• Scenario: route 10% of traffic to new Lambda version → Alias with weighted routing (canary)

• Max timeout: 900 seconds (15 min)
• Max memory: 10,240 MB
• Max deployment package: 50 MB (zipped), 250 MB (unzipped), 10 GB (container image)
• /tmp storage: 512 MB – 10,240 MB (configurable)
• Max concurrent executions (default): 1,000 per region
• TRAP: Lambda timeout default is 3 seconds — increase for long-running tasks
• TRAP: Lambda in VPC needs NAT Gateway for internet access — NOT just an internet gateway
• TRAP: Reserved Concurrency = 0 → Lambda is throttled completely (effectively disabled)
• TRAP: Lambda does NOT run continuously — it's invoked on-demand; use ECS/Fargate for long-running containers
• TRAP: Async invocation failure → Lambda retries 2 more times (3 total); must configure DLQ or Destinations for failed events

amazon-elastic-container-registry.text:
• Fully managed Docker-compatible container image registry
• Store, manage, share, and deploy container images and artifacts
• Private registry: fully isolated per AWS account and region
• Public registry: Amazon ECR Public (gallery.ecr.aws) — share images publicly
• Integrates natively with ECS, EKS, Fargate, Lambda, CodeBuild, CodePipeline

• IAM-based access control: resource-based policies on repositories
• Cross-account access: grant specific accounts pull/push permissions via repository policy
• Private endpoint: ECR VPC Endpoint (Interface) — pull images from within VPC without internet
• Encryption at rest: AES-256 (default) or KMS customer managed key
• Encryption in transit: HTTPS (TLS) always
• Image scanning:
  - Basic scanning: uses Clair open-source scanner; on-push or manual
  - Enhanced scanning: powered by Amazon Inspector; continuous scanning; supports OS + language (npm, pip) vulnerabilities

• Lifecycle Policies: automate cleanup of old or unused images to reduce storage costs
  - Rules: expire images by count (keep last N), by age (older than N days), by tag prefix
  - Example: keep last 3 tagged images per prefix, delete untagged images older than 7 days
• Immutable image tags: once pushed with a tag, that tag cannot be overwritten — ensures deployment consistency
• Cross-region replication: replicate images to other regions automatically for DR and multi-region deployments
• Cross-account replication: replicate to other AWS accounts
• Replication is configured at the registry level (not per repository)

• Scenario: store container images privately and deploy to ECS → ECR private repository → ECS Task Definition references ECR image URI
• Scenario: EC2/ECS cannot pull images from ECR inside a VPC without internet → ECR VPC Endpoint (Interface)
• Scenario: ensure that deployed image tags are never overwritten → enable immutable tags
• Scenario: reduce ECR storage costs by cleaning old images → lifecycle policy (expire untagged after 7 days)
• Scenario: share a base image across multiple AWS accounts → cross-account repository policy
• Scenario: identify container vulnerabilities in CI/CD pipeline → ECR Enhanced Scanning + Inspector

• TRAP: ECR is per-region — images must be replicated if needed in another region
• TRAP: ECS task role (not instance profile) must have ecr:GetAuthorizationToken + ecr:BatchGetImage permissions to pull images
• TRAP: ECR VPC Endpoint is required to pull images from private subnets with no internet — easy to miss in architecture questions
• TRAP: Lifecycle policies are evaluated daily — not real-time cleanup
• TRAP: Basic vs Enhanced scanning — Enhanced (Inspector) is continuous and covers language-level packages; Basic is push-only

amazon-elastic-container-service.text:
• Fully managed container orchestration service — run Docker containers at scale
• Two launch types:
  1. EC2 Launch Type: containers run on EC2 instances YOU provision and manage; must install ECS Agent; full control over instance types
  2. Fargate Launch Type: serverless — AWS manages infrastructure; define CPU/memory per task; no EC2 to manage
• Hybrid: run some services on EC2, others on Fargate within the same ECS cluster

• ECS Cluster: logical grouping of EC2 instances or Fargate capacity
• Task Definition: JSON blueprint defining one or more containers — image, CPU, memory, ports, volumes, environment variables, IAM role, logging
• Task: a running instance of a Task Definition (one-off or part of a Service)
• Service: maintains a desired number of simultaneously running Tasks; integrates with load balancers for HA
• Container Agent (EC2 type): daemon running on each EC2 instance to communicate with ECS control plane
• Service Discovery: use AWS Cloud Map or Route 53 for internal service-to-service discovery

• EC2 Instance Profile Role (EC2 launch type only): permissions for the ECS Agent running on the host — pull images from ECR, write logs to CloudWatch, register/deregister instances
• Task Role: permissions for the APPLICATION running inside the container — access DynamoDB, S3, SQS, etc.
  - Each Task Definition can have a different Task Role — fine-grained per-service permissions
• Fargate launch type: no instance profile — only Task Role
• CRITICAL exam concept: always assign Task Role for per-task AWS API access, NOT instance profile

• ALB (Application Load Balancer): supports dynamic port mapping — multiple tasks on same EC2 instance with different host ports; content-based routing
• NLB (Network Load Balancer): high throughput, ultra-low latency; static IPs; use for gaming, financial trading
• Network Modes (EC2 type): bridge (default, NAT), host (container uses host port directly), awsvpc (each task gets its own ENI and private IP — same as Fargate)
• awsvpc mode: required for Fargate; gives each task its own security group and ENI; best network isolation
• ECS Service Connect: built-in service-to-service discovery and communication using AWS Cloud Map

• ECS Service Auto Scaling: scales the number of Tasks (not EC2 instances)
  - Target Tracking: scale based on CPU utilization, memory utilization, ALB request count per target
  - Step Scaling: scale based on CloudWatch Alarm thresholds
  - Scheduled Scaling: scale at specific times
• EC2 Cluster Auto Scaling (EC2 type): use ECS Cluster Capacity Providers to auto-scale underlying EC2 instances based on task demand
  - MANAGED scaling: ECS drives the EC2 ASG scaling automatically
  - Fargate auto-scales compute automatically — no cluster scaling needed

• EC2 type storage: bind mount (share between containers in same task), EFS volume mount (persistent shared storage across tasks/AZs)
• Fargate storage: ephemeral storage 20–200 GB; EFS mount for persistence
• EFS + ECS: mount EFS volume in task definition — multiple tasks across multiple AZs share the same filesystem
• Logging: use awslogs driver → send container stdout/stderr to CloudWatch Logs
  - Log group, region, and stream prefix configured in Task Definition

• Scenario: run containers without managing servers → ECS + Fargate
• Scenario: containers need access to S3 and DynamoDB → add Task Role with S3/DynamoDB permissions
• Scenario: multiple containers in same task need shared filesystem → EFS volume mount
• Scenario: scale containers in and out based on SQS queue depth → ECS Service Auto Scaling with Step Scaling on SQS metric
• Scenario: run containers on GPU instances for ML inference → ECS EC2 launch type with GPU instance types
• Scenario: ensure container logs are available if container crashes → awslogs driver → CloudWatch Logs

• Max containers per task definition: 10 (soft limit)
• TRAP: Task Role and Instance Profile are different — always use Task Role for app-level AWS access
• TRAP: ECS Service Auto Scaling scales TASKS, not EC2 instances — you need Cluster Capacity Provider to also scale EC2 instances
• TRAP: ECS on EC2 requires ECS Agent installed — Amazon ECS-optimized AMIs have it pre-installed
• TRAP: bridge network mode = cannot use same host port for multiple tasks on same instance; use awsvpc or dynamic port mapping with ALB
• TRAP: EFS is the only way to share persistent data between ECS tasks across AZs

amazon-elastic-kubernetes-service.text:
• Fully managed Kubernetes control plane — AWS manages master nodes, etcd, API server
• You manage worker nodes (or use Fargate for serverless pods)
• Supports any Kubernetes-compatible workload with full API compatibility
• Node types:
  1. Managed Node Groups: AWS provisions and manages EC2 nodes in an ASG; supports node group updates with rolling replacements
  2. Self-Managed Nodes: you provision EC2 instances and register them; full control; more operational overhead
  3. Fargate: serverless pods — no nodes to manage; AWS runs each pod in isolated microVM

• VPC CNI (Container Network Interface): each pod gets a primary IP from the VPC subnet — pods are first-class VPC citizens
• Kubernetes Service types: ClusterIP (internal), NodePort, LoadBalancer (NLB/ALB), ExternalName
• AWS Load Balancer Controller: deploy ALB for Kubernetes Ingress; deploy NLB for LoadBalancer Services
• EBS CSI Driver: dynamically provision EBS volumes as Kubernetes PersistentVolumes (per-pod, not shared)
• EFS CSI Driver: mount EFS as PersistentVolume — shared across pods and nodes; ReadWriteMany access mode
• FSx for Lustre CSI: high-performance shared storage for HPC workloads on EKS

• IRSA (IAM Roles for Service Accounts): associate IAM roles with Kubernetes Service Accounts
  - Pods using that Service Account get fine-grained AWS permissions via projected service account token
  - More secure than assigning IAM role to the entire node
• EKS Pod Identity (newer): simpler alternative to IRSA — assign IAM role directly to pod identity associations
• RBAC: Kubernetes native role-based access control for pod/namespace/resource access
• EKS access modes: aws-auth ConfigMap (legacy) or Access Entries API (newer, recommended)
• Secrets: use AWS Secrets Manager or SSM Parameter Store with Secrets Store CSI Driver — mount secrets as volumes

• EKS Anywhere: deploy and operate Kubernetes clusters on your own on-premises infrastructure (VMware, bare metal)
• EKS Distro: the Kubernetes distribution used by EKS — open source; deploy on your own infrastructure
• EKS Connector: register any Kubernetes cluster (on-prem, other clouds) to EKS console for visibility
• EKS Add-ons: managed lifecycle for critical cluster components: CoreDNS, kube-proxy, VPC CNI, EBS CSI Driver, Pod Identity Agent

• Scenario: migrate existing on-premises Kubernetes workloads to AWS → EKS
• Scenario: pods need to access DynamoDB with least privilege → IRSA (IAM Role for Service Account)
• Scenario: deploy Kubernetes without managing nodes → EKS + Fargate
• Scenario: pods need shared persistent storage → EFS CSI Driver (ReadWriteMany)
• Scenario: expose a Kubernetes service with a load balancer → AWS Load Balancer Controller (ALB Ingress)
• Scenario: run Kubernetes on-premises with same EKS tooling → EKS Anywhere

• TRAP: ECS vs EKS — ECS is AWS-native orchestration; EKS is Kubernetes-compatible; choose EKS when Kubernetes expertise or portability is needed
• TRAP: EKS does NOT auto-scale nodes by default — you need Cluster Autoscaler or Karpenter for automatic node scaling
• TRAP: Node IAM role is for the kubelet to call AWS APIs (describe instances, pull ECR); pod permissions use IRSA — don't confuse them
