• TRAP: EKS Fargate does NOT support DaemonSets or privileged containers
• TRAP: EKS Fargate pods can only use EFS for persistent storage — EBS is NOT supported on Fargate
• TRAP: Managed Node Groups do NOT support Windows containers — use Self-Managed Nodes for Windows

aws-fargate.text:
• Serverless compute engine for containers — works with both ECS and EKS
• No EC2 instances to provision, patch, manage, or scale
• Define required CPU and memory per task/pod; AWS handles the rest
• Each task/pod runs in its own isolated kernel and compute environment (microVM)
• Pay per vCPU and memory per second of use

• ECS Fargate CPU options: 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 8, 16 vCPU
• ECS Fargate memory: must be compatible with CPU choice
  - 0.25 vCPU: 0.5–2 GB; 0.5 vCPU: 1–4 GB; 1 vCPU: 2–8 GB; 2 vCPU: 4–16 GB; 4 vCPU: 8–30 GB
• EKS Fargate: same flexible resource requests mapped to isolation boundary
• Ephemeral storage: 20 GB (included) up to 200 GB (ECS); shared between all containers in a task
• Persistent storage: mount EFS volumes for persistent, shared data

• Network mode: awsvpc only — each task gets its own ENI, private IP, and security group
• Public IP assignment: optional; if in public subnet, can assign public IP for internet access
• Private subnet + NAT Gateway: for internet access without public IP
• IAM Task Role: assign per-task IAM role for AWS API access
• Secrets: reference Secrets Manager or SSM Parameter Store values in task definition (injected as env vars)
• Fargate Platform Versions: track Fargate runtime updates; pin to specific version or use LATEST

• Run Fargate tasks at up to 70% discount vs standard Fargate pricing
• AWS can interrupt Fargate Spot tasks with 2-minute warning (similar to EC2 Spot)
• Suitable for: fault-tolerant, stateless, batch workloads
• ECS Capacity Provider Strategy: mix standard Fargate + Fargate Spot with weights and base values
• Not suitable for: databases, stateful workloads, user-facing production services that cannot tolerate interruption

• Scenario: run containers without managing any servers → Fargate (ECS or EKS)
• Scenario: batch job that processes nightly without running 24/7 → ECS + Fargate (scale to 0 when done)
• Scenario: run stateless API containers at lowest cost → ECS Fargate + Fargate Spot
• Scenario: containers need shared filesystem between tasks → EFS mount with Fargate
• Scenario: existing K8s workload, want serverless nodes → EKS + Fargate profile
• Scenario: GPU workloads → NOT Fargate (no GPU support); use ECS/EKS with EC2 GPU instances

• TRAP: Fargate does NOT support GPU — use EC2 launch type for GPU workloads
• TRAP: Fargate does NOT support Windows containers on EKS Fargate — Windows requires EC2 nodes
• TRAP: Fargate ephemeral storage is NOT shared across tasks — each task has its own isolated /tmp
• TRAP: ECS Fargate only supports awsvpc network mode — bridge and host are EC2-only
• TRAP: Fargate Spot = cheaper but interruptible — never use for production stateful services
• TRAP: EKS Fargate does not support DaemonSets (e.g., log collectors, monitoring agents) — use a sidecar container instead

amazon-aurora.text:
• AWS-built relational database — fully compatible with MySQL 5.7/8.0 and PostgreSQL 12/13/14/15
• 5x faster than standard MySQL, 3x faster than standard PostgreSQL
• Part of RDS family but with a fundamentally different distributed storage architecture
• Storage: auto-scales from 10 GB to 128 TB in 10 GB increments — no manual storage provisioning
• 6 copies of data across 3 AZs (2 copies per AZ) — self-healing with peer-to-peer replication

• Aurora always maintains 6 copies of data across 3 AZs — survives loss of up to 2 copies for writes, 3 for reads
• Automatic failover: promotes a Read Replica to primary in < 30 seconds
• Up to 15 Read Replicas per cluster (vs 5 for standard RDS)
• Read Replicas: can be in same region or cross-region; replicate from shared storage layer (no binlog replication lag)
• Cluster endpoint: always points to the primary writer — auto-updates on failover
• Reader endpoint: load-balances across all Read Replicas automatically
• Custom endpoints: subset of replicas for specific workloads (e.g., analytics queries only)

• Auto-scales capacity in fine-grained increments (0.5 ACU steps) based on demand — no pre-provisioning
• ACU (Aurora Capacity Unit): unit of compute (approx 2 GB RAM + corresponding CPU + networking)
• Scales from minimum ACUs to maximum ACUs within seconds
• Pay per ACU-second — no charge when database is paused (v2 does NOT pause/resume like v1)
• Use case: variable workloads, dev/test, new apps with unpredictable traffic
• v1 vs v2: v2 scales much faster and supports more Aurora features (Multi-AZ, Global Database, etc.)

• 1 Primary Region (read/write) + up to 5 Secondary Regions (read-only)
• Replication lag: typically < 1 second using dedicated storage-based replication (not log replication)
• RPO (Recovery Point Objective): < 1 second
• RTO (Recovery Time Objective): < 1 minute for managed failover
• Use case: globally distributed apps, disaster recovery across regions
• Cross-region read replicas: can be promoted to standalone in < 1 minute during region failure
• Differs from standard RDS cross-region Read Replicas: Global Database replicates at STORAGE level — much faster

• Backtrack: rewind the DB cluster to any point in time WITHOUT restoring a snapshot; in-place rollback in minutes
  - Aurora MySQL only; max backtrack window: 72 hours
• Aurora Multi-Master: all nodes are read/write — continuous write availability; no single write point
  - MySQL-compatible only; use case: zero-downtime writes during failover
• Aurora Machine Learning: run ML inference via SQL — integrates with SageMaker and Comprehend
• Aurora Parallel Query: pushes query processing to the storage layer — runs analytical queries faster without impacting write performance
• Aurora Export to S3: export snapshot data to S3 in Parquet format for analysis with Athena or Redshift Spectrum

• Scenario: MySQL app needs < 30 second failover and up to 15 read replicas → Aurora MySQL
• Scenario: global app needs < 1 second replication lag across regions → Aurora Global Database
• Scenario: accidentally deleted records, need to rollback quickly → Aurora Backtrack (MySQL)
• Scenario: new SaaS app with unknown traffic pattern → Aurora Serverless v2
• Scenario: need continuous write availability even during DB failover → Aurora Multi-Master
• Scenario: run analytical queries on operational DB without performance impact → Aurora Parallel Query

• Storage: auto-scales to 128 TB; 6 copies across 3 AZs
• Read Replicas: up to 15 (vs 5 for standard RDS)
• Failover time: < 30 seconds
• Global Database RPO: < 1s; RTO: < 1 min
• TRAP: Aurora Serverless v2 does NOT pause/resume like v1 — minimum ACU still runs
• TRAP: Aurora Read Replicas are not the same as RDS Read Replicas — Aurora uses shared distributed storage, so replication is near-instantaneous
• TRAP: Backtrack ≠ Point-in-Time Recovery — Backtrack is in-place rewind; PITR creates a new cluster
• TRAP: Aurora Multi-Master is MySQL only — not available for PostgreSQL-compatible Aurora
• TRAP: Aurora cross-region Read Replicas vs Global Database — Global Database is faster (storage-level) and designed for DR; cross-region replicas are slower (binlog)

amazon-documentdb.text:
• Fully managed document database with MongoDB API compatibility
• AWS implementation built on Aurora storage technology — NOT running actual MongoDB
• Supports MongoDB 3.6, 4.0, and 5.0 API (partial compatibility — not 100% feature-parity)
• Storage: automatically grows in 10 GB increments up to 64 TB
• 6 copies of data across 3 AZs — continuous backup to S3

• Cluster: 1 primary instance (read/write) + up to 15 read replicas
• Failover: promotes a read replica to primary automatically in < 30 seconds
• Storage: shared distributed storage cluster — same Aurora storage architecture
• Instance types: choose from memory-optimized, general purpose families
• Global Clusters: replicate to up to 5 secondary regions (< 1 second replication)
• Change Streams: real-time ordered sequence of document change events — stream to Lambda, Kinesis, etc.

• Scenario: migrate existing MongoDB application to AWS with minimal code changes → DocumentDB
• Scenario: need a managed, scalable document database with JSON data model → DocumentDB
• Scenario: app needs to react to document changes in real time → DocumentDB Change Streams → Lambda
• Scenario: MongoDB Atlas vs DocumentDB → if staying within AWS ecosystem, DocumentDB; if multi-cloud, MongoDB Atlas

• TRAP: DocumentDB is NOT actual MongoDB — it implements MongoDB API but with differences; some MongoDB features are not supported
• TRAP: DocumentDB is NOT serverless — you provision instances (no serverless option as of SAA-C03 exam scope)
• TRAP: DocumentDB vs DynamoDB — DocumentDB is MongoDB-compatible document DB; DynamoDB is AWS-native key-value + document (JSON) NoSQL; both are NoSQL but different APIs and use cases
• TRAP: DocumentDB ≠ DynamoDB for key-value access patterns — DynamoDB is simpler/faster for key-value; DocumentDB is for complex document queries

amazon-dynamodb.text:
• Fully managed, serverless, multi-region, multi-active, key-value and document NoSQL database
• Single-digit millisecond read/write performance at any scale
• No schema required — flexible attributes per item
• Tables → Items (rows) → Attributes (columns, flexible)
• Primary Key options:
  - Partition Key only (Hash Key): must be unique across all items
  - Partition Key + Sort Key (Composite Key): combination must be unique; items with same partition key stored together, sorted by sort key
• Secondary Indexes:
  - LSI (Local Secondary Index): same partition key, different sort key; created at table creation; strongly or eventually consistent
  - GSI (Global Secondary Index): different partition key and sort key; created anytime; eventually consistent only; has its own RCU/WCU

• Provisioned Capacity Mode:
  - Specify RCU (Read Capacity Units) and WCU (Write Capacity Units) in advance
  - 1 WCU = 1 KB write per second; 1 RCU = 4 KB strongly consistent read or 8 KB eventually consistent read
  - Auto Scaling: set min/max RCU/WCU and target utilization; DynamoDB adjusts capacity
  - Reserved Capacity: 1 or 3 year commitment for significant discount
• On-Demand Capacity Mode:
  - Pay per request; auto-scales instantly for any throughput
  - No capacity planning; higher per-request cost vs provisioned
  - Best for: unpredictable workloads, new apps, very low traffic tables
• ProvisionedThroughputExceededException: request throttled — use exponential backoff

• Read Consistency:
  - Eventually Consistent (default): may get slightly stale data; 0.5 RCU per 4 KB read
  - Strongly Consistent: always returns most recent data; costs 1 RCU per 4 KB read; not available for GSI reads
• DAX (DynamoDB Accelerator): in-memory write-through cache for DynamoDB
  - Microsecond read latency (vs millisecond); managed service
  - API-compatible with DynamoDB — minimal code change
  - Cache hit: returns from DAX; cache miss: reads from DynamoDB, caches result
  - Use case: read-heavy workloads, latency-sensitive apps, gaming leaderboards
  - NOT suitable for strongly consistent reads (DAX returns cached data)
• TTL (Time to Live): auto-delete items after expiry timestamp attribute
  - No additional cost; deleted within 48 hours of expiry; useful for session data, logs, temporary data

• DynamoDB Streams: ordered record of item changes (INSERT, MODIFY, REMOVE)
  - Retention: 24 hours
  - Integration: Lambda event source mapping; Kinesis Data Streams (longer retention)
  - Stream view types: KEYS_ONLY, NEW_IMAGE, OLD_IMAGE, NEW_AND_OLD_IMAGES
  - Use case: trigger Lambda on data changes, replicate to another table, feed search index
• Global Tables: multi-region, multi-active replication using DynamoDB Streams
  - All replica tables are writable — active-active across regions
  - Last-writer-wins conflict resolution
  - Same table name across regions; data propagated in < 1 second
  - Use case: globally distributed apps, disaster recovery, low-latency for global users

• Transactions: ACID transactions across multiple items and tables in same region
  - TransactWriteItems: up to 25 Put/Update/Delete/ConditionCheck in one atomic operation
  - TransactGetItems: up to 25 Get operations atomically
  - Costs 2x RCU/WCU (read phase + prepare phase)
• Point-in-Time Recovery (PITR): restore table to any second in past 35 days
  - No performance impact; continuous backups; restores to new table
• On-Demand Backup: create full table backup at any time; retained indefinitely until deleted; no performance impact
• Export to S3: export table data to S3 in JSON or ION format without consuming RCU; uses PITR
  - Use with Athena for analytics on DynamoDB data

• Scenario: serverless app needs a NoSQL DB with millisecond latency at any scale → DynamoDB
• Scenario: DynamoDB reads are slow and expensive → add DAX for caching
• Scenario: trigger Lambda when a DynamoDB item changes → DynamoDB Streams + Lambda
• Scenario: store user session data that expires after 30 minutes → DynamoDB + TTL
• Scenario: globally distributed gaming app needs low-latency writes from any region → DynamoDB Global Tables
• Scenario: analytics on DynamoDB data without affecting production → Export to S3 → Athena
• Scenario: need to undo accidental data deletion from last night → PITR (Point-in-Time Recovery)

• Max item size: 400 KB
• Max GSIs per table: 20; max LSIs per table: 5 (must be created at table creation time)
• DynamoDB Streams retention: 24 hours
• PITR retention: 35 days
• TRAP: DAX is NOT suitable for strongly consistent reads — returns cached (potentially stale) data
• TRAP: LSI must be created when the table is created — cannot add LSI to existing table
• TRAP: GSI uses eventually consistent reads only — cannot do strongly consistent reads on GSI
• TRAP: Transactions cost 2x normal RCU/WCU — account for this in capacity planning
• TRAP: Global Tables require DynamoDB Streams to be enabled AND On-Demand or Provisioned with Auto Scaling
• TRAP: TTL deletion is NOT instant — items may appear in queries for up to 48 hours after expiry

amazon-elasticache.text:
• Fully managed in-memory caching service — reduce database load and accelerate app performance
• Two engines:
  1. Redis: rich data structures, persistence, replication, pub/sub, Lua scripting, geospatial — single-threaded
  2. Memcached: pure caching, multi-threaded, horizontal scaling, no persistence — simpler
• Sub-millisecond read/write latency
• Use cases: database query caching, session storage, leaderboards, real-time analytics, pub/sub messaging

• Data structures: strings, hashes, lists, sets, sorted sets, bitmaps, HyperLogLog, streams, geospatial
• Persistence: RDB (point-in-time snapshots) and/or AOF (append-only file, log every write)
• Replication: primary-replica (read replicas); replicas can serve reads
• Multi-AZ with Auto-Failover: automatic failover to replica if primary fails (recommended for HA)
• Cluster Mode:
  - Disabled: single primary + up to 5 replicas; max 6.4 TB per node; single shard
  - Enabled: data partitioned across multiple shards (up to 500 shards); each shard has replicas; scales horizontally
• Encryption: at rest (KMS) and in transit (TLS); AUTH token for password protection; Redis RBAC (ACL)
• Sorted Sets: ideal for real-time leaderboards with ZADD/ZRANGEBYSCORE
• Global Datastore: replicate Redis cluster across regions for DR and global low-latency reads

• Multi-threaded: uses multiple CPU cores — higher throughput per node vs Redis
• Auto Discovery: clients automatically discover all nodes in the cluster
• No persistence: data lost on node failure or restart — pure cache only
• No replication: no Multi-AZ; no failover; simpler but less HA
• Horizontal scaling: add/remove nodes easily; data distributed across nodes
• Use case: simple large-scale caching where persistence/HA not required; multi-threaded performance matters

• Lazy Loading (Cache-Aside):
  - Read: check cache → if miss, read from DB → write to cache → return data
  - Pros: only caches requested data; resilient to cache failure
  - Cons: cache miss penalty (3 trips); stale data possible
• Write-Through:
  - Write: write to DB → also write to cache
  - Pros: cache always up to date; no stale data
  - Cons: write penalty; cache polluted with infrequently read data
• TTL (Time to Live): set expiry on cached items to avoid stale data buildup
• Session Store: store user sessions in ElastiCache (Redis) — stateless EC2 instances read session from cache

• Scenario: reduce RDS load from read-heavy app → ElastiCache (Redis) with Lazy Loading
• Scenario: real-time gaming leaderboard → Redis Sorted Sets
• Scenario: stateless EC2 app needs session management → Redis as session store
• Scenario: cache needs to survive node failure → Redis with Multi-AZ + persistence
• Scenario: simple caching, maximize throughput per node → Memcached (multi-threaded)
• Scenario: real-time pub/sub messaging → Redis Pub/Sub
• Scenario: Redis cluster needs to scale beyond single node → Redis Cluster Mode Enabled (sharding)

• Redis max node size (r6g.16xlarge): ~400 GB memory
• Redis Cluster mode: up to 500 shards
• TRAP: Memcached has NO persistence, NO replication, NO Multi-AZ — if node fails, all cached data is lost
• TRAP: Redis Multi-AZ ≠ Redis Cluster Mode — Multi-AZ = HA failover; Cluster Mode = horizontal sharding
• TRAP: ElastiCache cannot be accessed from on-premises directly — must use VPN or Direct Connect + VPC peering if needed
• TRAP: ElastiCache is NOT a database — it's a cache layer; source data must remain in RDS/DynamoDB
• TRAP: Redis AUTH (password) is different from Redis RBAC (ACL) — RBAC provides per-command/per-key permissions

amazon-rds.text:
• Fully managed relational database service — OLTP workloads
• Supported engines: MySQL, PostgreSQL, MariaDB, Oracle, Microsoft SQL Server, Amazon Aurora (separate service)
• AWS manages: hardware provisioning, OS patching, DB installation, automated backups, monitoring, Multi-AZ failover
• You manage: schema, SQL queries, data, application optimization
• NOT suitable for: BigData/OLAP workloads (use Redshift), NoSQL (use DynamoDB), in-memory cache (use ElastiCache)

• Storage backed by EBS: gp2, gp3, or io1
• gp2: burst to 3,000 IOPS; 3 IOPS/GB (minimum 100 IOPS)
• gp3: baseline 3,000 IOPS + 125 MB/s throughput; can provision up to 16,000 IOPS independently of storage
• io1/io2: high-performance; up to 256,000 IOPS; use for I/O-intensive databases
• Storage Auto Scaling: automatically increases storage when free space drops below 10%; set maximum storage threshold
  - Supports all RDS engines; enabled per instance; avoids manual intervention for growing databases

• Automated Backups:
  - Daily full backup during maintenance window + transaction logs every 5 minutes
  - Point-in-Time Recovery (PITR): restore to any second within retention period
  - Retention: 1–35 days (must be > 0 to enable PITR); 0 disables automated backups
  - Stored in S3; free storage equal to DB size
  - Deleted when instance is deleted (unless you create a final snapshot)
• Manual Snapshots:
  - Triggered by user; retained INDEFINITELY until manually deleted
  - Can copy cross-region for DR; can share with other accounts
  - Useful for long-term retention beyond 35 days
• Restore: always creates a NEW RDS instance (cannot restore in-place)

• Synchronous replication to a standby instance in a different AZ
• Standby is NOT accessible for reads — it's purely for failover (HA only, NOT read scaling)
• Automatic failover in 1–2 minutes: DNS endpoint updated; application reconnects
• Failover triggers: primary failure, AZ failure, DB instance class change, OS patching
• Multi-AZ for SQL Server: uses SQL Server Mirroring/Always On
• Multi-AZ DB Cluster (newer): 1 writer + 2 readable standbys across 3 AZs — both HA and read scaling
• TRAP: Multi-AZ standby cannot serve read traffic in standard Multi-AZ (only in Multi-AZ Cluster)

• Asynchronous replication — replica may lag behind primary
• Up to 5 Read Replicas per instance (up to 15 for Aurora)
• Can be in same AZ, different AZ, or different region (Cross-Region Read Replica)
• Each replica has its own DNS endpoint — application must direct reads to replica endpoint
• Can be promoted to standalone DB instance (breaks replication)
• Network cost for replication: FREE within same region; costs apply cross-region
• Cross-Region Read Replica: DR capability + reduce read latency for global users

• Fully managed database proxy that sits between app and RDS/Aurora
• Connection Pooling: maintains a pool of established connections — reduces connection overhead
• Why use it:
  - Lambda: Lambda can create many concurrent connections; Proxy pools them → prevents DB connection exhaustion
  - Microservices: many services connecting to same DB → Proxy reduces connection count
  - Failover: RDS Proxy reduces failover time by 66% (maintains existing connections during switchover)
• IAM Authentication: enforce IAM DB auth via Proxy (Secrets Manager integration)
• Never publicly accessible — must be accessed from within the VPC
• Supports: MySQL, PostgreSQL, MariaDB, SQL Server, Aurora

• Scenario: relational DB that needs HA with automatic failover → RDS Multi-AZ
• Scenario: read-heavy app needs to offload reads → RDS Read Replicas
• Scenario: Lambda functions exhausting DB connections → RDS Proxy
• Scenario: need to restore DB to exact state at 3:47am yesterday → PITR (automated backups)
• Scenario: copy DB to another region for DR → Cross-Region Read Replica or cross-region snapshot copy
• Scenario: DB storage growing rapidly → Storage Auto Scaling
• Scenario: DB needs > 35 days of backup retention → Manual Snapshots (retained indefinitely)

• Max Read Replicas: 5 (standard RDS); 15 (Aurora)
• PITR retention: 1–35 days
• Multi-AZ failover time: ~1–2 minutes
• TRAP: Read Replicas use ASYNCHRONOUS replication — data may be slightly stale; Multi-AZ uses SYNCHRONOUS
• TRAP: Multi-AZ standby CANNOT serve reads (standard Multi-AZ) — it only activates during failover
• TRAP: Restoring from backup always creates a NEW instance — you cannot restore over an existing instance
• TRAP: Deleting an RDS instance deletes automated backups — manual snapshots survive deletion
• TRAP: RDS Proxy is VPC-only — cannot be publicly accessed from the internet

amazon-redshift.text:
• Fully managed petabyte-scale data warehouse — OLAP (Online Analytical Processing)
• Columnar storage + MPP (Massively Parallel Processing) — optimized for analytical queries and aggregations
• Based on PostgreSQL 8.0.2 — SQL-compatible but NOT for OLTP
• Architecture: 1 Leader node (query parsing, planning, aggregating) + 1–128 Compute nodes (data storage + query execution)
• Node families: dc2 (SSD-backed, compute-dense); ra3 (managed storage backed by S3 — decouple compute from storage)

• Distribution Style (controls how rows are spread across compute nodes):
  - EVEN: round-robin — default for no obvious join/group key; minimizes skew
  - KEY: rows with same distribution key on same node — reduces data movement for joins
  - ALL: entire table replicated to all nodes — for small dimension tables
  - AUTO: Redshift chooses best style based on table size
• Sort Key: sorts data on disk; improves range queries and join performance
  - Compound: primary for range queries (date range, etc.)
  - Interleaved: equal weight to all sort key columns — better for multi-column filters

• Elastic Resize: add/remove nodes quickly; cluster briefly pauses (minutes)
• Classic Resize: change node type; cluster read-only for hours/days; old cluster stays until complete
• Concurrency Scaling: auto-add read clusters during burst; first 1 hour/day of concurrency scaling is free
• Multi-AZ: ra3 clusters support Multi-AZ deployment — replica cluster in another AZ; automatic failover
• Automated Snapshots: every 8 hours OR 5 GB of data changed; retained 1 day (configurable 1–35 days)
• Manual Snapshots: indefinite retention; cross-region copy for DR
• Cross-Region Snapshot Copy: replicate snapshots to another region automatically

• COPY command: bulk load from S3, EMR, DynamoDB, SSH — most efficient load method
  - Parallel loading: Redshift reads multiple S3 files in parallel (split files into slices matching compute node count)
  - Requires IAM role attached to Redshift cluster with S3 read access
• UNLOAD command: export query results to S3 as CSV or Parquet
• Enhanced VPC Routing: forces COPY/UNLOAD traffic through VPC (required if S3 bucket is VPC-restricted)
• Redshift Spectrum: query data directly in S3 without loading — uses Glue Data Catalog; billed per TB scanned
• Federated Query: live query from RDS PostgreSQL/Aurora PostgreSQL/MySQL without ETL
• Redshift ML: write CREATE MODEL in SQL → SageMaker trains → query with ML_PREDICT()

• Scenario: analytical queries on petabytes of data → Redshift
• Scenario: query S3 data lake from Redshift without loading → Redshift Spectrum
• Scenario: Redshift cluster slow during business hours → Concurrency Scaling
• Scenario: replicate Redshift data to another region for DR → cross-region snapshot copy
• Scenario: ETL pipeline loads into S3 then needs in Redshift → Glue ETL → S3 → COPY into Redshift
• Scenario: run ML model predictions in SQL queries → Redshift ML

• Max nodes: 128 compute nodes
• TRAP: Redshift is single-AZ by default (Multi-AZ only available for ra3 — newer feature)
• TRAP: COPY is far faster than INSERT for bulk loads — never bulk-insert row by row
• TRAP: Redshift Spectrum requires a Glue Data Catalog or Redshift external schema — not automatic
• TRAP: Redshift is NOT suitable for OLTP — high concurrency row-level transactions → use RDS/Aurora
• TRAP: Enhanced VPC Routing must be enabled if your S3 bucket access must stay within VPC
• TRAP: Redshift does not support full-text search — use OpenSearch for search

aws-database-migration-service.text:
• Managed service to migrate databases to AWS quickly with minimal downtime
• Source database remains fully operational during migration
• Supports homogeneous migrations (MySQL → MySQL) and heterogeneous migrations (Oracle → Aurora PostgreSQL)
• For heterogeneous migrations: use AWS Schema Conversion Tool (SCT) FIRST to convert schema + stored procedures
• DMS runs on a Replication Instance (EC2) in your VPC

• Full Load: migrate all existing data; source can remain live
• Full Load + CDC (Change Data Capture): migrate existing data AND continuously replicate changes — keeps target in sync
• CDC Only: only replicate ongoing changes (use when target is already pre-populated)
• Continuous Replication: ongoing sync after full load — used during cutover planning

• Sources: Oracle, SQL Server, MySQL, MariaDB, PostgreSQL, MongoDB, SAP, IBM DB2, S3, DocumentDB
• Targets: RDS, Aurora, Redshift, DynamoDB, S3, OpenSearch, Kinesis Data Streams, MSK, DocumentDB, Neptune, Redis
• Schema Conversion Tool (SCT): converts schema (DDL) + application code — not needed for same-engine migrations
• DMS does NOT convert schema — use SCT for that; DMS only moves data

• Multi-AZ Replication Instance: standby instance in different AZ; automatic failover if primary replication instance fails
• CloudWatch Metrics: monitor replication latency, read/write throughput, host CPU/memory
• AWS DMS Fleet Advisor: automatically inventory on-premises database servers and recommend migration targets
• AWS Migration Hub: track migration progress across DMS and other migration tools in one dashboard

• Scenario: migrate on-prem Oracle to AWS Aurora PostgreSQL with minimal downtime → SCT (convert schema) + DMS Full Load + CDC
• Scenario: replicate production MySQL to S3 for analytics → DMS CDC → S3
• Scenario: continuous replication from on-prem SQL Server to Redshift for reporting → DMS + SCT
• Scenario: database migration needs HA during the migration itself → Multi-AZ Replication Instance
• Scenario: large-scale migration, need to assess which databases to migrate first → DMS Fleet Advisor

• TRAP: DMS does NOT convert schema — SCT converts schema; DMS moves data
• TRAP: SCT is NOT needed for same-engine migrations (MySQL → MySQL); only for heterogeneous (Oracle → Aurora)
• TRAP: DMS Replication Instance must be in same VPC or connected network as source and target
• TRAP: Full Load without CDC means changes made during migration may be lost at cutover
• TRAP: DMS is NOT suitable for migrating non-database data (files, S3 objects) — use DataSync for that

aws-command-line-interface.text:
• Command-line tool for interacting with AWS services programmatically via terminal
• Version 2 (v2) is current — install separately from v1; supports SSO login natively
• Authentication methods:
  - Access Keys (Access Key ID + Secret Access Key) — for IAM users
  - IAM Role via STS (assumed role) — recommended for scripts and automation
  - EC2 Instance Profile / ECS Task Role — recommended for code running on AWS compute
  - AWS SSO / IAM Identity Center — for human users in organizations
• Configuration: aws configure → stores credentials in ~/.aws/credentials and config in ~/.aws/config
• Never store Access Keys on EC2 instances — use Instance Profiles instead

• --dry-run flag: test permissions for EC2 actions without actually executing — checks if you'd succeed
• --query flag: JMESPath expressions to filter JSON output in the CLI
• --output flag: json (default), text, table, yaml
• --profile: select named profile (multiple accounts/roles configured)
• STS decode-authorization-message: decode encoded error messages from unauthorized API calls
• aws sts get-caller-identity: verify which IAM identity the CLI is using
• aws sts get-session-token --serial-number [MFA ARN] --token-code [code]: get temp credentials for MFA-protected API calls
• Pagination: some commands paginate results — use --max-items, --page-size, --starting-token; or --no-paginate for all results

• Instance Metadata Service (IMDS): provides info about the running EC2 instance from within the instance
  - URL: http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/
  - No IAM authentication required to access (available to any process on the instance)
• IMDSv1: simple GET requests — no token required (legacy, less secure)
• IMDSv2 (recommended): token-based, two-step process — must first get session token, then use it
  - Step 1: PUT request to get token (TTL 1–21600 seconds)
  - Step 2: GET request with token header
  - Block IMDSv1: set HttpTokens=required to enforce IMDSv2 on instance
• Common metadata paths: /public-ipv4, /local-ipv4, /iam/security-credentials/[role-name], /user-data, /hostname

• Scenario: EC2 application needs to call AWS APIs → attach IAM Instance Profile Role (NOT access keys on instance)
• Scenario: CLI command returns 'UnauthorizedOperation' error → use sts decode-authorization-message
• Scenario: script runs on EC2 and needs current instance's private IP → query IMDS /local-ipv4
• Scenario: enforce MFA for CLI API calls → use aws sts get-session-token with MFA; use temp credentials
• Scenario: org has multiple AWS accounts, switch between them → named profiles in ~/.aws/config

• TRAP: never hardcode access keys in code or store on EC2 → always use IAM roles
• TRAP: IMDSv1 is a security risk — any process on the instance can access credentials; enforce IMDSv2
• TRAP: --dry-run only works for EC2 API calls — not all AWS services support it
• TRAP: CLI access keys have same permissions as the IAM user — if user has admin access, CLI does too
• TRAP: rotating access keys requires updating them everywhere they're used — use Secrets Manager for managed rotation

aws-cloud-development-kit.text:
• Define cloud infrastructure using familiar programming languages: TypeScript (most common), Python, Java, C# (.NET), Go
• Compile (synthesize) code into CloudFormation templates automatically
• Higher-level abstractions over raw CloudFormation — less boilerplate, more reuse
• CDK App → Stacks → Constructs → CloudFormation Resources
• Open source; maintained by AWS; large community Construct Library

• L1 Constructs (Cfn* prefix): direct 1:1 mapping to CloudFormation resources — e.g., CfnBucket; no defaults; full control
• L2 Constructs: higher-level abstractions with sensible defaults and helper methods — e.g., s3.Bucket; most commonly used
• L3 Constructs (Patterns): opinionated multi-service patterns — e.g., ecs_patterns.ApplicationLoadBalancedFargateService creates ECS + ALB + Route 53 together
• Custom Constructs: build and share reusable Constructs across teams or as npm/PyPI packages

• cdk init: scaffold new CDK app in chosen language
• cdk synth: synthesize CDK app into CloudFormation template (written to cdk.out/)
• cdk diff: compare deployed stack vs local CDK code — shows what will change
• cdk deploy: deploy stack (runs synth then CloudFormation deploy)
• cdk destroy: delete deployed stack
• CDK Bootstrap (cdk bootstrap): one-time setup per account/region — creates CDKToolkit stack (S3 bucket for assets + IAM roles)
• cdk doctor: diagnose CDK environment issues

• Scenario: team wants to define infrastructure using Python instead of YAML → AWS CDK
• Scenario: need to deploy same infrastructure pattern across 20 AWS accounts → CDK App with multiple stacks + CDK Pipelines
• Scenario: developers want reusable infrastructure components with type safety → CDK L2/L3 Constructs
• Scenario: generate CloudFormation template from CDK code without deploying → cdk synth
• Exam distinction: CDK vs CloudFormation → CDK generates CloudFormation; CDK = infrastructure as real code; CloudFormation = infrastructure as YAML/JSON

• TRAP: CDK synthesizes to CloudFormation — it's NOT a separate deployment engine; CloudFormation does the actual provisioning
• TRAP: CDK Bootstrap must be run in each account/region before first deploy — otherwise deploy fails
• TRAP: CDK does NOT eliminate CloudFormation — it generates and submits CloudFormation templates
• TRAP: Different CDK languages are NOT interoperable — a Python CDK app cannot use a TypeScript construct directly without JSII

aws-cloudshell.text:
• Browser-based shell with pre-authenticated AWS CLI access in the AWS Console
• No access key configuration needed — uses your current console session credentials
• Pre-installed tools: AWS CLI v2, Python 3, Node.js, git, bash, zsh, vim, jq, and more
• 1 GB of persistent storage per region (stored in home directory ~/) — survives shell restarts
• Available in most (not all) AWS regions
• Free to use — no additional charges

• File uploads/downloads: upload files from local machine to CloudShell home directory; download files back
• Multiple tabs: open multiple CloudShell sessions in same browser
• Environment customization: install additional packages (pip, npm, yum, etc.) — persists to next session
• Permissions: CloudShell inherits the permissions of the IAM identity used to log into the console
• Network: CloudShell can access public AWS endpoints; access to private VPC resources requires VPC-connected CloudShell (newer feature)

• Scenario: admin needs to run CLI commands without setting up local CLI/credentials → CloudShell
• Scenario: quickly test IAM policy permissions from the console → CloudShell with aws sts get-caller-identity + test commands
• Scenario: script needs to run in a terminal with AWS access without an EC2 instance → CloudShell

• TRAP: CloudShell storage is per-region (1 GB) — files in us-east-1 CloudShell are NOT available in eu-west-1
• TRAP: CloudShell is NOT available in all regions — check before assuming it's available
• TRAP: CloudShell sessions time out after 20 minutes of inactivity — not suitable for long-running processes
• TRAP: CloudShell uses your console IAM permissions — if you have limited permissions, CloudShell does too

aws-codeartifact.text:
• Fully managed artifact and package management repository service
• Store, publish, and share software packages securely within your organization
• Supports package formats: npm, PyPI, Maven, Gradle, NuGet, Swift, generic packages
• Integrates with CodeBuild, CodePipeline, IDEs (IntelliJ, VS Code, Eclipse)
• Acts as a proxy and cache for public repositories (npmjs.com, PyPI, Maven Central, NuGet Gallery)

• Domains: top-level grouping of repositories (one per organization; share upstream connections)
• Repositories: within a domain; store packages; can have upstream repos
• Upstream Repositories: chain repos — requests for missing packages fetch from upstream (public or another CodeArtifact repo)
• External Connection: connect a CodeArtifact repo to a public external repository; packages cached after first pull
• Cross-account sharing: share repositories across AWS accounts using resource-based policies
• Retention: packages stored indefinitely until explicitly deleted; no TTL by default

• Scenario: CodeBuild builds should not fetch packages from the internet every time → CodeArtifact with external connection caches packages
• Scenario: enforce that all npm packages come from approved sources → CodeArtifact as internal npm registry; block direct internet access
• Scenario: share private packages across multiple teams in different AWS accounts → CodeArtifact cross-account domain sharing
• Scenario: audit which packages are being used in builds → CodeArtifact package usage tracking

• TRAP: CodeArtifact ≠ ECR — CodeArtifact is for software packages (npm, pip, Maven); ECR is for Docker container images
• TRAP: CodeArtifact requires configuration in build tools (npm, pip) to point to CodeArtifact endpoint instead of public registry
• TRAP: auth tokens for CodeArtifact expire after 12 hours — automation must refresh tokens (aws codeartifact get-authorization-token)

aws-codebuild.text:
• Fully managed CI build service — compiles source code, runs unit tests, produces artifacts ready for deployment
• No servers to provision or manage — scales automatically to handle multiple concurrent builds
• Charges per build minute (based on compute type used)
• Uses buildspec.yml file in source root to define build commands
• Build output artifacts stored in S3; logs in CloudWatch Logs and/or S3

• Must be named buildspec.yml and placed in root of source code (or specify custom path)
• Phases:
  - install: install dependencies, runtime version
  - pre_build: commands before build (e.g., login to ECR: $(aws ecr get-login-password...))
  - build: main build commands (e.g., mvn package, docker build, pytest)
  - post_build: commands after build (e.g., push Docker image to ECR, send notification)
• Environment variables: in buildspec or CodeBuild console; use SSM Parameter Store or Secrets Manager for sensitive values
• Artifacts: define files to output to S3
• Cache: cache dependencies (node_modules, .m2) in S3 or local — speeds up subsequent builds

• Build environments: prebuilt Docker images (Amazon Linux 2, Ubuntu) for common languages; or custom Docker image from ECR/DockerHub
• Compute types: general1.small (3 GB RAM), general1.medium (7 GB), general1.large (15 GB), general1.2xlarge (145 GB)
• VPC support: run builds inside your VPC to access private resources (RDS, ElastiCache, internal services)
  - Requires specifying VPC, subnets, security groups in CodeBuild project
  - CodeBuild in VPC can also access internet via NAT Gateway
• Environment variables: plaintext, SSM Parameter Store, Secrets Manager (auto-decrypted)

• Scenario: build and test a Node.js app on every GitHub commit → CodePipeline source (GitHub) → CodeBuild
• Scenario: build Docker image and push to ECR in CI pipeline → CodeBuild with Docker support + ECR login in pre_build
• Scenario: CodeBuild needs to run integration tests against private RDS → CodeBuild with VPC configuration
• Scenario: store API keys used in build securely → CodeBuild environment variables from Secrets Manager
• Scenario: speed up builds by caching node_modules → CodeBuild caching with S3 bucket

• TRAP: buildspec.yml must be in the ROOT of the source code directory (or specify alternate path)
• TRAP: CodeBuild does NOT deploy — it only builds and tests; use CodeDeploy or CloudFormation for deployment
• TRAP: CodeBuild in VPC cannot access internet by default — needs NAT Gateway for internet access
• TRAP: CodeBuild service role must have permissions to access CodeArtifact, S3, Secrets Manager, ECR, etc.
• TRAP: Docker builds in CodeBuild require the privileged flag to be enabled in the build environment

aws-codecommit.text:
• Fully managed private Git repository hosting service — no server management
• Encrypted at rest (KMS) and in transit (HTTPS/SSH)
• Integrates with CodePipeline (source action), CodeBuild, Lambda triggers, SNS notifications
• Authentication: HTTPS (Git credentials generated from IAM console, or AWS CLI credential helper), SSH (upload public key to IAM)
• IAM-based access control — no separate user management; use IAM policies and groups
• Repository triggers: invoke Lambda or SNS on push events for custom workflows
• Important note: AWS announced CodeCommit is no longer available to new customers (July 2024) — existing customers continue; exam may still include it

• Scenario: host private Git repos within AWS, integrated with CI/CD pipeline → CodeCommit
• Scenario: notify team via SNS when a developer pushes to main branch → CodeCommit repository trigger → SNS
• Scenario: enforce that developers use MFA when accessing CodeCommit → IAM policy with MFA condition on codecommit:* actions

• TRAP: CodeCommit does NOT support Git LFS (Large File Storage) natively — use S3 for binary assets
• TRAP: CodeCommit access is controlled entirely by IAM — no built-in UI for PR reviews like GitHub
• TRAP: SSH keys for CodeCommit are uploaded to IAM user settings — NOT the same as EC2 key pairs

aws-codedeploy.text:
• Fully managed deployment service — automates application deployments to EC2, on-premises servers, Lambda, ECS
• Works with any programming language and OS
• Does NOT provision infrastructure — deploys to already-existing targets
• Application specification: appspec.yml (EC2/on-prem) or appspec.json (Lambda/ECS)
• Deployment Group: set of EC2 instances, Lambda functions, or ECS services to deploy to

• appspec.yml must be at the ROOT of the application revision (in S3 or CodeCommit)
• EC2/on-prem lifecycle event order:
  1. ApplicationStop
  2. DownloadBundle
  3. BeforeInstall
  4. Install
  5. AfterInstall
  6. ApplicationStart
  7. ValidateService
• Lambda lifecycle: BeforeAllowTraffic → AllowTraffic → AfterAllowTraffic
• ECS lifecycle: BeforeInstall → Install → AfterInstall → AllowTestTraffic → AfterAllowTestTraffic → BeforeAllowTraffic → AllowTraffic → AfterAllowTraffic

• In-place (EC2/on-prem only): stop old version, install new version on same instance; brief downtime per instance
• Blue/Green:
  - EC2: provision new instances, deploy, shift load balancer traffic, terminate old instances
  - Lambda: traffic shifted from current function version to new version (canary, linear, all-at-once)
  - ECS: new task set with new container version; traffic shifted via ALB; old task set terminated
• Deployment Configurations (EC2):
  - CodeDeployDefault.AllAtOnce: all instances at once — fastest, highest risk
  - CodeDeployDefault.HalfAtATime: 50% at a time
  - CodeDeployDefault.OneAtATime: one at a time — slowest, safest
  - Custom: define minimum healthy hosts (% or count)
• Lambda Traffic Shifting:
  - Canary: e.g., 10% → wait → 90% (two increments)
  - Linear: e.g., 10% every 5 minutes until 100%
  - All-at-once: immediate shift

• Automatic Rollback: triggered by deployment failure or CloudWatch Alarm threshold breach
• Manual Rollback: redeploy previous revision
• CodeDeploy Agent: must be installed and running on EC2/on-prem instances; communicates with CodeDeploy service
• Amazon Linux 2 / Ubuntu: install via yum/apt or include in AMI/user data
• CloudWatch Alarms: monitor metrics during deployment; trigger rollback if alarm fires

• Scenario: zero-downtime deployment to EC2 fleet → CodeDeploy Blue/Green
• Scenario: gradually shift Lambda traffic to new version → CodeDeploy Lambda Canary or Linear
• Scenario: automatically rollback if error rate increases after deployment → CodeDeploy + CloudWatch Alarm-triggered rollback
• Scenario: deploy to on-premises servers AND EC2 with same tooling → CodeDeploy (both supported)
• Scenario: need deployment lifecycle hooks (run tests before/after deploy) → appspec.yml lifecycle hooks

• TRAP: CodeDeploy does NOT provision infrastructure — Elastic Beanstalk or CloudFormation does that
• TRAP: CodeDeploy Agent must be installed on EC2/on-prem instances — Fargate/Lambda don't need it
• TRAP: appspec.yml must be in root of deployment bundle — not in a subdirectory
• TRAP: Blue/Green for EC2 requires a Load Balancer — cannot do Blue/Green without ELB
• TRAP: In-place deployment to all instances simultaneously (AllAtOnce) causes downtime — use OneAtATime or custom for production

aws-codepipeline.text:
• Fully managed CI/CD pipeline orchestration service — automates build, test, and release process
• Visual pipeline with stages and actions; each stage passes artifacts to the next
• Integrates with AWS and third-party services at each stage
• Charged per active pipeline per month ($1/month per pipeline after first free)

• Source stage: CodeCommit, GitHub (v1/v2 connection), S3, ECR, Bitbucket
• Build stage: CodeBuild, Jenkins
• Test stage: CodeBuild, AWS Device Farm, third-party test services
• Deploy stage: CodeDeploy, CloudFormation, Elastic Beanstalk, ECS, S3, Service Catalog, Amplify
• Approval stage: Manual approval — sends SNS notification; pipeline pauses until approved or rejected
• Invoke stage: Lambda function, Step Functions
• Artifacts: S3 bucket used to pass artifacts between stages; encrypted with KMS
• Cross-region actions: deploy to resources in multiple regions from a single pipeline

• Source change triggers: CodeCommit (CloudWatch Events), GitHub (webhook), S3 (CloudWatch Events)
• EventBridge: trigger pipelines based on any AWS event
• Schedule: trigger pipeline on a cron schedule via EventBridge
• Manual trigger: start pipeline execution from console or CLI
• Pipeline variables: pass variable values from one action to another within same execution

• Scenario: automate code push → build → test → deploy to prod → CodePipeline with CodeCommit + CodeBuild + CodeDeploy
• Scenario: require manager approval before deploying to production → CodePipeline Manual Approval action + SNS notification
• Scenario: deploy to multiple regions → CodePipeline with cross-region Deploy actions
• Scenario: trigger pipeline when Docker image is pushed to ECR → ECR as source + CodePipeline
• Scenario: full CI/CD for ECS application → CodePipeline → CodeBuild (build + push to ECR) → CodeDeploy ECS Blue/Green

• TRAP: CodePipeline is an ORCHESTRATOR — it does NOT build or deploy itself; it coordinates CodeBuild, CodeDeploy, etc.
• TRAP: Pipeline artifacts are stored in S3 — ensure CodePipeline service role has S3 access
• TRAP: pipeline stages must complete in order — parallel actions within a stage are possible, but stages are sequential
• TRAP: CodePipeline does NOT support rollback natively — you must run the pipeline again with previous version
• TRAP: Manual Approval action can expire — configure timeout and re-run if approval window missed

aws-x-ray.text:
• Distributed tracing service — analyze and debug production distributed applications and microservices
• Provides end-to-end view of requests as they flow through your application
• Identifies performance bottlenecks, latency, errors, and throttles across services
• Service Map: visual graph showing all application components and their connections + health

• Trace: end-to-end path of a request through all services; composed of Segments
• Segment: data about the work done by a single service/resource (e.g., Lambda, EC2 app, DynamoDB call)
• Subsegment: granular detail within a segment (individual DB query, HTTP call, external API)
• Annotations: indexed key-value pairs (string, number, boolean) — used to FILTER and group traces in console
• Metadata: non-indexed key-value data — stored in trace but NOT searchable/filterable
• Sampling: X-Ray does NOT record 100% of requests by default
  - Default rule: 1 request/second + 5% of additional requests
  - Custom sampling rules: configure per route, method, service

• X-Ray Daemon: sidecar UDP listener (port 2000) that buffers and sends trace data to X-Ray API
  - Must be running on EC2 instances and ECS containers
  - Lambda: daemon runs automatically — just enable Active Tracing in function config
  - Elastic Beanstalk: enable via Beanstalk config or .ebextensions
• X-Ray SDK: instrument application code to send trace data
  - Intercepts: incoming HTTP requests, outgoing AWS SDK calls, SQL queries, HTTP clients
  - Supported: Java, Python, Go, Ruby, .NET, Node.js
• X-Ray API: ingest trace data (PutTraceSegments), read traces (GetTraceSummaries, GetTrace)

• Lambda: active tracing checkbox — auto-instruments Lambda function + downstream calls
• API Gateway: enable X-Ray tracing per stage — traces from client through API GW to backend
• ECS: run X-Ray Daemon as sidecar container in task definition
• EC2: install X-Ray Daemon; IAM role must have xray:PutTraceSegments + xray:PutTelemetryRecords
• Elastic Beanstalk: enable X-Ray daemon via platform option
• SNS, SQS, DynamoDB, S3, Aurora, RDS Proxy: auto-instrumented via SDK

• Scenario: microservices app has high latency — need to identify which service is slow → X-Ray Service Map + Traces
• Scenario: Lambda function calling DynamoDB — trace end-to-end → Enable Active Tracing on Lambda (X-Ray auto-instruments downstream calls)
• Scenario: filter traces by specific user ID → use X-Ray Annotations (indexed) with userId as annotation key
• Scenario: no traces appearing in X-Ray → check X-Ray Daemon is running + IAM role has xray:PutTraceSegments

• TRAP: X-Ray does NOT record 100% of requests by default — configure custom sampling rules for higher coverage
• TRAP: Annotations are indexed (filterable); Metadata is NOT indexed — use Annotations for trace filtering
• TRAP: EC2 requires manually installing X-Ray Daemon AND proper IAM permissions — not automatic
• TRAP: X-Ray ≠ CloudWatch — X-Ray = distributed tracing across services; CloudWatch = metrics, logs, alarms
• TRAP: X-Ray does NOT alert you — it's an analysis tool; use CloudWatch Alarms for proactive alerting

aws-amplify.text:
• Full-stack development platform for building web and mobile apps on AWS
• Abstracts and provisions backend AWS services with minimal configuration
• Components: Amplify Studio (visual builder), Amplify CLI (command-line), Amplify Libraries (client SDKs), Amplify Hosting (CI/CD + CDN)
• Supports: React, Next.js, Vue, Angular, React Native, Flutter, iOS, Android

• Authentication: backed by Cognito User Pools — sign-up, sign-in, MFA, social federation (Google, Apple, Facebook)
• API: REST (API Gateway + Lambda) or GraphQL (AppSync)
• Storage: S3 for file storage with per-user or public access control
• Database: DynamoDB via AppSync; or direct RDS via custom Lambda resolvers
• Functions: Lambda for custom backend logic
• Analytics: Pinpoint for user analytics and engagement
• Push Notifications: via Pinpoint and SNS

• CI/CD + global CDN hosting for frontend web apps
• Connect to GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket, CodeCommit — auto-deploy on git push
• Deploy environments per branch (e.g., main → production; dev → staging)
• Supports: SSR (Next.js), SSG, SPA (React, Vue, Angular)
• Custom domains with automatic HTTPS (via ACM)
• Pull request previews: automatically deploy preview URL for each PR
• Password protection: restrict preview environments to authorized users

• Scenario: build a mobile app with user auth, API, and file uploads quickly on AWS → Amplify
• Scenario: host a Next.js app with CI/CD from GitHub → Amplify Hosting
• Scenario: need different backend environments per feature branch → Amplify branch deployments
• Exam distinction: Amplify vs Elastic Beanstalk → Amplify is for web/mobile frontend + serverless backend; Beanstalk is for server-side app hosting on EC2

• TRAP: Amplify is NOT serverless compute — it provisions serverless services (Lambda, AppSync, Cognito) behind the scenes
• TRAP: Amplify Hosting is for STATIC and SSR web apps — not for backend APIs or long-running servers
• TRAP: Amplify configures Cognito, not a custom auth system — if you need custom auth logic, you must extend via Lambda triggers
• TRAP: Amplify environments are NOT the same as AWS environments — Amplify env = branch-specific backend; not related to VPC or account environments

aws-appsync.text:
• Fully managed GraphQL and Pub/Sub API service
• Connect to multiple data sources with a single GraphQL endpoint
• Real-time subscriptions: push data updates to clients via WebSockets (without polling)
• Offline synchronization: Amplify DataStore uses AppSync for conflict resolution
• Managed scalability, availability, and security — no infrastructure to manage

• Supported data sources: DynamoDB, Lambda, RDS (Aurora Serverless v1 via Data API), OpenSearch, HTTP endpoints, EventBridge, None (local resolvers)
• Resolvers: connect GraphQL fields to data sources
  - VTL (Velocity Template Language) resolvers: transform request/response without Lambda
  - JS Resolvers (APPSYNC_JS runtime): use JavaScript for resolver logic
  - Direct Lambda Resolver: invoke Lambda directly — most flexible for complex logic
• Pipeline Resolvers: chain multiple functions (with different data sources) for one GraphQL operation

• API Key: simple token for public/test access; expires in 1–365 days
• AWS IAM: sign requests with SigV4; for backend services and Cognito Identity Pool credentials
• Cognito User Pools: use Cognito JWT for authenticated users; most common for user-facing apps
• OpenID Connect (OIDC): integrate with any OIDC-compliant IdP (Okta, Auth0, etc.)
• Lambda Authorizer: custom authorization logic — invoke Lambda to validate token and return policy
• Multi-Auth: configure multiple authorization modes; default + additional per field

• Server-side caching: cache at resolver level or full-request level; TTL configurable
• Reduces downstream calls to DynamoDB/Lambda for frequently requested data
• Cache encryption: enable at rest and in transit
• Batching: enable batching for DynamoDB data source — reduces read/write operations

• Scenario: mobile app needs real-time data updates without polling → AppSync subscriptions (WebSocket)
• Scenario: single API endpoint that aggregates data from DynamoDB + Lambda + RDS → AppSync with multiple data sources
• Scenario: GraphQL API with per-user authorization → AppSync + Cognito User Pools auth
• Scenario: build collaborative app with offline sync → AppSync + Amplify DataStore
• Exam distinction: AppSync (GraphQL, real-time, multi-source) vs API Gateway (REST/HTTP, simpler, more mature)

• TRAP: AppSync is GraphQL, not REST — if the question says REST API, the answer is usually API Gateway
• TRAP: AppSync subscriptions use WebSockets — different from HTTP polling; client must support WebSocket
• TRAP: DynamoDB with AppSync uses VTL resolvers by default — complex queries may require Lambda resolver
• TRAP: AppSync does NOT support all DynamoDB query patterns natively — complex joins/filters may need Lambda

amazon-comprehend.text:
• Natural Language Processing (NLP) service — extract insights from unstructured text using ML
• No ML expertise required — fully managed
• Capabilities:
  - Entity recognition: people, places, organizations, dates, quantities, commercial items
  - Key phrase extraction: identify important phrases
  - Language detection: identify the dominant language (up to 25 languages)
  - Sentiment analysis: Positive, Negative, Neutral, Mixed — per document or per sentence
  - PII detection and redaction: identify and optionally redact PII (names, SSNs, credit cards)
  - Custom Classification: train your own document classifier using your labeled data
  - Custom Entity Recognition: train model to detect domain-specific entities

• Specialized variant for healthcare and life sciences text
• Detects medical entities: medications, dosage, frequency, medical conditions, anatomy, tests/procedures
• PHI (Protected Health Information) detection and redaction
• ICD-10-CM and RxNorm ontology linking — standardize medical entities to codes
• Use case: automate medical record analysis, clinical trial matching, adverse event detection

• Scenario: analyze thousands of customer reviews for sentiment → Comprehend sentiment analysis
• Scenario: automatically classify support tickets into categories → Comprehend Custom Classifier
• Scenario: redact PII from documents before storing → Comprehend PII detection + redaction
• Scenario: process clinical notes to extract diagnoses and medications → Comprehend Medical
• Exam distinction: Comprehend = analyze text content (NLP); Textract = extract text FROM documents (OCR+); Translate = translate between languages

• TRAP: Comprehend analyzes text content — it does NOT extract text from images or PDFs (use Textract for that)
• TRAP: Comprehend does NOT translate — use Translate for language translation
• TRAP: Comprehend Custom models require labeled training data (minimum 10 documents per class)
• TRAP: Comprehend Medical is a separate API from standard Comprehend — not the same service

amazon-kendra.text:
• Intelligent enterprise search service powered by ML
• Returns precise answers (question-answering), not just document links
• Understands natural language queries — conversational search experience
• Indexes documents from diverse data sources; learns from user feedback over time
• Fully managed — no ML expertise needed

• Native connectors: S3, SharePoint, Salesforce, ServiceNow, OneDrive, Confluence, GitHub, RDS, Aurora, custom WebCrawler
• Supported document types: PDF, HTML, XML, JSON, Microsoft Word, PowerPoint, plain text
• Index: stores processed document content and metadata for search
• Relevance tuning: boost certain fields or document types to influence search ranking
• Incremental learning: Kendra improves relevance based on user interactions (thumbs up/down, click-through)

• Scenario: employees can't find information across SharePoint, S3, and Confluence → Kendra enterprise search
• Scenario: customer service chatbot needs to answer questions from knowledge base docs → Kendra + Lex
• Scenario: give users ability to ask natural language questions about company policies → Kendra
• Exam distinction: Kendra = intelligent document search/Q&A; Comprehend = analyze text; OpenSearch = full-text search/analytics

• TRAP: Kendra is NOT a vector database or RAG service — it's a managed search service; for generative AI RAG, use Knowledge Bases for Amazon Bedrock
• TRAP: Kendra is NOT real-time — indexing takes time after connector sync
• TRAP: Kendra requires index creation with data sources — not a simple API call to search a document

amazon-lex.text:
• Build conversational chatbots and voice interfaces using the same technology as Alexa
• Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR): convert speech to text
• Natural Language Understanding (NLU): recognize user intent from text or speech
• Fully managed — no ML infrastructure to provision
• Deploy to: web, mobile apps, Slack, Twilio, Facebook Messenger, Kendra, Lambda

• Bot: the chatbot/voice assistant you build
• Intent: what the user wants to do (e.g., BookFlight, OrderPizza, GetBalance)
• Utterances: sample phrases that trigger an intent (e.g., 'I want to book a flight')
• Slots: variables to collect from user (e.g., destination city, departure date)
• Slot Types: built-in (dates, cities, numbers) or custom (your own values)
• Fulfillment: Lambda function that processes the intent and returns a response
• Conversation flow: Lex collects required slots → invokes Lambda for fulfillment → returns response

• Scenario: customer service chatbot that books appointments → Lex with booking intent + Lambda fulfillment
• Scenario: voice-enabled ordering system → Lex (ASR + NLU) + Lambda
• Scenario: chatbot that searches knowledge base for answers → Lex + Kendra integration
• Exam distinction: Lex = build chatbots/voice UIs; Polly = text-to-speech; Transcribe = audio-to-text transcription

• TRAP: Lex ≠ Transcribe — Lex understands INTENT; Transcribe just converts speech to text transcript
• TRAP: Lex ≠ Polly — Lex can listen and understand; Polly speaks (text-to-speech); they're often used together
• TRAP: Lex requires Lambda for fulfillment logic — without Lambda, Lex can only have simple static responses

amazon-polly.text:
• Turns text into lifelike speech using deep learning
• Standard Voices: concatenative synthesis — natural but not as expressive
• Neural Voices (NTTS): neural TTS — much more natural and expressive; higher quality
• Newscaster style: special neural voice for news broadcast-style delivery
• Conversational style: optimized for back-and-forth dialogue (some voices)
• 60+ voices across 30+ languages

• SSML (Speech Synthesis Markup Language): XML-based markup to control speech
  - Pause: <break time='2s'/>
  - Emphasis: <emphasis level='strong'>important</emphasis>
  - Whisper: <amazon:effect name='whispered'>secret</amazon:effect>
  - Phonemes: specify exact pronunciation
• Lexicons: customize pronunciation of specific words (e.g., brand names, acronyms) stored per region
• Speech Marks: return JSON metadata about word/sentence timing and position in audio stream — for lip sync, karaoke highlighting
• Streaming synthesis: return audio stream directly; Storage synthesis: output to S3

• Scenario: make website accessible for visually impaired users → Polly to speak page content
• Scenario: generate audio version of articles automatically → Polly neural voice + S3 storage
• Scenario: ensure brand name 'Acme' is pronounced correctly in speech → Polly Lexicon
• Scenario: build voice response for chatbot built with Lex → Lex (understands) + Polly (speaks)
• Scenario: create karaoke app highlighting words as they're spoken → Polly Speech Marks

• TRAP: Polly ≠ Transcribe — Polly converts text TO speech; Transcribe converts speech TO text
• TRAP: Polly ≠ Lex — Polly is purely text-to-speech; Lex is conversational AI understanding intent
• TRAP: Neural voices are only available for certain languages and regions — check availability
• TRAP: Lexicons are region-specific — must recreate in each region you use Polly

amazon-rekognition.text:
• ML-powered image and video analysis — no ML expertise required
• Image capabilities:
  - Object and scene detection (labels): 'cat', 'outdoor', 'car'
  - Facial analysis: gender, age range, emotions, glasses, eyes open/closed
  - Face comparison: compare faces across images (not recognition of WHO — just SAME person)
  - Face search: match face against a collection of known faces (recognition)
  - Text detection: read text in images (simpler than Textract; single-layer text in images)
  - Content moderation: detect unsafe/explicit content (nudity, violence, drugs, etc.)
  - Custom Labels: train model to detect your domain-specific objects or scenes

• Rekognition Video: analyze stored video (from S3) or streaming video (from Kinesis Video Streams)
• Asynchronous video analysis: start job → receive SNS notification → get results
• Capabilities in video: detect people, track faces across frames, detect activities, content moderation
• Streaming analysis: real-time detection from live video feeds
• Use cases: smart home security, sports analytics, industrial safety monitoring

• Scenario: automatically moderate user-uploaded images for inappropriate content → Rekognition content moderation
• Scenario: identity verification — compare user's selfie to ID photo → Rekognition face comparison
• Scenario: detect PPE (hard hats, vests) in workplace images → Rekognition Custom Labels
• Scenario: detect celebrities in uploaded photos → Rekognition celebrity recognition
• Scenario: real-time monitoring of warehouse camera for safety violations → Rekognition Video + Kinesis Video Streams
• Exam distinction: Rekognition = image/video visual analysis; Textract = structured text/form extraction from documents

• TRAP: Rekognition ≠ Textract — Rekognition detects objects/faces/labels in images; Textract extracts structured text/forms from documents
• TRAP: Face comparison (CompareFaces) does NOT identify WHO the person is — it only determines if two faces are the SAME person
• TRAP: Face search (SearchFaces) requires a Face Collection to be indexed first with IndexFaces API
• TRAP: Rekognition text detection is for simple text in images (signs, labels) — NOT for multi-column documents (use Textract for that)

amazon-sagemaker-ai.text:
• Fully managed end-to-end ML platform — build, train, deploy, and monitor ML models
• Removes heavy lifting from each step of the ML workflow
• SageMaker Studio: unified web-based IDE for all ML development tasks
• SageMaker Notebooks (Studio/Classic): managed Jupyter notebooks with pre-installed ML libraries

• Classification/Regression: XGBoost, Linear Learner, KNN, Factorization Machines
• Clustering: K-Means
• Computer Vision: Image Classification, Object Detection, Semantic Segmentation
• NLP: Seq2Seq, BlazingText (Word2Vec, text classification), Neural Topic Model
• Anomaly Detection: Random Cut Forest, IP Insights
• Recommendation: Factorization Machines
• Time Series: DeepAR

• Training Jobs: pull data from S3, train on managed ML instances, save model artifacts to S3
• Instance types for training: ml.m/c/r/p/g/trn families; p3/p4 for GPU training
• Spot Training: use Spot Instances for training — up to 90% savings; enable checkpointing to S3 to resume interrupted jobs
• Batch Transform: run batch inference on large datasets — no persistent endpoint; cost-effective for non-real-time inference
• Real-time Endpoints: persistent HTTPS endpoint for low-latency predictions; auto-scales
• Serverless Inference: pay-per-request; scales to zero; for infrequent inference
• Asynchronous Inference: queue large requests; output to S3; for long-running inference
• Multi-Model Endpoints: host multiple models on one endpoint to reduce cost

• SageMaker Autopilot: AutoML — automatically explores data, trains models, tunes hyperparameters, picks best model; full visibility
• SageMaker Pipelines: CI/CD for ML — define, automate, and manage end-to-end ML workflows as DAGs
• SageMaker Feature Store: central repository to store, retrieve, and share ML features; online (low-latency) and offline (S3) stores
• SageMaker Model Monitor: detect data drift and model quality degradation in production
• SageMaker Clarify: detect bias in datasets and model predictions; explain model behavior (feature importance)
• SageMaker Ground Truth: human labeling service for training data; auto-labeling for obvious cases
• SageMaker JumpStart: pre-trained foundation models and ML solutions you can fine-tune and deploy

• Scenario: train a fraud detection model on historical transactions in S3 → SageMaker Training Job
• Scenario: reduce training cost for long ML training jobs → SageMaker Spot Training + checkpointing
• Scenario: data scientist needs no-code AutoML → SageMaker Autopilot
• Scenario: detect if deployed model is drifting from baseline → SageMaker Model Monitor
• Scenario: run batch predictions on 1M records overnight → SageMaker Batch Transform
• Scenario: label 100,000 images for training data → SageMaker Ground Truth

• TRAP: SageMaker does NOT automatically retrain models when data drifts — Model Monitor detects drift; you must trigger retraining separately
• TRAP: Spot Training may be interrupted — always enable checkpointing to S3 to avoid losing progress
• TRAP: Real-time Endpoint ≠ Batch Transform — Real-time = persistent, low-latency; Batch Transform = offline, non-persistent, cheaper per inference at scale
• TRAP: SageMaker Ground Truth is NOT automatic labeling — it uses humans + ML-assisted labeling; Autopilot is AutoML

amazon-textract.text:
• ML service to automatically extract text and data from scanned documents and images
• Goes beyond simple OCR — understands document structure
• Capabilities:
  - Raw text extraction: detect and extract printed and handwritten text
  - Form extraction: identify key-value pairs (field label → field value) from forms
  - Table extraction: detect and extract table data with row/column structure
  - Queries: ask specific questions about a document (e.g., 'What is the date?')
  - Signature detection: identify presence of a signature
• Supported formats: JPEG, PNG, TIFF, PDF (up to 3,000 pages)

• Synchronous (DetectDocumentText, AnalyzeDocument): single-page images only; returns immediately; max 10 MB
• Asynchronous (StartDocumentAnalysis, GetDocumentAnalysis): multi-page PDFs; start job → poll/SNS notification → get results
• Human Review: Amazon Augmented AI (A2I) integration — route low-confidence extractions to human reviewers

• Scenario: process thousands of W-2 tax forms automatically → Textract form extraction → Lambda to store in DynamoDB
• Scenario: digitize handwritten medical intake forms → Textract (supports handwriting)
• Scenario: extract data from multi-page PDF contracts → Textract asynchronous API
• Scenario: validate extracted data with human review when confidence is low → Textract + A2I
• Exam distinction: Textract = extract text/structure FROM documents; Comprehend = analyze text CONTENT; Rekognition = image/video analysis

• TRAP: Textract is NOT just OCR — it understands forms and tables structurally; OCR only gives raw text
• TRAP: Synchronous API only supports single-page; use Asynchronous for multi-page PDFs
• TRAP: Textract does NOT translate — use Translate after extraction if translation is needed
• TRAP: Textract ≠ Rekognition — Textract is for document text extraction; Rekognition is for image/video visual analysis

amazon-transcribe.text:
• Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) — convert audio/video to text transcript
• Supports 100+ languages; streaming and batch transcription
• Key features:
  - Speaker diarization: identify and label different speakers ('Speaker 1:', 'Speaker 2:')
  - Custom Vocabulary: improve accuracy for domain-specific terms, names, acronyms
  - Custom Language Models: fine-tune for specific domain (medical, legal, technical)
  - PII redaction: automatically identify and redact PII in transcripts
  - Content Redaction: mask sensitive content
  - Vocabulary Filtering: remove profanity or specific words

• Optimized for clinical and medical speech recognition
• Understands medical terminology, physician dictation style
• Supports specialties: Primary Care, Cardiology, Neurology, Oncology, Radiology, Urology
• HIPAA-eligible
• Use case: physician dictation → EMR notes automation

• Scenario: transcribe customer service call recordings to text for analysis → Transcribe batch
• Scenario: real-time live captions for a video conferencing app → Transcribe streaming
• Scenario: identify who said what in a meeting recording → Transcribe + speaker diarization
• Scenario: transcribe physician notes automatically → Transcribe Medical
• Scenario: customer calls contain credit card numbers — need transcript without PII → Transcribe PII redaction
• Exam distinction: Transcribe = audio/speech to text; Polly = text to speech; Lex = understand intent from speech

• TRAP: Transcribe ≠ Translate — Transcribe converts speech to text in the SAME language; use Translate after to convert language
• TRAP: Transcribe ≠ Comprehend — Transcribe creates text transcripts; Comprehend analyzes text for insights
• TRAP: Transcribe Medical is a SEPARATE API from standard Transcribe — cannot mix them
• TRAP: Speaker diarization max speakers: 10 (batch); streaming has different limits

amazon-translate.text:
• Neural machine translation — translate text between 75+ languages with high accuracy
• Supports real-time translation (synchronous) and batch translation (asynchronous, stored in S3)
• Custom Terminology: upload a CSV/TMX file to ensure brand names, product names, or technical terms translate consistently
• Active Custom Translation (ACT): use parallel translation examples to improve quality for your domain
• Profanity masking: mask profanity in translated output
• Formality: control level of formality in output (formal vs informal) for supported languages

• Scenario: translate user-generated content (reviews, comments) to multiple languages in real-time → Translate API
• Scenario: translate thousands of product descriptions stored in S3 overnight → Translate Batch
• Scenario: ensure company name 'Acme Corp' is never translated but kept as-is → Custom Terminology
• Scenario: multilingual customer support chatbot → Lex (understand intent) + Translate (localize response) + Polly (speak)
• Exam distinction: Translate = language-to-language translation; Comprehend = language detection + NLP analysis

• TRAP: Translate does NOT detect language automatically by default — use Comprehend language detection first to identify source language, then pass to Translate
• TRAP: Translate is NOT real-time streaming — for real-time use the synchronous API; batch processes S3 files
• TRAP: Custom Terminology works best for EXACT term substitution — not for complex grammar restructuring

amazon-cloudwatch.text:
• Collects metrics from AWS services automatically and from custom sources
• Default EC2 metrics (free): CPU Utilization, Network In/Out, Disk Read/Write (NOT RAM or disk space)
• Detailed Monitoring: 1-minute granularity for EC2 (vs default 5-minute); costs extra
• Custom Metrics: publish your own metrics via PutMetricData API — RAM, disk space, application metrics
  - Standard resolution: 1-minute intervals; High Resolution: 1-second intervals (extra cost)
• Metric Math: create new metrics by combining existing ones with math expressions
• CloudWatch Alarms: trigger actions when metrics breach thresholds
  - States: OK, ALARM, INSUFFICIENT_DATA
  - Actions: EC2 actions (stop/start/terminate/reboot), Auto Scaling (add/remove capacity), SNS notification
  - Evaluation Periods: N of M data points must breach threshold to alarm (avoid flapping)
  - Composite Alarms: combine multiple alarms with AND/OR logic — reduce alarm noise

• Collect, store, monitor, and analyze log data
• Log Group: logical container of log streams (one per application/service)
• Log Stream: sequence of log events from the same source (one per instance/container/function)
• Retention: 1 day to 10 years (or Never Expire); default = Never Expire (costs money to store indefinitely)
• Sources: EC2 (CloudWatch Agent), Lambda (auto), ECS/Fargate (awslogs driver), API Gateway, CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs, Route 53
• CloudWatch Agent: installed on EC2/on-premises to collect logs AND system-level metrics (RAM, disk)
• Log Insights: interactive SQL-like query language for searching and analyzing log data in CloudWatch Logs
  - Query across multiple log groups simultaneously
  - Visualize results as bar/line charts
• Metric Filters: parse log data and create CloudWatch Metrics from log patterns (e.g., count errors per minute)
  - Trigger Alarms from log-derived metrics
• Subscription Filters: real-time stream log data to Lambda, Kinesis Data Streams, or Firehose
• Cross-account log sharing: use Subscription Filters to centralize logs from multiple accounts

• Container Insights: collect, aggregate, and summarize metrics and logs from ECS, EKS, Kubernetes on EC2
  - Provides cluster/service/task/pod level metrics as CloudWatch Metrics
  - Powered by the CloudWatch Agent or Fluent Bit daemonset
• Lambda Insights: detailed lambda performance metrics — cold starts, memory usage, latency — as CloudWatch Metrics
• Contributor Insights: identify top contributors (top IPs, users, URLs) causing issues in logs — real-time
• Application Insights: automated dashboards for .NET and SQL-based applications on EC2
• CloudWatch Synthetics: configurable scripts (Canaries) that run on schedule to monitor endpoints and APIs

• Dashboards: create custom visualizations; global (no region selector); cross-account + cross-region possible
• CloudWatch Events: respond to state changes in AWS resources → create rules to match events → trigger targets
  - Now superseded by EventBridge (same underlying service — default event bus)
• Anomaly Detection: ML-based baseline for metrics; alarm when metric goes outside expected band

• Scenario: monitor EC2 RAM usage → install CloudWatch Agent (RAM is not a default metric)
• Scenario: alert when 5XX errors appear in ALB logs → CloudWatch Metric Filter on ALB log group → Alarm → SNS
• Scenario: stream application logs to S3 for long-term archival → CloudWatch Logs Subscription Filter → Firehose → S3
• Scenario: query logs across all Lambda functions to find error patterns → CloudWatch Log Insights
• Scenario: reduce alert noise from many individual alarms → Composite Alarm (AND/OR logic)
• Scenario: monitor custom application metric (e.g., active users) → PutMetricData API → CloudWatch Alarm

• Default EC2 metrics: 5-minute granularity; Detailed Monitoring: 1 minute
• Custom Metric high resolution: 1 second
• CloudWatch Alarm period minimum: 10 seconds (high resolution); 1 minute (standard)
• TRAP: RAM and disk space are NOT default EC2 metrics — must install CloudWatch Agent
• TRAP: CloudWatch Logs retention is Never Expire by default — costs accumulate; always set retention policy
• TRAP: CloudWatch Alarms on high-resolution metrics must match the resolution — you can't alarm on 1-second data with a 5-minute evaluation period
• TRAP: Log Insights queries cost $0.005 per GB scanned — optimize queries to reduce cost
• TRAP: CloudWatch Events = same as EventBridge default event bus — same service, different name

aws-cloudformation.text:
• Infrastructure as Code (IaC) — define AWS resources declaratively in YAML or JSON templates
• Provisions resources as a Stack (collection of AWS resources managed together)
• Free service — pay only for provisioned resources
• Template sections (only Resources is required):
  - AWSTemplateFormatVersion: '2010-09-09' (always use this version)
  - Description: text description of template
  - Parameters: input values at deploy time (with Type, Default, AllowedValues, etc.)
  - Mappings: static key-value lookup tables (e.g., AMI IDs per region)
  - Conditions: conditionally create resources (e.g., create ElasticIP only in Prod)
  - Resources: REQUIRED — all AWS resources to provision
  - Outputs: values to export from stack (e.g., VPC ID, S3 bucket name) for cross-stack references

• Stack creation: CloudFormation provisions resources in correct dependency order
• Change Sets: preview what will change before executing an update — review additions/modifications/deletions
• Drift Detection: identify manual changes (out-of-band) to stack resources vs template definition
• Stack Rollback: automatically rolls back on failure (all resources or individual resources)
• Termination Protection: prevent accidental stack deletion — enable per stack
• DeletionPolicy per resource:
  - Delete (default): delete resource when stack is deleted
  - Retain: keep resource when stack is deleted
  - Snapshot: take snapshot before deletion (EBS, RDS, ElastiCache, Redshift)

• Nested Stacks: embed stacks within stacks — reuse template components; parent stack manages child stacks
• StackSets: deploy the same CloudFormation template across multiple accounts AND regions simultaneously
  - Trust relationship required; use Organizations for automatic deployment to accounts/OUs
