• Cross-Stack References: export Output values from one stack and import (Fn::ImportValue) in another
• CloudFormation Registry: manage and use third-party resource types and modules
• cfn-init: CloudFormation helper script — runs on EC2 instance to apply resource metadata (packages, files, services)
• cfn-signal: signal CloudFormation when instance is fully configured (for CreationPolicy WaitCondition)

• Ref: reference a parameter or resource (returns logical ID or parameter value)
• Fn::GetAtt: get an attribute of a resource (e.g., !GetAtt MyBucket.Arn)
• Fn::Sub: substitute variables in a string (cleaner than Fn::Join for complex strings)
• Fn::If: conditional resource property based on a Condition
• Fn::FindInMap: look up a value from Mappings
• Fn::Select: select item from a list by index
• Fn::Split / Fn::Join: split or join strings

• Scenario: deploy same VPC + EC2 + RDS architecture across 5 AWS accounts → StackSets
• Scenario: safely preview changes before updating a stack → Change Sets
• Scenario: resource was manually changed in console, detect difference → Drift Detection
• Scenario: ensure RDS is not deleted when stack is deleted → DeletionPolicy: Snapshot on RDS resource
• Scenario: reuse common VPC template across multiple stacks → Nested Stacks
• Scenario: EC2 instance needs to finish software installation before stack shows CREATE_COMPLETE → cfn-init + cfn-signal + CreationPolicy

• TRAP: CloudFormation does NOT auto-deploy when template changes — you must manually update the stack or use a CI/CD pipeline
• TRAP: DeletionPolicy: Retain means the resource exists outside CloudFormation after stack deletion — not managed by CloudFormation anymore
• TRAP: StackSets require appropriate permissions — self-managed (IAM roles) or service-managed (Organizations)
• TRAP: Cross-stack references require the exporting stack's Output to exist before the importing stack is created
• TRAP: Nested stack updates: updating a child stack template requires updating the parent stack (change the template URL)

aws-cloudtrail.text:
• Records API calls and console actions across your AWS account — audit trail for governance and compliance
• Enabled by default — last 90 days of management events visible in Event History (console, no S3 needed)
• Trail: configuration to deliver logs to S3 bucket (and optionally CloudWatch Logs) for indefinite retention
• Scope: All Regions (recommended — captures all activity) or Single Region
• SSE-S3 encryption by default; optionally use SSE-KMS for additional control

• Management Events (Control Plane): operations on AWS resources — CreateBucket, RunInstances, CreateUser, DeleteTable
  - Logged by default; separate Read and Write events (can filter)
• Data Events (Data Plane): resource-specific operations on data — S3 GetObject/PutObject, Lambda Invoke, DynamoDB GetItem
  - NOT logged by default (high volume); must enable per trail; costs extra
• Insights Events: detect unusual API call patterns (unusual write activity — e.g., spike in TerminateInstances)
  - CloudTrail compares current activity to a 7-day baseline
  - Delivered to S3 + EventBridge; must enable Insights per trail; costs extra

• Log File Integrity Validation: SHA-256 hashing + digital signing — detect if logs were tampered with or deleted
  - aws cloudtrail validate-logs command to verify
• Organization Trails: single trail captures all member accounts' events — managed from management account
  - Member accounts cannot modify or delete organization trails
• S3 bucket for CloudTrail: enable S3 Object Lock (WORM) for tamper-evident log storage (compliance)
• CloudWatch Logs integration: stream CloudTrail events to CloudWatch Logs → use Metric Filters + Alarms for real-time alerting
• EventBridge: react to CloudTrail events in near-real-time (< 15 minutes)

• Scenario: who deleted the S3 bucket yesterday? → CloudTrail Event History → filter by DeleteBucket event
• Scenario: set up alerting when root account is used → CloudTrail → CloudWatch Logs → Metric Filter (root account usage) → Alarm → SNS
• Scenario: ensure CloudTrail logs cannot be tampered with for compliance → S3 Object Lock (WORM) on log bucket + Log File Integrity Validation
• Scenario: audit all API activity across 50 member accounts centrally → Organization Trail → S3
• Scenario: detect unusual spike in EC2 instance launches → CloudTrail Insights

• Default Event History retention: 90 days (console only; no S3 required)
• Trail delivers logs within ~15 minutes of API call
• TRAP: CloudTrail does NOT log all events by default — Data Events (S3, Lambda) must be explicitly enabled per trail
• TRAP: CloudTrail Insights is NOT enabled by default — must enable per trail; costs extra
• TRAP: CloudTrail ≠ CloudWatch — CloudTrail = API audit log; CloudWatch = metrics + performance monitoring
• TRAP: CloudTrail ≠ Config — CloudTrail = who did what; Config = what is the current/historical configuration of resources
• TRAP: CloudTrail logs from all regions go to the trail's designated S3 bucket — you don't need a trail per region if using an all-regions trail

aws-config.text:
• Assess, audit, and evaluate configurations of AWS resources continuously
• Records configuration history — snapshot of resource configuration at any point in time
• Evaluate compliance against Config Rules
• Regional service — aggregate across regions and accounts with Aggregators
• Stores configuration snapshots and change history in S3
• Does NOT prevent changes — it only records and evaluates (unless you set up auto-remediation)

• AWS Managed Rules: 150+ pre-built rules — encrypted-volumes, restricted-ssh, s3-bucket-public-read-prohibited, etc.
• Custom Rules: Lambda-backed rules — define your own compliance logic in any language
• Evaluation triggers:
  - Configuration change: evaluate when resource config changes
  - Periodic: evaluate on schedule (e.g., every 24 hours)
• Results: COMPLIANT or NON_COMPLIANT per resource per rule
• Scope: per resource type, per tag, or all supported resources

• Remediation: automatically remediate non-compliant resources using SSM Automation Documents
  - Auto-remediation: trigger automatically when non-compliant; retry up to 5 times
  - Manual remediation: trigger from console
  - Example: automatically enable S3 versioning on non-compliant buckets
• Conformance Packs: bundle of Config rules + remediation actions as a single deployable package
  - Pre-built packs: CIS Benchmark, NIST, HIPAA, PCI DSS, AWS Security Best Practices
  - Deploy via StackSets across multiple accounts/regions
• Config Aggregator: aggregate compliance data from multiple accounts and regions into one dashboard
  - Organization Aggregator: auto-adds all member accounts

• Scenario: detect when S3 buckets are made publicly accessible → Config rule: s3-bucket-public-read-prohibited
• Scenario: automatically remediate security groups that open port 22 to 0.0.0.0/0 → Config rule + SSM remediation action
• Scenario: prove to auditors that encryption was always enabled on EBS volumes → Config historical timeline
• Scenario: enforce compliance rules across 50 AWS accounts centrally → Conformance Pack deployed via Organizations
• Scenario: detect what changed in a resource's config last Thursday → Config Resource Timeline

• TRAP: Config does NOT prevent non-compliant changes — it detects and reports them; use SCPs or IAM to prevent
• TRAP: Config charges per configuration item recorded + per active rule + per conformance pack evaluation
• TRAP: Config ≠ CloudTrail — Config = WHAT the configuration IS (current/historical); CloudTrail = WHO changed it and WHEN via API call
• TRAP: Config recording must be enabled — it's not automatic; configure the Config Recorder first
• TRAP: Config remediation may fail if the SSM Automation Document doesn't have proper IAM permissions

aws-health-dashboard.text:
• Two components:
  1. Service Health Dashboard (status.aws.amazon.com): global, public view of all AWS service statuses across all regions — shows current incidents and historical uptime
  2. AWS Personal Health Dashboard (PHD) / AWS Health: personalized view of events affecting YOUR specific resources and account
• PHD shows: open issues, scheduled changes (maintenance), account notifications that impact resources YOU use

• AWS Health API: programmatic access to health events (requires Business or Enterprise support plan)
• EventBridge integration: create rules to react to AWS Health events automatically
  - Example: when an EC2 instance is scheduled for retirement → trigger Lambda to migrate workload
  - Example: when RDS maintenance is scheduled → notify team via SNS
• Organization Health View: see health events across all accounts in AWS Organizations (from management account)
• Health event types: issue (active problem), scheduledChange (upcoming maintenance), accountNotification (billing, policy changes)

• Scenario: automate response when an EC2 instance is flagged for retirement → AWS Health EventBridge rule → Lambda
• Scenario: centralized view of health events across 100 AWS accounts → Organization Health View
• Scenario: get notified before scheduled RDS maintenance → AWS Health EventBridge rule → SNS → email
• Scenario: check if an AWS service outage is affecting your account → Personal Health Dashboard

• TRAP: Service Health Dashboard is PUBLIC — anyone can view it; Personal Health Dashboard is account-specific
• TRAP: AWS Health API requires Business or Enterprise Support plan — not available on Basic/Developer plans
• TRAP: Health events are NOT real-time — there may be a short delay between an issue and it appearing in PHD
• TRAP: AWS Health ≠ CloudWatch — Health shows AWS-side infrastructure events; CloudWatch shows your application/resource metrics

aws-license-manager.text:
• Centrally manage software licenses from vendors across AWS and on-premises
• Supported license models: vCPU-based, physical core-based, socket-based, instance count-based
• Vendors supported: Microsoft (SQL Server, Windows Server), SAP, Oracle, IBM, and others
• Integrates with AWS Organizations for cross-account visibility
• Tracks license usage against entitlements to prevent over-provisioning (costly true-up events)

• License Configuration: define rules based on license terms (e.g., max 100 SQL Server licenses)
• Enforcement: optionally block launching instances that would exceed license limits (hard stop)
• License Rules: attach to AMIs, instance types, or launch templates — restrict usage to compliant configurations
• Managed Entitlements: track software subscriptions from AWS Marketplace vendors
• Automated Discovery: AWS Systems Manager Integration — discover installed software across EC2 and on-prem managed instances

• Scenario: track and enforce SQL Server license usage across EC2 to avoid audit risk → License Manager
• Scenario: need BYOL Windows Server on Dedicated Hosts with license compliance tracking → License Manager + Dedicated Hosts
• Scenario: centralize license governance across 30 AWS accounts → License Manager + Organizations

• TRAP: License Manager does NOT purchase licenses — it manages and tracks licenses you already own or subscribe to
• TRAP: License Manager enforcement is optional — you can configure it to only track (not block)
• TRAP: BYOL on Dedicated Hosts requires License Manager to be configured correctly — otherwise license compliance cannot be verified

aws-management-console.text:
• Web-based GUI to access, configure, and manage AWS services visually
• Access via browser — supports MFA (virtual MFA, hardware token, biometric)
• Cross-account role switching: switch to another account's role directly in the console
• Resource Groups: group AWS resources by tags or CloudFormation stack — manage and monitor together
• Tag Editor: search for resources across services/regions and add/edit tags in bulk
• Console Mobile App: limited management capabilities from mobile devices

• TRAP: Console actions are still recorded by CloudTrail — all console clicks = API calls underneath
• TRAP: Root account should NOT be used for daily console tasks — create IAM admin user instead
• TRAP: Console does NOT support programmatic automation — use CLI or SDK for automation
• TRAP: Console access requires username + password (+ MFA); API/CLI access uses Access Keys — these are separate credentials

aws-organizations.text:
• Manage and centrally govern multiple AWS accounts
• Hierarchy: Root → Organizational Units (OUs) → Member Accounts
• Management Account (Master): creates the organization; pays all bills; NOT restricted by SCPs
• Consolidated Billing: single payment method for all accounts; volume pricing discounts aggregated; shared Reserved Instances and Savings Plans across accounts
• Free service — no additional cost for Organizations itself

• Policy type that restricts permissions for member accounts and OUs
• SCPs do NOT grant permissions — they only restrict (define the maximum allowed permissions)
• Effective permissions = IAM policy permissions ∩ SCP permissions (intersection — most restrictive wins)
• SCPs apply to ALL principals in the account including root user of member account
• SCPs do NOT apply to the Management Account — management account always has full access
• SCP attached to OU: applies to all accounts in that OU and all child OUs (inheritance)
• Common use cases: prevent disabling CloudTrail, require MFA for root operations, restrict regions

• Tag Policies: enforce consistent tagging standards across accounts (define allowed tag keys and values)
• Backup Policies: centrally manage AWS Backup plans across all member accounts
• AI Services Opt-out Policies: opt out of AI services using your data for model improvement
• Delegated Administrator: assign a member account to manage a specific AWS service (e.g., Security Hub, GuardDuty, Macie) on behalf of the organization
• AWS Resource Access Manager (RAM): share resources (subnets, Transit Gateways, Route 53 rules) across accounts in the organization without sharing credentials
• AWS Account Management: create, close, and manage accounts programmatically

• Scenario: prevent any member account from disabling CloudTrail → SCP on root or OU: Deny cloudtrail:StopLogging
• Scenario: single billing across 100 accounts with volume discounts → Organizations Consolidated Billing
• Scenario: share Reserved Instances across accounts → automatically done with Consolidated Billing
• Scenario: enforce that all resources must be tagged with 'CostCenter' → Tag Policies
• Scenario: centrally manage GuardDuty across all accounts → Delegated Administrator + GuardDuty in organization mode
• Scenario: allow shared VPC subnets across accounts → RAM + Organizations (VPC sharing)

• Max accounts per organization: default 10 (soft limit, can increase significantly)
• TRAP: SCPs do NOT apply to the management account — always apply controls at member account level
• TRAP: SCP Deny + IAM Allow = DENIED — SCP takes precedence
• TRAP: Consolidated Billing shares Reserved Instance discounts — but can disable sharing if accounts prefer isolation
• TRAP: Removing an account from an org — the account becomes standalone; its resources remain; no SCP protection
• TRAP: An SCP attached to Root applies to ALL accounts including management? NO — management account is always exempt

aws-systems-manager.text:
• Operational management platform for EC2 instances, on-premises servers, and VMs
• SSM Agent: pre-installed on Amazon Linux 2, Amazon Linux 2023, Windows AMIs; must install manually on other OS
• Hybrid support: register on-premises servers as SSM Managed Instances via activation codes
• No open inbound ports required — SSM Agent initiates outbound HTTPS connection to SSM endpoints
• Most features are FREE (Parameter Store Standard, Run Command, etc.)

• Secure shell/RDP access to instances without opening SSH/RDP ports or using bastion hosts
• No key pair management — access controlled entirely by IAM
• Session logs: stream session activity to S3 or CloudWatch Logs for audit
• Port forwarding: tunnel to private resources (RDS, private services) through Session Manager
• Requires: SSM Agent running + IAM instance profile with ssm:StartSession permission
• Preferred method for secure, auditable instance access in production

• Run Command: execute scripts or commands across multiple managed instances without SSH
  - Run commands from console, CLI, Lambda, EventBridge
  - Target by instance ID, tag, resource group
  - Output to S3 or CloudWatch Logs; SNS notification on completion
  - Common use case: run shell scripts, install software, configure instances at scale
• Patch Manager: automate patching of OS and applications
  - Patch Baselines: define which patches to approve/reject
  - Patch Groups: tag instances to associate with baseline
  - Maintenance Windows: schedule patching during off-hours
  - Compliance reporting: view patching compliance in Config or SSM dashboard

• Secure, hierarchical key-value store for configuration and secrets
• Two tiers:
  - Standard: free; up to 10,000 parameters; max 4 KB per parameter; no throughput throttling
  - Advanced: $0.05/parameter/month; up to 8 KB; parameter policies (TTL, expiry notification)
• Parameter types: String, StringList, SecureString (encrypted with KMS)
• Hierarchical paths: /myapp/prod/db-password; /myapp/dev/db-password — control access per path with IAM
• Integration: Lambda, EC2 User Data, CodeBuild, CodeDeploy, ECS Task Definitions — reference parameters by name
• Versioning: every update creates a new version; can reference specific versions

• Automation: define and run multi-step operational tasks (runbooks) using SSM Documents
  - Common runbooks: AWS-RestartEC2Instance, AWS-CreateImage, AWS-ConfigureS3BucketLogging, etc.
  - Custom automation documents for complex workflows
  - Triggered by: console, CLI, Lambda, EventBridge, Config remediation
• SSM Documents: JSON or YAML workflows used by Run Command, Automation, Session Manager
• Inventory: collect metadata about managed instances — OS, applications, network config, files, registry
  - Query inventory in Console or export to S3 + Athena for analysis
• State Manager: define and maintain desired state of instances (install/configure software, configure network settings)

• Scenario: access EC2 in private subnet without opening port 22 → Session Manager (no bastion host needed)
• Scenario: run a patch script across 500 EC2 instances simultaneously → Run Command
• Scenario: store DB connection strings securely and rotate them → Parameter Store SecureString (or Secrets Manager for auto-rotation)
• Scenario: automatically patch EC2 instances on Sunday nights → Patch Manager + Maintenance Window
• Scenario: automate response to a Config non-compliance finding → Config rule + SSM Automation remediation

• TRAP: SSM Agent must be installed AND instance must have proper IAM Instance Profile (AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore policy) — both required
• TRAP: Parameter Store Standard is free; Secrets Manager charges per secret — for simple config without rotation, use Parameter Store
• TRAP: Parameter Store does NOT auto-rotate secrets — use Secrets Manager if auto-rotation is required
• TRAP: Session Manager requires VPC Endpoint (com.amazonaws.region.ssm, ssmmessages, ec2messages) if instance is in private subnet with no internet access
• TRAP: SSM Run Command is NOT a scheduler — use Maintenance Windows or EventBridge for scheduled execution

aws-trusted-advisor.text:
• Automated tool providing real-time best-practice recommendations across 5 pillars:
  1. Cost Optimization: idle/underutilized resources (EC2, EBS, RDS), RI optimization, S3 savings
  2. Performance: high-utilization EC2 instances, CloudFront optimizations, EBS throughput
  3. Security: S3 bucket permissions, security group rules, MFA on root, exposed access keys, CloudTrail enabled
  4. Fault Tolerance: EBS snapshots, RDS Multi-AZ, Auto Scaling, Route 53 health checks
  5. Service Limits (Service Quotas): usage vs limits for 50+ services

• Basic / Developer Support: 7 core security checks + all service limit checks
  - Core checks: S3 bucket permissions, security groups unrestricted access, MFA on root, IAM use, EBS public snapshots, RDS public snapshots, service limits
• Business / Enterprise Support: ALL checks enabled + Trusted Advisor API access
• Trusted Advisor API: programmatically retrieve check results; build automation (e.g., auto-remediate flagged issues)
• Notification: weekly email digest of check results
• Organizations: view Trusted Advisor data for all accounts from management account (Business/Enterprise)

• Scenario: quickly identify underutilized EC2 instances across the account → Trusted Advisor Cost Optimization
• Scenario: check if any S3 buckets are publicly accessible → Trusted Advisor Security check (or Config rule)
• Scenario: approaching service limit for EC2 instances → Trusted Advisor Service Limits check → request limit increase
• Scenario: want to automate response to Trusted Advisor findings → Trusted Advisor API + EventBridge + Lambda (requires Business/Enterprise support)

• TRAP: most Trusted Advisor checks require Business or Enterprise support plan — don't assume all checks are available
• TRAP: Trusted Advisor is recommendations only — it does NOT make changes; you must act on recommendations yourself (or automate via API)
• TRAP: Trusted Advisor ≠ AWS Config — Config tracks resource configuration history and compliance; Trusted Advisor gives best-practice recommendations
• TRAP: Trusted Advisor service limit checks show you current usage vs limits — use Service Quotas console to actually request increases

aws-well-architected-tool.text:
• Free tool to review your workloads against AWS Well-Architected Framework best practices
• Generates a report identifying high/medium risks with prioritized improvement recommendations
• 6 Pillars of the Well-Architected Framework:
  1. Operational Excellence: run and monitor systems, continually improve processes — IaC, frequent small changes, anticipate failure
  2. Security: protect data, systems, assets — IAM, encryption, detective controls, incident response
  3. Reliability: recover from failures, scale to meet demand — multi-AZ, Auto Scaling, backup/recovery
  4. Performance Efficiency: use resources efficiently — right instance types, serverless, global reach, monitoring
  5. Cost Optimization: avoid waste, understand spending — rightsizing, Reserved/Spot Instances, managed services
  6. Sustainability: minimize environmental impact — efficient code, right-sizing, managed services

• Workload: define the application or system being reviewed
• Questionnaire: answer questions per pillar; each question maps to design principles and best practices
• Risk findings: HRI (High Risk Items) and MRI (Medium Risk Items) per pillar
• Improvement Plan: prioritized list of actions with links to documentation
• Milestones: snapshot workload state over time — track improvement
• Custom Lenses: define your own questions and best practices for industry-specific requirements
• AWS Lenses: pre-built lenses for specific domains (Serverless, SaaS, IoT, Machine Learning, SAP, FTR)

• Scenario: architect wants to evaluate a new workload against AWS best practices → Well-Architected Tool
• Scenario: which pillar covers disaster recovery and fault tolerance → Reliability pillar
• Scenario: which pillar covers least-privilege IAM and encryption → Security pillar
• Scenario: which pillar covers using Spot Instances and rightsizing → Cost Optimization pillar
• Exam tip: the 6 pillar names and their domains appear regularly in SAA-C03 scenario questions

• TRAP: Well-Architected Tool does NOT make changes — it only provides recommendations; implementation is manual
• TRAP: Sustainability is the 6th pillar (added in 2021) — some older resources still list only 5 pillars
• TRAP: Trusted Advisor ≠ Well-Architected Tool — Trusted Advisor gives automated account-level checks; WAT provides deeper architectural guidance through a questionnaire
• TRAP: Well-Architected Review should be done BEFORE building (design review) AND periodically after (improvement reviews)

amazon-kinesis-video-streams.text:
• Securely ingest, process, and store video streams from connected devices
• Fully managed — automatically provisions and scales infrastructure
• Stores video durably in S3, encrypted at rest and in transit
• Producers: IP cameras, smartphones, drones, RADAR, LIDAR, audio feeds
• Consumers: Amazon Rekognition Video, SageMaker, custom ML apps, Kinesis Data Streams

• Playback: HLS (HTTP Live Streaming) and DASH for live and on-demand video playback
• WebRTC support: two-way real-time video/audio streaming (< 500ms latency)
• Producer SDK: libraries for embedded devices (C++), Android, Java
• Time-indexed data: each frame tagged with producer and server-side timestamps
• Retention: configure per-stream data retention (hours to years)
• Fragments: video is broken into time-bounded fragments stored as MKV

• Scenario: build a smart home camera system that feeds ML-based object detection → Kinesis Video Streams → Rekognition Video
• Scenario: industrial IoT video monitoring with real-time anomaly detection → KVS → custom SageMaker model
• Scenario: need two-way real-time video for a telehealth app → KVS WebRTC
• TRAP: KVS is NOT the same as Kinesis Data Streams — KVS is specifically for video/audio media; KDS is for generic data records

aws-application-discovery-service.text:
• Discover on-premises servers, dependencies, and performance data to plan cloud migrations
• Agentless mode (VMware only): deploy Agentless Collector OVA appliance — collects VM inventory, CPU, memory, disk usage without installing agents
• Agent-based mode: install Discovery Agent on each server (Windows/Linux) — captures detailed process, network connection, and performance data
• All data stored in AWS Migration Hub

• Migration Hub: central place to track migration status across all AWS migration tools
• Integrates with AWS Migration Evaluator to build a business case (TCO analysis)
• Exports collected data to S3 for analysis with Athena or QuickSight
• Dependency mapping: identifies which servers communicate with each other (critical for grouping migrations)
• Tags discovered servers and creates server groups for wave planning

• Scenario: large enterprise wants to migrate 500 on-prem servers but doesn't know what they have → use ADS first to discover and map the environment
• Scenario: need to identify server dependencies before migration to avoid breaking apps → ADS agent-based mode for network dependency mapping
• Scenario: VMware environment, want discovery without installing agents on every VM → ADS agentless via OVA appliance
• TRAP: ADS is a DISCOVERY tool, not a migration tool — use it BEFORE Application Migration Service (MGN)

aws-application-migration-service.text:
• Lift-and-shift (rehost) migration — moves servers to AWS with minimal changes
• Successor to CloudEndure Migration and AWS Server Migration Service (SMS)
• Continuously replicates source servers to AWS using block-level replication (like rsync at block level)
• Supports: physical servers, VMware, Hyper-V, Azure, GCP, other clouds → EC2
• Supports Windows and Linux

• Install AWS Replication Agent on source server
• Agent continuously replicates disk data to a Replication Server in your target AWS account (staging area)
• During replication: source remains fully operational — zero production impact
• Test cutover: launch test instances in AWS to validate before actual cutover
• Cutover: finalize replication, launch production instances, decommission source
• Cutover window: typically minutes — only the delta changes since last sync need to transfer

• Scenario: migrate on-prem servers to AWS with minimal downtime and no application changes → MGN (lift-and-shift)
• Scenario: need to test the migrated workload before committing to cutover → MGN test cutover feature
• Scenario: migrating from another cloud (Azure/GCP) to AWS → MGN supports cross-cloud
• TRAP: MGN = lift-and-shift rehost; for re-platform (e.g., Oracle → Aurora) use Database Migration Service (DMS)
• TRAP: MGN does NOT modernize or refactor the application — it moves it as-is

aws-datasync.text:
• Automated, scheduled data transfer service for moving large amounts of data to/from AWS
• Online transfer — uses your network (internet or Direct Connect/VPN)
• Sources: on-premises NFS, SMB, HDFS, S3-compatible; also AWS-to-AWS transfers
• Destinations: Amazon S3 (all storage classes), EFS, FSx for Windows, FSx for Lustre, FSx for OpenZFS, FSx for NetApp ONTAP
• Uses DataSync Agent (VM appliance on-prem) for on-premises to AWS transfers

• Encryption in transit using TLS; end-to-end data integrity verification (checksums)
• Transfer speed: up to 10 Gbps per agent; deploy multiple agents for higher throughput
• Scheduling: one-time or recurring (hourly, daily, weekly) transfers
• Preserves file metadata: permissions, timestamps, ownership
• Bandwidth throttling: set limits to avoid saturating your network link
• Task reports: per-file transfer results with success/failure status
• AWS PrivateLink support: transfer without traversing the public internet

• Scenario: migrate NFS shares from on-prem data center to EFS → DataSync agent + DataSync task
• Scenario: ongoing nightly sync of on-prem data to S3 for analytics → DataSync scheduled task
• Scenario: need to replicate data between two S3 buckets in different regions → DataSync (or S3 Cross-Region Replication)
• DataSync vs Storage Gateway: DataSync = bulk migration or periodic sync; Storage Gateway = continuous hybrid access (NFS/SMB mount that writes to S3)
• DataSync vs Snowball: DataSync = online (network-based); Snowball = offline (physical device), used when network is too slow or data too large
• DataSync vs S3 CRR: DataSync can move data between more storage types and includes on-prem; CRR is only S3-to-S3
• TRAP: DataSync agent is required for on-prem transfers but NOT needed for AWS-to-AWS transfers

aws-transfer-family.text:
• Fully managed file transfer into/out of S3 and EFS using legacy protocols
• Supported protocols: SFTP (SSH File Transfer Protocol), FTPS (FTP over SSL), FTP, AS2 (Applicability Statement 2)
• No need to change existing file transfer workflows, scripts, or client software
• Managed server endpoints with custom hostnames via Route 53
• Multi-AZ deployment for high availability

• Storage targets: Amazon S3 or Amazon EFS
• Authentication options: service-managed (username/password or SSH key), custom Lambda authorizer, AWS Directory Service (AD), Okta, Azure AD via LDAP
• Logging: CloudWatch Logs for transfer activity
• AS2: used for B2B data exchanges (EDI transactions) — common in healthcare, financial services
• Managed workflows: trigger Lambda or other services post-upload for processing automation
• Elastic IPs: assign static IPs to endpoints for firewall whitelisting by trading partners

• Scenario: company uses SFTP today and wants to move files to S3 without changing their partners' upload scripts → AWS Transfer Family (SFTP)
• Scenario: healthcare company exchanges EDI data with partners using AS2 protocol → Transfer Family with AS2
• Scenario: need to trigger a Lambda to process a file immediately after an SFTP upload → Transfer Family managed workflows
• TRAP: Transfer Family is NOT a migration tool — it's for ongoing file exchange workflows
• TRAP: DataSync transfers data efficiently in bulk; Transfer Family exposes protocol endpoints for interactive file upload/download
• Key distinction: Transfer Family = protocol gateway to S3/EFS; DataSync = high-speed bulk data transfer agent

amazon-api-gateway.text:
• Fully managed service to create, publish, secure, and monitor REST, HTTP, and WebSocket APIs
• Acts as the front door for backend services: Lambda, HTTP endpoints, AWS services, VPC resources
• Handles: throttling, caching, auth, CORS, versioning, API keys, request/response transformation
• Three API types: REST API (full features), HTTP API (simpler, cheaper, lower latency), WebSocket API (real-time bidirectional)

• Edge-Optimized: routed through CloudFront edge locations — best for global clients
• Regional: deployed in a specific region — use with CloudFront manually for custom caching
• Private: accessible only within a VPC via Interface VPC Endpoint
• Integrations: Lambda Proxy, Lambda Custom, HTTP Proxy, AWS Service (direct SQS, SNS, Step Functions, etc.), VPC Link (private ALB/NLB)
• VPC Link: connect API Gateway to private resources inside a VPC (e.g., private ALB)
• Stage Variables: like environment variables for API stages (dev, staging, prod) — use to point to different Lambda aliases or backends

• Auth options: IAM (SigV4), Lambda Authorizer (custom JWT/OAuth), Cognito User Pools
• Resource Policies: control which IPs, VPCs, or accounts can call the API
• Throttling: 10,000 RPS default account limit, 5,000 burst — can be increased via quota request
• Usage Plans + API Keys: throttle and quota individual clients/apps
• Mutual TLS (mTLS): require client certificates for API calls
• WAF integration: attach AWS WAF Web ACL to protect against SQLi, XSS, IP blocking

• Caching: TTL 0–3,600s (default 300s); reduces backend calls; cache keyed on query strings/headers
• Cache invalidation: clients can bypass cache with Cache-Control: max-age=0 header (if authorized)
• Canary Deployments: route a % of traffic to a new stage version for gradual rollouts
• HTTP API vs REST API: HTTP API is ~70% cheaper, lower latency, supports JWT auth natively, but lacks caching, usage plans, request validation, and some integrations

• Scenario: expose a Lambda function as a public HTTPS endpoint → API Gateway + Lambda
• Scenario: need private API accessible only from within VPC → Private API Gateway + Interface VPC Endpoint
• Scenario: need to call a private ALB from API Gateway → VPC Link
• Scenario: throttle a specific customer's API usage → Usage Plans + API Keys
• Scenario: real-time chat application → WebSocket API
• TRAP: REST API ≠ HTTP API — REST API has more features (caching, request validation, WAF, usage plans); HTTP API is for simple Lambda/HTTP proxy use cases
• TRAP: API Gateway has a 29-second integration timeout — Lambda functions must complete within 29s for synchronous calls

amazon-cloudfront.text:
• CDN — caches and delivers content from 400+ edge locations (Points of Presence) globally
• Reduces latency for static AND dynamic content
• DDoS protection via AWS Shield Standard (always on, no extra cost)
• Origins: S3 bucket, ALB, EC2, API Gateway, any custom HTTP server
• Distributions: Web (HTTP/S) or RTMP (deprecated)

• Origin Access Control (OAC): restricts S3 bucket to only accept requests from CloudFront — replaces legacy OAI
• Origin Access Identity (OAI): legacy method, still on exam — creates a special CloudFront identity used in S3 bucket policy
• Field-Level Encryption: encrypt sensitive POST data (e.g., credit cards) at the edge using public keys
• HTTPS: enforce HTTPS between viewer↔CloudFront and CloudFront↔origin independently
• Signed URLs: grant time-limited access to individual files (use for paid/premium content)
• Signed Cookies: grant time-limited access to multiple files without changing URLs
• Geo Restriction: allowlist (whitelist) or blocklist countries based on IP geolocation

• Cache Behaviors: match requests by path pattern (/api/*, /images/*) and route to different origins or apply different cache policies
• Cache based on: query strings, headers, cookies — configure what to forward to origin
• TTL: set via Cache-Control or Expires headers from origin; or override with CloudFront cache policies
• Cache Invalidation: manually invalidate before TTL expires — $0.005/path after first 1,000/month
• Cache Hit Ratio: maximize by caching on fewer dimensions (avoid forwarding all headers/cookies)

• CloudFront Functions: lightweight JS at edge PoP — sub-millisecond, millions of requests/sec
  - Use for: URL rewrites/redirects, header manipulation, simple auth token validation
  - Cannot access network or file system; max 10ms execution time; 2MB code size
• Lambda@Edge: Node.js or Python functions at Regional Edge Caches — more powerful
  - Use for: A/B testing, server-side rendering, complex auth, request body inspection
  - Can make network calls; max 5s (viewer) or 30s (origin) execution
  - More expensive and higher latency than CloudFront Functions

• Scenario: serve S3 static website globally with low latency, restrict direct S3 access → CloudFront + OAC
• Scenario: serve different content to different countries → Geo Restriction (block) or Lambda@Edge (customize per country)
• Scenario: A/B test two versions of a webpage → Lambda@Edge to modify response based on cookie
• Scenario: protect premium video content from being shared → CloudFront Signed URLs
• TRAP: CloudFront ≠ Global Accelerator — CloudFront CACHES content at edge; GA routes TCP/UDP traffic over AWS backbone without caching
• TRAP: CloudFront cannot write to S3 origins; it only reads/caches
• TRAP: EC2 origins must have a public IP or be behind an ALB — CloudFront cannot reach private EC2 directly
• TRAP: CloudFront certificates must be in us-east-1 (N. Virginia) — ACM certificates for CloudFront are always provisioned in us-east-1

amazon-route-53.text:
• Highly available, scalable cloud DNS service — 100% SLA
• Functions: domain registration, DNS routing, health checking
• Hosted Zones: Public (internet-facing DNS records) or Private (DNS within one or more VPCs)
• Supports all standard DNS record types: A, AAAA, CNAME, MX, NS, SOA, TXT, CAA, PTR, SRV
• Alias records: Route 53-specific — point to AWS resources (ELB, CloudFront, S3, API Gateway) with no charge per query; can be at zone apex (root domain)

• Simple: single resource; no health checks; multiple values → client picks one randomly
• Weighted: distribute traffic by % weights across resources; use for blue/green or gradual rollouts
• Latency-based: route to the region with lowest network latency for the user
• Failover: primary + secondary (active-passive); mandatory health check on primary
• Geolocation: route based on user's country, continent, or US state; requires a default record
• Geoproximity: route based on location with bias adjustments to shift traffic toward/away from regions; requires Traffic Flow
• Multi-value Answer: returns up to 8 healthy records — NOT a replacement for a load balancer
• IP-based: route based on client IP CIDR ranges — for known network segments

• Monitor public endpoints (HTTP, HTTPS, TCP) — not private VPC resources directly
• Health check types: Endpoint, Calculated (combine multiple checks), CloudWatch Alarm
• Thresholds: healthy/unhealthy after 3 consecutive checks (default); interval 10s or 30s
• Private resources: create a CloudWatch metric + alarm → Route 53 health check on that alarm
• Used with: Failover routing, Weighted (to skip unhealthy resources), Multi-value
• String matching: check response body for a specific string (up to first 5,120 bytes)

• Scenario: route users to the closest region automatically → Latency-based routing
• Scenario: 90% traffic to v1, 10% to v2 for testing → Weighted routing
• Scenario: failover to S3 static site if primary EC2 goes down → Failover routing + health check
• Scenario: serve different content to users in different countries → Geolocation routing
• Scenario: gradual traffic migration from old to new region → Weighted routing (shift weights incrementally)
• TRAP: CNAME cannot be used at the zone apex (root domain like example.com) — use Alias record instead
• TRAP: Alias records are FREE to query; CNAME records are charged
• TRAP: Geolocation ≠ Latency-based — Geolocation routes by WHERE the user is; Latency routes by what gives the FASTEST response
• TRAP: Multi-value is NOT a load balancer — it's just DNS with health checks; use ELB for actual load balancing

elastic-load-balancing.text:
• Distributes incoming application traffic across multiple targets in multiple AZs
• Fully managed — AWS handles scaling, patching, HA
• Types: Application Load Balancer (ALB) — Layer 7, Network Load Balancer (NLB) — Layer 4, Gateway Load Balancer (GWLB) — Layer 3
• Classic Load Balancer (CLB): legacy, Layer 4+7 — avoid on new architectures
• Integrates with: EC2 Auto Scaling, ECS, Lambda, ACM (SSL/TLS), WAF (ALB only), CloudWatch, Route 53

• Layer 7 (HTTP/HTTPS/gRPC) — content-based routing
• Routing rules: by path (/api vs /images), hostname (api.example.com), HTTP headers, query strings, source IP
• Target types: EC2 instances, ECS tasks, Lambda functions, IP addresses
• Dynamic port mapping: ALB can route to multiple ECS containers on the same EC2 with different ports
• Sticky Sessions: Application (app-managed cookie) or Duration (LB-generated cookie)
• X-Forwarded-For header: preserves original client IP (source IP seen by ALB is the client)
• Fixed response actions: return static responses without forwarding to a target
• Redirect actions: HTTP → HTTPS redirect at the load balancer level
• Authentication: native OIDC and Cognito User Pool integration for user authentication

• Layer 4 (TCP/UDP/TLS) — ultra-high performance, millions of requests per second
• Ultra-low latency — ~100ms vs ~400ms for ALB
• One static IP per AZ (or Elastic IP) — useful for firewall whitelisting
• Preserves source IP of client — targets see actual client IP directly
• Health checks: TCP, HTTP, HTTPS
• Supports TLS termination at NLB (offloads TLS from targets)
• Use NLB for: gaming, IoT, financial trading, real-time video — anything needing extreme throughput
• PrivateLink: NLB is used as the backbone of AWS PrivateLink to expose services to other VPCs

• Layer 3 (IP packets) — routes traffic to virtual network appliances (firewalls, IDS/IPS, deep packet inspection)
• Uses GENEVE protocol on port 6081
• Transparent bump-in-the-wire: traffic goes in → appliance → GWLB returns it → destination
• Use case: inspect all traffic entering/leaving a VPC through 3rd-party security appliances
• Deployed in a dedicated inspection VPC; use Gateway Load Balancer Endpoints in spoke VPCs

• Cross-Zone Load Balancing: distribute traffic evenly across ALL registered targets in ALL AZs
  - ALB: enabled by default, no charge
  - NLB/GWLB: disabled by default, charged for inter-AZ data transfer when enabled
• Connection Draining (Deregistration Delay): time to allow in-flight requests to complete when deregistering a target
  - Default: 300s; range 1–3600s; set to 0 to disable immediately
• Sticky Sessions: route the same client to the same target using a cookie
  - Duration-based: LB generates cookie
  - Application-based: app generates its own cookie (ALB only)

• Scenario: route /api/* to microservice A and /web/* to microservice B → ALB path-based routing
• Scenario: need a static IP for the load balancer for firewall rules → NLB (static IP per AZ)
• Scenario: inspect all VPC traffic with a 3rd-party firewall appliance → GWLB
• Scenario: authenticate users via Cognito before reaching the app → ALB with Cognito auth action
• Scenario: EC2 instances taking too long to drain on scale-in → reduce Deregistration Delay
• TRAP: ALB does NOT preserve client IP in source IP field — use X-Forwarded-For header
• TRAP: NLB DOES preserve client IP — security groups on targets must allow the actual client IP range
• TRAP: ALB cannot handle pure TCP or UDP traffic — use NLB
• TRAP: CLB is legacy — exam still mentions it but always prefer ALB or NLB for new architectures

aws-direct-connect.text:
• Dedicated private network connection from your data center / office to AWS
• Bypasses public internet → more consistent latency, higher throughput, lower jitter
• Speeds: 1 Gbps, 10 Gbps, 100 Gbps at AWS Direct Connect locations
• Sub-1 Gbps (50 Mbps – 500 Mbps): via hosted connections through APN partners
• Lead time: typically 1+ month to establish a new dedicated connection

• Private VIF: connect to resources inside a VPC (via Virtual Private Gateway or Transit Gateway)
• Public VIF: connect to AWS public services (S3, DynamoDB, CloudFront) without traversing internet
• Transit VIF: connect to a Transit Gateway — enables access to multiple VPCs across regions
• Hosted VIF: a VIF provisioned by a partner and shared with your account
• Direct Connect Gateway: single DX connection → access VPCs in MULTIPLE regions (global resource)

• Direct Connect is NOT encrypted by default — data travels over a private link but without encryption
• Encryption option 1: VPN over Direct Connect — IPSec VPN over a Public VIF
• Encryption option 2: MACsec — Layer 2 encryption on dedicated connections (10G/100G)
• Resiliency models:
  - Maximum resiliency: two independent connections at two separate DX locations
  - High resiliency: two connections at one DX location
  - Development/Test: single connection
• Backup strategy: use Site-to-Site VPN as a backup path when DX is down

• Scenario: company needs consistent low-latency connection to AWS for financial workloads → Direct Connect
• Scenario: need to access both VPC resources AND S3 over Direct Connect → Private VIF (for VPC) + Public VIF (for S3)
• Scenario: need to access VPCs in multiple regions from one DX connection → Direct Connect Gateway + Transit VIF
• Scenario: Direct Connect link goes down and need failover → Site-to-Site VPN as backup
• Scenario: need private connection to AWS AND encryption in transit → VPN over Direct Connect (IPSec on Public VIF)
• TRAP: Direct Connect takes weeks to provision — for immediate connectivity use VPN
• TRAP: Direct Connect alone is NOT encrypted — this is a very common exam trick
• TRAP: Direct Connect Gateway ≠ Transit Gateway — DX Gateway connects DX to multiple VPCs; TGW connects VPCs/VPNs to each other

aws-global-accelerator.text:
• Routes user traffic over the AWS global private network backbone instead of the public internet
• Provides two static Anycast IP addresses as global entry points
• Traffic enters AWS network at the nearest Edge Location and stays on AWS backbone to endpoint
• Endpoints: ALB, NLB, EC2 instances, Elastic IPs — in any AWS region
• ~60% improvement in first byte latency compared to internet routing (varies)

• Health checks: continuously monitors endpoints; automatically routes around unhealthy ones
• Traffic Dials: control % of traffic sent to each endpoint group (region) — useful for blue/green or gradual rollouts
• Weighted Endpoints: control distribution within an endpoint group
• Client Affinity: route same client to same endpoint (like sticky sessions but at network level)
• Instant regional failover: ~30-second failover — much faster than DNS-based failover (which depends on TTL)
• Static IPs: two fixed IPs — simplifies firewall rules and client whitelisting

• Scenario: gaming app needs low-latency global TCP connections → Global Accelerator (not CloudFront — no caching needed)
• Scenario: multi-region active-active app needs instant failover without DNS TTL delays → Global Accelerator
• Scenario: need a fixed IP for a globally distributed application → Global Accelerator static IPs
• Scenario: IoT devices connect via TCP/UDP — need routing optimization → Global Accelerator
• TRAP: Global Accelerator ≠ CloudFront — GA does NOT cache content; it optimizes routing for ANY TCP/UDP traffic; CloudFront caches HTTP/S content
• TRAP: Global Accelerator is NOT a CDN — content is NOT cached at edge locations
• TRAP: For HTTP/S caching and static content delivery → CloudFront; for TCP/UDP performance and non-HTTP → Global Accelerator

aws-transit-gateway.text:
• Regional network hub — connect VPCs, VPN connections, and Direct Connect in a hub-and-spoke model
• Eliminates complex full-mesh VPC peering (N*(N-1)/2 peering connections → 1 TGW)
• Transitive routing: traffic flows between any attached network through TGW (VPC peering is NOT transitive)
• Regional resource — for cross-region, use TGW peering
• Supports up to 5,000 VPC attachments per TGW

• Attachment types: VPC, Site-to-Site VPN, Direct Connect Gateway, TGW Peering (cross-region), SD-WAN appliances
• TGW Route Tables: control routing between attachments — create isolated route tables for segmentation
• Example: shared services VPC (DNS, AD) can route to all VPCs; dev VPCs cannot route to prod VPCs
• VPC attachments: one ENI per AZ in the VPC's subnet
• Propagation: automatically propagate VPC CIDR routes into TGW route tables

• ECMP (Equal Cost Multipath): load-balance across multiple Site-to-Site VPN tunnels for higher bandwidth
  - Each VPN tunnel = 1.25 Gbps; use ECMP with multiple tunnels to aggregate bandwidth
• Multicast: route multicast traffic across connected VPCs (one-to-many)
• Network Manager: centrally manage and monitor global network built on TGW
• Appliance Mode: for stateful appliances (firewalls), ensure traffic goes to the same AZ appliance for both flows of a connection
• RAM (Resource Access Manager): share TGW across accounts in AWS Organizations

• Scenario: 20 VPCs need to communicate — avoid managing 190 peering connections → Transit Gateway
• Scenario: connect on-prem data center to multiple VPCs → Direct Connect + Transit VIF + TGW
• Scenario: need > 1.25 Gbps VPN bandwidth → TGW + multiple VPN connections with ECMP
• Scenario: isolate dev and prod VPCs on the same TGW → separate TGW route tables per environment
• Scenario: share TGW with other AWS accounts in the org → RAM
• TRAP: VPC Peering is NOT transitive — A↔B and B↔C does NOT mean A↔C; use TGW for transitive routing
• TRAP: TGW costs money per attachment and per GB processed — VPC Peering has no per-hour cost
• TRAP: TGW is regional — for multi-region you need TGW Peering (not cross-region automatically)

amazon-cognito.text:
• User identity and access management for web and mobile applications
• Two components: User Pools (authentication) and Identity Pools (AWS authorization)
• User Pools: user directory — handles sign-up, sign-in, MFA, social federation
• Identity Pools: exchange tokens for temporary AWS credentials via STS AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity

• Returns JWT tokens: ID Token (user claims), Access Token (API access), Refresh Token
• Built-in UI: hosted UI for sign-in/sign-up pages (customizable)
• Social federation: Google, Facebook, Apple, Amazon (OIDC/OAuth 2.0)
• Enterprise federation: SAML 2.0 (Active Directory, Okta, Azure AD)
• Advanced security: adaptive authentication, compromised credential checks
• Lambda triggers: pre-signup, post-confirmation, pre-token generation, custom message, user migration
• App clients: create multiple app clients per user pool with different auth flows and token expiry

• Inputs: Cognito User Pool token, Google, Facebook, SAML, OpenID Connect, or unauthenticated (guest)
• Output: temporary IAM credentials (Access Key, Secret Key, Session Token) via STS
• Two IAM roles: one for authenticated users, one for unauthenticated (guest) users
• Policy variables: use cognito-identity.amazonaws.com:sub in IAM policies to give users access to ONLY their own S3 prefix or DynamoDB items
• Row-level security pattern: each user gets IAM credentials scoped to their own data

• Scenario: mobile app needs user login with Google/Facebook → Cognito User Pool with social federation
• Scenario: after login, user needs to access their own S3 folder → Identity Pool → IAM role with cognito sub variable
• Scenario: enterprise SSO via Active Directory into a web app → User Pool with SAML federation
• Scenario: app needs to call AWS APIs directly from client (mobile) → Identity Pool temporary credentials
• TRAP: User Pools = authentication only (who are you); Identity Pools = AWS authorization (what can you do)
• TRAP: User Pools do NOT give AWS credentials — only JWTs; you need Identity Pools for AWS access
• TRAP: Cognito is for app users (external); IAM is for AWS users (internal/developers)

amazon-detective.text:
• Security investigation service — analyzes root cause of security findings
• Automatically collects and processes data from: GuardDuty, CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs, EKS audit logs
• Uses ML, statistical analysis, and graph theory to build behavior baselines and interactive graphs
• Fully managed — no agents, no configuration, enable with one click
• Visualizes relationships between IPs, users, resources across time

• Scenario: GuardDuty flagged suspicious EC2 behavior — need to investigate what happened → Amazon Detective
• Scenario: need to understand the full scope of a security incident across multiple services → Detective
• TRAP: Detective ≠ GuardDuty — GuardDuty DETECTS threats; Detective INVESTIGATES them
• TRAP: Detective ≠ Security Hub — Security Hub AGGREGATES findings; Detective ANALYZES them in depth
• Think of it as: GuardDuty = alarm; Security Hub = dashboard; Detective = forensics

amazon-guardduty.text:
• Intelligent threat detection using ML, anomaly detection, and integrated threat intelligence
• Enable with one click — no agents, no software, no infrastructure
• Analyzes: CloudTrail Management Events, CloudTrail S3 Data Events, VPC Flow Logs, DNS Logs, EKS Audit Logs, RDS Login Events, Lambda Network Activity
• Findings appear in GuardDuty console + EventBridge for automated response

• Finding categories: Backdoor, Behavior, CryptoCurrency, PenTest, Persistence, Policy, Recon, ResourceConsumption, Stealth, Trojan, UnauthorizedAccess
• Finding severity: Low, Medium, High
• Multi-account: designate a GuardDuty administrator account via AWS Organizations
• Trusted IP lists: whitelist known IPs to reduce false positives
• Threat intel feeds: add custom threat lists (malicious IPs/domains)
• GuardDuty Malware Protection: scan EBS volumes on potentially compromised EC2/containers
• Automated response: EventBridge rule → Lambda → isolate EC2 (modify SG), block IP in WAF/NACL

• Scenario: detect cryptocurrency mining on EC2 instances → GuardDuty (CryptoCurrency finding type)
• Scenario: EC2 instance communicating with known malicious IPs → GuardDuty + EventBridge + Lambda to isolate
• Scenario: detect unusual IAM API calls (e.g., root account usage) → GuardDuty CloudTrail findings
• Scenario: centralize threat detection across 50 AWS accounts → GuardDuty with Organizations delegated admin
• TRAP: GuardDuty does NOT block traffic — it only detects; use Lambda + Security Groups/NACLs/WAF for blocking
• TRAP: disabling GuardDuty deletes all existing findings — suppression rules are safer for false positives
• TRAP: GuardDuty is regional — must enable in each region separately (or use Organizations for bulk enablement)

amazon-inspector.text:
• Automated vulnerability assessment — continuously scans for software vulnerabilities and unintended network exposure
• Targets: EC2 instances, container images in ECR, Lambda functions
• Powered by the Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures (CVE) database
• Findings routed to: Inspector console, Security Hub, EventBridge

• EC2 scanning: requires SSM Agent; scans OS packages for CVEs + checks network reachability (open ports accessible from internet)
• ECR scanning: scans container images on push (basic) or continuously (enhanced with Inspector)
• Lambda scanning: scans Lambda function code and package dependencies for CVEs
• Risk score: Inspector prioritizes findings with a contextual risk score (CVSS base + environment factors)
• Multi-account: delegate Inspector administrator via Organizations

• Scenario: automatically scan EC2 instances for known OS vulnerabilities → Inspector
• Scenario: scan container images for CVEs before deployment → Inspector ECR scanning
• Scenario: find EC2 instances with port 22 open to the internet → Inspector network reachability
• TRAP: Inspector ≠ GuardDuty — Inspector = vulnerability scanning (what COULD be exploited); GuardDuty = threat detection (what IS happening)
• TRAP: Inspector ≠ Macie — Macie = sensitive data in S3; Inspector = software vulnerabilities
• TRAP: Inspector requires SSM Agent on EC2 for OS-level scanning

amazon-macie.text:
• ML-powered security service to discover, classify, and protect sensitive data in Amazon S3
• Automatically identifies: PII (names, SSNs, credit cards, passports), financial data, credentials, API keys, private keys
• Generates findings → Security Hub, EventBridge for automated response
• Multi-account: delegate Macie administrator via Organizations
• Pay per GB of S3 data evaluated

• Managed data identifiers: built-in patterns for 100+ sensitive data types (PII, PHI, financial)
• Custom data identifiers: define your own regex patterns for proprietary data types
• Sensitive data discovery jobs: one-time or scheduled scans of S3 buckets
• Bucket inventory: continuously evaluates S3 bucket security posture (public access, encryption, sharing)
• Suppression rules: filter out known-safe findings to reduce noise

• Scenario: detect if S3 buckets contain credit card numbers or SSNs → Macie
• Scenario: alert when PII is uploaded to an S3 bucket → Macie + EventBridge + SNS
• Scenario: audit S3 buckets for public access and unencrypted data → Macie bucket inventory
• TRAP: Macie is S3-only — it does not scan EBS, RDS, DynamoDB, or EFS
• TRAP: Macie ≠ Inspector — Inspector = CVE/vulnerability scanning; Macie = sensitive data classification
• TRAP: Macie ≠ GuardDuty — GuardDuty = threat detection; Macie = data privacy

amazon-verified-permissions.text:
• Fine-grained authorization service for applications using Cedar policy language
• Externalize and centralize authorization logic outside application code
• Works with Cognito User Pools for authentication context
• Define policies: principals (users/roles), actions, resources, and conditions
• IsAuthorized API: application asks 'can user X do action Y on resource Z?' → Allow/Deny response

• Scenario: SaaS app needs per-tenant fine-grained access control (RBAC/ABAC) → Verified Permissions
• Scenario: externalize authorization so policy changes don't require code deployments → Verified Permissions
• TRAP: Verified Permissions = application-level authz for your app's users; IAM = AWS resource access control for AWS API calls
• TRAP: Not a replacement for IAM — they operate at different layers (your app vs AWS services)

aws-artifact.text:
• Self-service portal for AWS compliance reports and security documentation — free service
• Reports: SOC 1, SOC 2, SOC 3, PCI DSS, ISO 27001, ISO 27017, ISO 27018, FedRAMP, HIPAA, GDPR Data Processing Addendum, C5, ENS
• Agreements: review and accept BAAs (HIPAA), DPAs (GDPR), NDAs directly in console
• Organization agreements: accept agreements on behalf of all accounts in AWS Organizations

• Scenario: auditor needs AWS SOC 2 report → download from AWS Artifact
• Scenario: company needs to sign a HIPAA BAA with AWS → AWS Artifact Agreements
• TRAP: Artifact = AWS's own compliance docs/reports; it does NOT monitor your environment
• TRAP: Artifact ≠ Audit Manager — Audit Manager collects evidence about YOUR AWS usage; Artifact provides AWS's compliance certifications

aws-audit-manager.text:
• Continuously audit your AWS usage to simplify compliance and risk assessment
• Automates evidence collection from AWS services: CloudTrail, Config, Security Hub, IAM, and more
• Pre-built frameworks: PCI DSS, GDPR, HIPAA, SOC 2, CIS AWS Foundations, NIST, FedRAMP
• Custom frameworks: define your own controls and evidence requirements
• Generates audit-ready assessment reports for auditors

• Scenario: company preparing for SOC 2 audit needs to automate evidence collection → Audit Manager
• Scenario: continuously monitor AWS environment for GDPR compliance → Audit Manager with GDPR framework
• TRAP: Audit Manager ≠ AWS Artifact — Artifact = get AWS's compliance reports; Audit Manager = generate YOUR compliance evidence
• TRAP: Audit Manager does not remediate — it only collects evidence and reports findings

aws-certificate-manager.text:
• Provision, manage, and deploy SSL/TLS certificates for AWS services
• Public certificates: FREE for use with supported AWS services
• Private certificates: ACM Private CA — for internal PKI (charged per CA per month + per certificate)
• Auto-renewal: ACM automatically renews managed certificates before expiry (no manual intervention)
• Cannot export private keys of ACM-managed public certificates

• Supported services: ALB, NLB, CloudFront, API Gateway, Elastic Beanstalk, AppSync, CloudFormation
• NOT supported directly: EC2 instances (use ACM Private CA or self-managed certs)
• Validation methods: DNS validation (recommended — add CNAME to hosted zone, auto-renewed) or Email validation
• Importing external certificates: import and manage 3rd-party certs in ACM (no auto-renewal)
• Regional service: certificates are regional EXCEPT CloudFront which requires us-east-1 (N. Virginia)
• Wildcard certs: *.example.com — covers all subdomains

• Scenario: HTTPS for an ALB without managing certificate renewals → ACM public certificate on ALB
• Scenario: CloudFront distribution needs SSL → provision ACM cert in us-east-1
• Scenario: internal microservices need mutual TLS with private certificates → ACM Private CA
• TRAP: ACM certs for CloudFront MUST be in us-east-1 — this is a very common exam trick
• TRAP: ACM does NOT work directly on EC2 — must use ACM with ALB/NLB in front of EC2
• TRAP: importing a cert into ACM does NOT enable auto-renewal — only ACM-provisioned certs auto-renew

aws-directory-service.text:
• Managed Microsoft Active Directory or compatible directory service in the cloud
• Three options:
  1. AWS Managed Microsoft AD: full Windows Server AD in your VPC; supports MFA, trusts with on-prem AD, Group Policy
  2. AD Connector: proxy that redirects all AD requests to your on-premises AD; no AD data stored in AWS
  3. Simple AD: Samba 4-based standalone managed directory; subset of AD features; no trust relationships; cheapest option

• AWS Managed Microsoft AD:
  - Two domain controllers deployed across 2 AZs automatically
  - Trust relationships: establish forest trust with on-prem AD for SSO
  - Integrates with: EC2 domain join, RDS for SQL Server, WorkSpaces, QuickSight, Chime, Connect
  - MFA: integrates with RADIUS-based MFA
• AD Connector:
  - Requires existing on-prem AD — all auth proxied to on-prem
  - Users/passwords never stored in AWS
  - Cannot function if on-prem connection (VPN/DX) goes down
• Simple AD:
  - No on-prem dependency
  - Does NOT support MFA, trust relationships, or RDS SQL Server integration

• Scenario: company has on-prem AD and wants AWS resources to authenticate against it → AD Connector
• Scenario: need full AD in AWS with no on-prem dependency and support for trust relationships → AWS Managed Microsoft AD
• Scenario: small company needs basic LDAP directory in AWS cheaply → Simple AD
• Scenario: WorkSpaces need to authenticate with existing corporate AD → AD Connector or trust with Managed AD
• TRAP: AD Connector requires working VPN/Direct Connect — if connection drops, authentication fails
• TRAP: Simple AD does NOT support MFA or trust relationships — use Managed Microsoft AD for those
• TRAP: IAM Identity Center can use any of these as the identity source for AWS SSO

aws-identity-and-access-management.text:
• Global service — IAM entities are not region-specific
• Root account: full unrestricted access — secure immediately with MFA, never use for daily tasks
• Users: individual identities with long-term credentials (password, access keys)
• Groups: collection of users — attach policies to groups, not individual users
• Roles: temporary identity assumed by services, users, or external identities — no long-term credentials
• Policies: JSON documents defining permissions — attached to users, groups, or roles

• Identity-based policies: attached to users/groups/roles (inline or managed)
• Resource-based policies: attached to resources (S3 bucket policy, KMS key policy, Lambda resource policy) — specify Principal
• Permission Boundaries: set MAXIMUM permissions an IAM entity can have (does not grant permissions)
• Service Control Policies (SCPs): applied via Organizations — restrict what accounts/OUs can do
• Session Policies: passed when assuming a role to further restrict permissions for that session
• Policy evaluation order: explicit DENY > SCP > resource-based policy > identity-based policy > permission boundary
• Explicit Deny ALWAYS wins — no matter how many allows exist

• STS AssumeRole: get temporary credentials (15min–12h) to assume a role in same or different account
• Cross-account pattern: Account A user → AssumeRole in Account B → temporary creds → access B's resources
• EC2 Instance Profile: attach IAM role to EC2 — never put access keys on EC2
• Service roles: allow AWS services to call other AWS services on your behalf (Lambda, ECS task role, etc.)
• Role chaining: assuming a role from another role — max session duration limited to 1 hour
• External ID: used with 3rd-party roles to prevent confused deputy problem

• IAM Credentials Report: CSV of all users and their credential status (MFA, key rotation, last used)
• IAM Access Advisor: shows service permissions granted and last accessed — use for least privilege
• IAM Access Analyzer: identifies resources shared externally (S3, IAM roles, KMS, Lambda, SQS) — zone of trust
• Password Policy: enforce complexity, expiry, reuse prevention, allow user self-service rotation
• MFA: Virtual (Authenticator app), Hardware TOTP token, FIDO Security Key (U2F)
• Best practices: MFA on root + all users, rotate access keys, use roles not users for services, apply least privilege

• Scenario: EC2 needs to read from S3 → attach IAM role with S3 read policy to EC2 (never use access keys on EC2)
• Scenario: developer in Account A needs to access DynamoDB in Account B → cross-account role + AssumeRole
• Scenario: ensure a developer can NEVER delete S3 objects even if a policy grants it → Permission Boundary
• Scenario: check if a user has actually been using all their granted permissions → IAM Access Advisor
• TRAP: Permission Boundaries do NOT grant permissions — they only limit the maximum
• TRAP: SCPs apply to member accounts only — the management/master account is NOT restricted by SCPs
• TRAP: Resource-based policies allow cross-account access without AssumeRole (e.g., S3 bucket policy granting another account access)
• TRAP: Groups cannot assume roles — only users and services can assume roles

aws-iam-identity-center.text:
• Centralized SSO for all AWS accounts and cloud applications — one login for everything
• Successor to AWS Single Sign-On (SSO)
• Free service included with AWS Organizations
• Identity sources: built-in store, AWS Managed Microsoft AD, AD Connector, external SAML 2.0 IdP (Okta, Azure AD, PingFederate)

• Permission Sets: define the IAM access a user/group has in an AWS account — deployed as IAM roles
• Account assignments: assign users/groups from your identity source to AWS accounts with a permission set
• Multi-account access: users log in once → access portal shows all assigned accounts → switch without re-authenticating
• Application assignments: SSO to business apps (Salesforce, GitHub, Slack, Office 365) via SAML 2.0/OIDC
• ABAC (Attribute-based access control): use user attributes from IdP (department, title, cost center) to control access dynamically
• SCIM: automatic user provisioning/deprovisioning from external IdPs

• Scenario: company has 30 AWS accounts and wants developers to log in once and switch accounts easily → IAM Identity Center
• Scenario: use Okta as IdP for AWS access → IAM Identity Center with Okta as external identity source
• Scenario: new employee provisioned in Azure AD should automatically get AWS access → SCIM sync between Azure AD and Identity Center
• TRAP: Identity Center ≠ Cognito — Identity Center is for workforce/employee AWS access; Cognito is for app end-users
• TRAP: Identity Center replaces creating individual IAM users per account — centralizes it all in one place

aws-key-management-service.text:
• Managed encryption key service — create, manage, and control cryptographic keys
• Integrated with 100+ AWS services for seamless encryption
• Keys never leave KMS unencrypted — FIPS 140-2 Level 2 validated (Level 3 for CloudHSM)
• Key material: AWS-generated or customer-imported
• Audit: all KMS API calls logged in CloudTrail

• AWS Owned Keys: managed by AWS across multiple accounts; no visibility or control; free; used by default for many services
• AWS Managed Keys (aws/service): created by AWS for a specific service in your account; auto-rotated every year; free; cannot change key policy
• Customer Managed Keys (CMK): you create and control; $1/month per key + $0.03 per 10,000 API calls; full key policy control; optional auto-rotation (every year)
• Symmetric (AES-256): single key for encrypt and decrypt — default and most common; used for AWS service integrations
• Asymmetric (RSA, ECC, SM2): public/private key pair; public key downloadable; used for signing or encryption outside AWS
• Multi-Region Keys: same key material replicated to multiple regions; primary + replica keys; used for cross-region encrypt/decrypt without re-encryption

• Envelope Encryption: KMS generates a data key → data key encrypts the actual data → KMS key encrypts the data key
  - Why: KMS max payload is 4 KB; envelope encryption handles any data size
  - GenerateDataKey API: returns plaintext + encrypted data key
  - Decrypt API: KMS decrypts the encrypted data key → you use plaintext data key to decrypt data
• Key Policy: resource-based policy on every KMS key — MUST explicitly allow account root; without it, IAM policies alone cannot grant access
• Key Grants: programmatically delegate key usage to AWS services or other principals temporarily
• Key deletion: 7–30 day waiting period before deletion (can be cancelled)

• Scenario: encrypt a 1 GB file using KMS → use Envelope Encryption (GenerateDataKey)
• Scenario: encrypt EBS volume with your own key material and control rotation → Customer Managed CMK
• Scenario: audit who decrypted an S3 object → CloudTrail logs of KMS Decrypt API calls
• Scenario: cross-region DR — decrypt in another region without re-encrypting → KMS Multi-Region Keys
• Scenario: dedicated single-tenant HSM for compliance → CloudHSM (not KMS)
• TRAP: KMS is multi-tenant; CloudHSM is dedicated single-tenant hardware — for stricter compliance requirements use CloudHSM
• TRAP: deleting a CMK is irreversible after the waiting period — all data encrypted with it becomes unrecoverable
• TRAP: KMS key policies MUST grant root account access — unlike IAM where root has implicit access to everything

aws-network-firewall.text:
• Managed stateful network firewall + IDS/IPS for VPCs — deployed at VPC perimeter
• Inspects traffic at Layers 3-7: IP, port, protocol, domain names, patterns
• Deployed in a dedicated firewall subnet in each AZ
• Traffic flows: Internet Gateway → Firewall Endpoint → workload subnets (via route table manipulation)
• Centralize across accounts using AWS Firewall Manager

• Stateless rules: evaluate each packet independently (5-tuple: src IP, dst IP, src port, dst port, protocol) — like NACLs
• Stateful rules: track connection state; Suricata-compatible IDS/IPS rules for deep packet inspection
• Domain list rules: allow/block traffic to specific FQDNs (e.g., block *.malware.com) — DNS-based filtering
• Actions: Pass, Drop, Alert (log without blocking)
• Rule priority: stateless rules evaluated first, then stateful
• Managed threat intelligence: subscribe to AWS managed Suricata rule groups

• Scenario: block outbound traffic to specific malicious domains from VPC → Network Firewall domain list rules
• Scenario: intrusion detection/prevention for all traffic entering a VPC → Network Firewall stateful Suricata rules
• Scenario: centrally manage firewall rules across 20 AWS accounts → Firewall Manager + Network Firewall
• TRAP: Network Firewall ≠ WAF — Network Firewall is VPC-level (L3-L7 any traffic); WAF is HTTP-only for ALB/CloudFront/API GW
• TRAP: Security Groups and NACLs cannot do domain filtering or deep packet inspection — use Network Firewall for that
• TRAP: Network Firewall requires routing changes (route tables) to redirect traffic through firewall endpoints

aws-resource-access-manager.text:
• Share AWS resources across AWS accounts or within AWS Organizations — no resource duplication
• Shareable resources include: VPC Subnets, Transit Gateway, Route 53 Resolver Rules, License Manager configurations, Aurora DB clusters, ACM Private CA, Glue Data Catalog, CodeBuild projects, and more
• Cross-account sharing without VPC peering or complex IAM role setups

• Share VPC subnets with other accounts → multiple accounts deploy resources into the same VPC
• Owner account: creates and manages the VPC and subnets
• Participant accounts: launch EC2, RDS, ECS tasks into shared subnets — see only their own resources
• Benefits: centralized network management, shared NAT Gateway costs, overlapping CIDR avoidance
• Participants cannot modify shared subnets or VPC route tables

• Scenario: 5 AWS accounts need to share the same VPC without VPC peering → RAM VPC subnet sharing
• Scenario: share Transit Gateway with accounts in the organization → RAM TGW sharing
• Scenario: share Route 53 Resolver rules for DNS across accounts → RAM
• TRAP: RAM is for sharing AWS resources — not for sharing data or S3 buckets (use S3 bucket policies for that)
• TRAP: Participants in a shared subnet can see each other's resources by IP but not in the console

aws-secrets-manager.text:
• Store, manage, retrieve, and automatically rotate secrets (DB passwords, API keys, OAuth tokens)
• Encrypted with KMS (CMK or AWS managed key aws/secretsmanager)
• Charged: $0.40 per secret per month + $0.05 per 10,000 API calls
• Cross-account access: share secrets via resource-based policies
• Secret replication: replicate secrets to multiple regions for disaster recovery and multi-region apps

• Native rotation support for: Amazon RDS (all engines), Aurora, Redshift, DocumentDB
• How it works: Secrets Manager calls a Lambda function (built-in or custom) on schedule to rotate the secret
• Rotation stages: createSecret → setSecret → testSecret → finishSecret
• Rotation schedules: days or cron expression
• Applications always call GetSecretValue API — get the latest rotated credentials automatically
• Custom Lambda rotation: for non-native services (e.g., MongoDB, on-prem databases)

• Scenario: RDS password needs to rotate every 30 days automatically without downtime → Secrets Manager with native RDS rotation
• Scenario: Lambda needs to connect to RDS without hardcoding credentials → Secrets Manager GetSecretValue at runtime
• Scenario: store and access secrets in multiple regions for a multi-region app → Secrets Manager replication
• TRAP: SSM Parameter Store SecureString can store secrets but does NOT natively rotate them — Secrets Manager does
• TRAP: Secrets Manager is more expensive than SSM Parameter Store — use SSM for non-secret config; Secrets Manager for credentials requiring rotation
• Key exam rule: 'automatic rotation of database credentials' → always Secrets Manager

aws-security-hub.text:
• Single pane of glass for security findings across AWS accounts and services
• Aggregates findings from: GuardDuty, Inspector, Macie, IAM Access Analyzer, Firewall Manager, Config, AWS Partner solutions
• Evaluates your environment against security standards automatically
• Sends aggregated findings to EventBridge for automated remediation
• Multi-account: designate Security Hub administrator via Organizations

• CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark: checks for root account MFA, CloudTrail enabled, security groups, etc.
• AWS Foundational Security Best Practices (FSBP)
• PCI DSS: payment card industry controls
• Compliance score: % of passed controls per standard
• Automated findings: Config rules + Security Hub standards work together

• Scenario: centralize security findings from GuardDuty, Inspector, and Macie into one place → Security Hub
• Scenario: automatically remediate a Security Hub finding (e.g., non-compliant S3 bucket) → EventBridge rule → Lambda
• Scenario: check if your AWS environment meets CIS Benchmark → Security Hub CIS standard
• TRAP: Security Hub does NOT detect threats — it aggregates and normalizes findings from other services
• TRAP: Security Hub ≠ Detective — Security Hub shows WHAT findings exist; Detective investigates WHY they happened
• TRAP: must enable AWS Config for many Security Hub checks to work

aws-shield.text:
• DDoS (Distributed Denial of Service) protection service
• Shield Standard: free, automatically enabled for all AWS customers
  - Protects against: SYN/UDP floods, reflection attacks, Layer 3/4 volumetric attacks
  - No action required to enable
• Shield Advanced: paid — $3,000/month per organization (covers all accounts)
  - Protects: EC2, ELB, CloudFront, Route 53, AWS Global Accelerator
  - Includes: 24/7 DDoS Response Team (DRT) access, financial protection (credits for scaling costs during attack), advanced attack diagnostics

• Near real-time attack visibility and detailed analytics
• Integration with WAF: automatic WAF rule creation to block attack traffic during DDoS
• Proactive engagement: DRT proactively contacts you during large-scale attacks
• Protected resources: must explicitly add resources to Shield Advanced protection
• Attack history: 13 months of attack event history
• Cost protection: AWS credits you for EC2, data transfer, ELB costs that spike during an attack

• Scenario: protect a public-facing web application from DDoS at no extra cost → Shield Standard (always on)
• Scenario: need DDoS response team support and financial protection → Shield Advanced
• Scenario: protect a critical financial application from sophisticated Layer 7 DDoS → Shield Advanced + WAF
• TRAP: Shield Standard does NOT protect against Layer 7 (application) attacks — use WAF for Layer 7
• TRAP: Shield Advanced does NOT replace WAF — they complement each other (Shield = volumetric DDoS; WAF = application-layer filtering)
• TRAP: Shield Advanced has a 1-year subscription commitment

aws-waf.text:
• Web Application Firewall — filter, monitor, and block HTTP/HTTPS traffic at Layer 7
• Deploy on: ALB, API Gateway, CloudFront, AppSync, Cognito User Pool, App Runner, Verified Access
• Web ACL: ordered collection of rules — evaluated top to bottom by priority
• Actions: Allow, Block, Count (log without blocking), CAPTCHA, Challenge

• IP Set rules: allow/block specific IP addresses or CIDR ranges
• SQL injection match: detect SQLi patterns in request
• XSS match: detect cross-site scripting patterns
• Size constraint: block requests where body/header/URI exceeds a size limit
• Geo match: allow/block traffic from specific countries
• Rate-based rules: automatically block IPs that exceed X requests in 5 minutes — DDoS mitigation
• Regex pattern match: match custom patterns in any request component
• Managed Rule Groups: pre-built rules from AWS (OWASP Top 10, Known Bad Inputs, Admin protection) or AWS Marketplace partners
• Label matching: rules can add labels to requests; subsequent rules can match on those labels

• Centrally manage WAF rules, Shield Advanced protections, Security Groups, Network Firewall policies across accounts in Organizations
• Requires: AWS Organizations + Security Hub or Firewall Manager admin account
• Automatically applies policies to new accounts/resources as they are created
• Use case: enforce consistent WAF rules across 50 accounts automatically

• Scenario: block SQL injection and XSS attacks on a web application → WAF on ALB or CloudFront
• Scenario: automatically block IPs making > 2000 requests per 5 minutes → WAF rate-based rule
