• Scenario: block all traffic except from specific countries → WAF geo match rule
• Scenario: enforce WAF rules across all accounts in the organization automatically → Firewall Manager
• TRAP: WAF alone does NOT protect against volumetric DDoS — use Shield + WAF together
• TRAP: WAF on CloudFront is global; WAF on ALB/API Gateway is regional — separate Web ACLs needed
• TRAP: WAF cannot block by ASN (Autonomous System Number) — only by IP or country

amazon-api-gateway.text:
• Fully managed service to create, publish, secure, and monitor REST, HTTP, and WebSocket APIs
• Acts as the front door for backend services: Lambda, HTTP endpoints, AWS services, VPC resources
• Handles: throttling, caching, auth, CORS, versioning, API keys, request/response transformation
• Three API types: REST API (full features), HTTP API (simpler, cheaper, lower latency), WebSocket API (real-time bidirectional)

• Edge-Optimized (default): routed through CloudFront edge — best for globally distributed clients
• Regional: deployed in a specific region — attach your own CloudFront manually for caching control
• Private: accessible only within a VPC via Interface VPC Endpoint
• Integration types:
  - Lambda Proxy: raw request/response forwarded to Lambda (most common)
  - Lambda Custom: transform request/response using mapping templates (VTL)
  - HTTP Proxy: forward to any HTTP endpoint
  - AWS Service: directly integrate with SQS, SNS, Step Functions, DynamoDB, etc. (no Lambda needed)
  - VPC Link: reach private ALB/NLB or ECS services inside a VPC

• Auth options: IAM (SigV4 signing), Lambda Authorizer (custom token/request auth), Cognito User Pools
• Resource policies: restrict by IP, VPC endpoint, or AWS account
• Throttling defaults: 10,000 RPS account limit, 5,000 burst — configurable per stage and method
• Usage Plans + API Keys: rate limit and quota individual clients
• Caching: configurable TTL 0–3600s (default 300s), reduces backend load
• CORS: must be enabled on API Gateway for browser-based JavaScript calls
• WAF: attach Web ACL to REST API or HTTP API for Layer 7 protection

• Stages: named deployments (dev, staging, prod) — each has its own URL
• Stage Variables: like env vars per stage — use to point to different Lambda aliases or backends
• Canary deployments: send X% of traffic to new deployment before full release
• Deployment history: roll back to any previous deployment
• Logging: access logs (custom format) and execution logs (CloudWatch)

• Scenario: expose Lambda as HTTPS endpoint → API Gateway + Lambda Proxy integration
• Scenario: directly write to SQS from API Gateway without Lambda → AWS Service integration
• Scenario: private API only accessible from within VPC → Private API Gateway + Interface VPC Endpoint
• Scenario: throttle a specific 3rd-party consumer → Usage Plan + API Key
• Scenario: real-time chat → WebSocket API
• TRAP: REST API ≠ HTTP API — HTTP API lacks caching, usage plans, request validation, WAF, and resource policies
• TRAP: 29-second hard timeout — Lambda must respond within 29s for synchronous API Gateway calls
• TRAP: CORS errors in browser → enable CORS on API Gateway, not just the Lambda function

aws-appsync.text:
• Managed GraphQL and Pub/Sub API service
• Connects to multiple data sources in a single query: DynamoDB, Lambda, RDS (Aurora Serverless), HTTP endpoints, OpenSearch
• Real-time subscriptions: push updates to connected clients via WebSockets automatically
• Offline sync: via Amplify DataStore — conflict resolution and delta sync when device reconnects

• Schema: GraphQL schema defines types, queries, mutations, and subscriptions
• Resolvers: connect each GraphQL operation to a data source
  - Unit resolvers: VTL (Velocity Template Language) mapping templates
  - Pipeline resolvers: chain multiple functions for complex logic
  - Direct Lambda resolver: forward GraphQL op directly to Lambda (no VTL)
• Caching: server-side caching per resolver or full request cache (reduces data source hits)
• Authorization modes: API Key (dev/test), AWS IAM (service-to-service), Cognito User Pools (user auth), OIDC, Lambda (custom)
• Multiple auth modes: use different auth modes per API operation

• Scenario: mobile app needs real-time data updates (e.g., live collaboration, chat) → AppSync subscriptions
• Scenario: aggregate data from DynamoDB + RDS in a single API call → AppSync with multiple resolvers
• Scenario: offline-capable mobile app with sync when reconnected → AppSync + Amplify DataStore
• TRAP: AppSync ≠ API Gateway — AppSync is GraphQL with real-time/offline; API Gateway is REST/HTTP/WebSocket
• TRAP: AppSync subscriptions work over WebSockets — clients must support WebSocket connections
• TRAP: AppSync is NOT serverless in the sense that you define the schema and resolvers — but AWS manages all infrastructure

aws-lambda.text:
• Functions as a Service (FaaS) — run code without managing servers
• Supported runtimes: Node.js, Python, Java, C#/.NET, Go, Ruby, PowerShell, custom (via Runtime API)
• Max timeout: 900 seconds (15 minutes)
• Memory: 128 MB to 10,240 MB (CPU and network bandwidth scale proportionally with memory)
• Ephemeral storage: /tmp — 512 MB default, up to 10,240 MB
• Deployment package: up to 50 MB zipped, 250 MB unzipped (use Lambda Layers for shared code)

• Synchronous: caller waits for response — API Gateway, ALB, Cognito, SDK direct call; errors returned to caller
• Asynchronous: Lambda retries on failure (2 retries); configure Dead Letter Queue (SQS/SNS) for failed events; EventBridge, S3 events, SNS
• Event Source Mapping (poll-based): Lambda polls the source — Kinesis, DynamoDB Streams, SQS, MQ, MSK, Kafka
  - SQS: Lambda polls and processes batches; configure batch size and batch window
  - Kinesis/DynamoDB Streams: processed in shard order; parallelization factor per shard

• Concurrency: number of simultaneous executions — default 1,000 per region (soft limit, can increase)
• Reserved Concurrency: guarantee a max number of concurrent executions for a function — also acts as a throttle cap
• Provisioned Concurrency: pre-warm instances to eliminate cold start latency — ideal for latency-sensitive APIs
• Cold start: first invocation initializes execution environment (runtime + code) — adds latency
• SnapStart (Java 11+): pre-initialize execution environment and take snapshot — reduces Java cold starts by up to 90%
• Lambda@Edge and CloudFront Functions: run code at edge locations; cannot use Provisioned Concurrency

• VPC integration: Lambda can access VPC resources (RDS, ElastiCache) — creates ENIs in specified subnets
  - Requires: VPC, subnets, security groups; ENI creation takes ~10s on first cold start in VPC
  - Lambda in VPC has no internet access by default — add NAT Gateway for internet + VPC for private resources
• Lambda Layers: zip archives attached to functions — share common dependencies (SDKs, libraries) across functions; up to 5 layers per function
• Environment variables: key-value pairs available at runtime; encrypt with KMS for sensitive values
• Lambda Extensions: run monitoring/security agents alongside function code (in same execution environment)

• Scenario: Lambda needs to access RDS in VPC → deploy Lambda in same VPC + security group; use RDS Proxy for connection pooling
• Scenario: Lambda cold starts causing latency spikes → Provisioned Concurrency
• Scenario: Lambda Java function cold starts too slow → SnapStart
• Scenario: process SQS messages in Lambda → Event Source Mapping; set DLQ on SQS for failures
• Scenario: share common Python library across 10 Lambda functions → Lambda Layer
• TRAP: Lambda in VPC needs NAT Gateway for internet access — VPC Lambda loses default internet access
• TRAP: Lambda cannot run longer than 15 minutes — for longer jobs use ECS/Fargate, AWS Batch, or Step Functions
• TRAP: increasing memory increases cost but also increases CPU — sometimes faster = cheaper
• TRAP: SQS visibility timeout should be at least 6x the Lambda timeout to avoid duplicate processing

aws-step-functions.text:
• Serverless workflow orchestration using visual state machines
• Coordinates distributed components and microservices using Amazon States Language (JSON-based)
• State types: Task (do work), Choice (branch logic), Wait (pause), Parallel (run branches simultaneously), Map (iterate over array), Pass (passthrough), Succeed, Fail
• Two workflow types: Standard and Express

• Standard Workflows:
  - Exactly-once execution
  - Max duration: 1 year
  - Async only — start execution, poll for result
  - Priced per state transition
  - Use for: long-running business processes, human approval flows, order processing
• Express Workflows:
  - At-least-once execution (async) or exactly-once (sync)
  - Max duration: 5 minutes
  - Synchronous Express: caller waits for result (like a function call) — ideal for API Gateway integration
  - Priced per execution + duration + memory
  - Use for: high-volume, short-duration event processing (IoT, streaming, microservices)

• Service integrations: Lambda, DynamoDB, ECS, SNS, SQS, API Gateway, Bedrock, SageMaker, Glue, EMR, Athena, and more
• Integration patterns:
  - Request-Response (default): call service, don't wait for job to finish
  - Sync (.sync): wait for job completion (ECS, Glue, EMR)
  - Wait for Callback (.waitForTaskToken): pause workflow and send token to external system; resumes when token is returned via SendTaskSuccess/Failure
• Human approval pattern: Step Functions → SNS email with approve/reject link → API Gateway → SendTaskSuccess
• Error handling: Retry (exponential backoff, max attempts) and Catch (route to error handling state) defined per state

• Scenario: order processing workflow with multiple steps, retries, and human approval → Step Functions Standard
• Scenario: high-volume IoT event processing with multiple parallel transformations → Step Functions Express
• Scenario: orchestrate ETL pipeline: S3 → Glue → Athena → QuickSight → Step Functions with .sync integrations
• Scenario: pause workflow until a human approves via email → waitForTaskToken pattern
• Scenario: fan out to 100 parallel Lambda invocations → Map state with MaxConcurrency
• TRAP: Step Functions should orchestrate multiple Lambda functions — NOT chaining Lambdas calling each other (anti-pattern — hard to manage errors and retries)
• TRAP: Standard Workflows are exactly-once; Express are at-least-once for async — idempotency matters
• TRAP: Step Functions Express max is 5 minutes — for longer workflows use Standard

amazon-elastic-block-store.text:
• Network-attached persistent block storage for EC2 instances
• Tied to a specific AZ — must be in same AZ as the EC2 instance
• Persists independently from EC2 — survives stop/start/reboot; deleted on termination only if DeleteOnTermination=true
• Can detach and reattach to another instance in the same AZ
• Multi-Attach: attach one io1/io2 volume to up to 16 EC2 instances in the same AZ (for clustered apps like RAC)
• Billed per GB provisioned per month + IOPS for io1/io2

• gp2 (General Purpose SSD): baseline 3 IOPS/GB, bursts to 3,000 IOPS; max 16,000 IOPS at 5,334 GB; IOPS and size linked
• gp3 (General Purpose SSD): baseline 3,000 IOPS + 125 MB/s independently of size; max 16,000 IOPS + 1,000 MB/s; more cost-effective than gp2
• io1 (Provisioned IOPS SSD): max 64,000 IOPS (Nitro EC2) or 32,000 (others); IOPS:GB ratio max 50:1; for critical databases
• io2 Block Express: max 256,000 IOPS; sub-millisecond latency; IOPS:GB ratio 1000:1; highest performance
• st1 (Throughput Optimized HDD): max 500 IOPS, 500 MB/s throughput; for big data, log processing; cannot be boot volume
• sc1 (Cold HDD): max 250 IOPS, 250 MB/s; lowest cost; for infrequently accessed cold data; cannot be boot volume
• Only SSD types (gp2, gp3, io1, io2) can be used as boot volumes

• Incremental backups to S3 — only changed blocks since last snapshot are saved
• Can copy snapshots across regions for DR or migration
• Can create AMIs from snapshots
• Snapshot Archive: move snapshots to archive tier — 75% cheaper; restore takes 24–72 hours
• Recycle Bin: retain deleted snapshots for 1 day to 1 year — accidental deletion protection
• Fast Snapshot Restore (FSR): pre-warm snapshot so restored volumes are immediately at full performance (charged per AZ per snapshot)
• Snapshots during use: can take snapshot while volume is in use (slight performance impact)

• Encryption uses KMS (AES-256) — transparent to EC2; no performance impact
• Encrypted volumes: data at rest, in transit between EC2 and EBS, and all snapshots are encrypted
• Unencrypted → encrypted: snapshot the volume → copy snapshot with encryption enabled → create new volume from encrypted snapshot
• Encrypted by default: can enable account-level default encryption for all new EBS volumes
• Cannot encrypt an existing unencrypted volume directly — must go through snapshot copy process

• Scenario: high-performance OLTP database needs max IOPS → io2 Block Express
• Scenario: general web server with OS disk → gp3 (cheaper and more flexible than gp2)
• Scenario: Hadoop/big data workloads needing high throughput not IOPS → st1
• Scenario: move EBS volume to another AZ → snapshot → create new volume in target AZ from snapshot
• Scenario: move EBS volume to another region → snapshot → copy snapshot to target region → create volume
• TRAP: EBS volumes are AZ-locked — cannot directly attach to EC2 in different AZ
• TRAP: gp2 IOPS scales with size (linked); gp3 IOPS are independent of size
• TRAP: st1 and sc1 cannot be boot volumes — exam may try to trick you here
• TRAP: Multi-Attach only for io1/io2 and only within the same AZ; application must manage concurrent writes

amazon-elastic-file-system.text:
• Fully managed, elastic NFS (Network File System) — shared file storage for Linux workloads
• Multi-AZ: mount simultaneously from thousands of EC2 instances across multiple AZs
• Elastic: automatically grows and shrinks — no capacity planning or provisioning
• POSIX-compliant: Linux only (not Windows)
• Higher cost than EBS — pay per GB stored (not provisioned)
• Use cases: content management, web serving, data sharing, home directories, container storage

• Performance modes (set at creation, cannot change):
  - General Purpose (default): lowest latency; ideal for web serving, CMS, general file serving
  - Max I/O: higher throughput + higher latency; for massively parallel big data and media processing
• Throughput modes:
  - Bursting: throughput scales with storage size (baseline 50 KB/s per GB + burst credits)
  - Provisioned: fixed throughput regardless of storage size — when you need more throughput than storage-based burst allows
  - Elastic (recommended): automatically scales throughput up/down based on workload — best for spiky or unpredictable workloads; up to 3 GB/s read, 1 GB/s write

• Storage tiers:
  - Standard: frequently accessed files; highest availability
  - Standard-IA (Infrequent Access): lower storage cost + per-access retrieval fee
  - Archive: for files accessed a few times per year; cheapest; higher retrieval latency
• Lifecycle Management: automatically move files between tiers based on last access time (e.g., move to IA after 30 days)
• EFS Intelligent Tiering: automatically moves files between Standard and IA based on access patterns

• Encryption at rest: KMS (enable at creation time)
• Encryption in transit: TLS (enforce via mount options)
• Security Groups: control network access to EFS mount targets
• IAM authorization: EFS access points enforce user identity and root directory per application
• EFS Access Points: create named entry points into EFS with specific POSIX user/group and root directory — isolate app access
• VPC: EFS mount targets are created per AZ in the VPC; clients mount using DNS name

• Scenario: shared file storage for multiple EC2 instances across AZs → EFS
• Scenario: WordPress media files shared across an Auto Scaling Group → EFS
• Scenario: containerized app (ECS/EKS) needs shared persistent storage → EFS
• Scenario: reduce EFS costs for infrequently accessed files → EFS Lifecycle Management → Standard-IA
• TRAP: EFS is Linux-only (NFS); for Windows shared file storage use FSx for Windows File Server
• TRAP: EFS is more expensive than EBS per GB — use EBS for single-instance block storage
• TRAP: EFS Standard-IA has a per-access retrieval charge — if files are accessed frequently, Standard is cheaper
• TRAP: EFS performance mode cannot be changed after creation — choose General Purpose unless you know you need Max I/O

amazon-s3.text:
• Infinitely scalable object storage — store any amount of data, pay for what you use
• Objects stored in buckets; buckets are region-specific but names must be globally unique
• Max object size: 5 TB; single PUT limit: 5 GB
• Multipart upload: recommended for > 100 MB, required for > 5 GB; parallelizes upload for speed
• S3 Transfer Acceleration: upload to nearest CloudFront edge → AWS private network → S3 bucket
• Strong read-after-write consistency for all operations (since Dec 2020)
• URL format: https://bucket-name.s3.region.amazonaws.com/key

• S3 Standard: 99.99% availability, 11 nines durability, 3+ AZs; for frequently accessed data; no retrieval fee
• S3 Intelligent-Tiering: auto-moves objects between access tiers based on patterns; no retrieval fee; small monthly monitoring fee; tiers: Frequent, Infrequent, Archive Instant, Archive, Deep Archive
• S3 Standard-IA: infrequent access; lower storage cost; retrieval fee per GB; min 30-day storage; min 128 KB object size charge
• S3 One Zone-IA: single AZ; 20% cheaper than Standard-IA; data lost if AZ fails; min 30 days; for re-creatable data
• S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval: millisecond retrieval; min 90-day storage; for quarterly access data
• S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval: Expedited (1-5 min), Standard (3-5h), Bulk (5-12h); min 90-day storage
• S3 Glacier Deep Archive: cheapest; Standard (12h), Bulk (48h); min 180-day storage; for 7-10 year retention

• Bucket policies: resource-based JSON policies — grant cross-account access, enforce HTTPS, restrict by IP or VPC endpoint
• ACLs: legacy object/bucket-level access control — disabled by default on new buckets (use bucket policies instead)
• Block Public Access: 4 settings to prevent public access — enable at account level to prevent accidental exposure
• S3 Object Ownership: enforce bucket owner ownership of all objects (disables ACLs) — recommended setting
• Presigned URLs: grant temporary access (upload or download) to private objects without exposing credentials; set expiry time
• VPC Endpoint for S3 (Gateway type): private access from VPC to S3 without internet/NAT; free; add to route table
• S3 Access Points: named network endpoints with their own policies — simplify access management for large shared buckets

• Versioning: keep all versions of objects; once enabled, can only be suspended (never fully disabled); MFA Delete for extra protection
• Cross-Region Replication (CRR): replicate objects to bucket in another region; requires versioning on both; for compliance, lower latency reads
• Same-Region Replication (SRR): replicate within same region; for log aggregation, test/prod sync
• Replication: only new objects after enabling; existing objects need S3 Batch Replication; delete markers optionally replicated
• Lifecycle Rules: transition actions (move to cheaper class after N days) + expiration actions (delete after N days)
• Lifecycle targets: current versions, previous versions, incomplete multipart uploads

• Event Notifications: trigger on s3:ObjectCreated, s3:ObjectRemoved, s3:ObjectRestore, etc.
  - Destinations: SNS, SQS, Lambda (direct); EventBridge (all events + advanced filtering + more targets)
• S3 Object Lambda: attach Lambda to S3 GET requests — transform data on the fly (redact PII, convert formats, add watermarks)
• S3 Select: retrieve subset of data from CSV/JSON/Parquet using SQL — reduces data transfer and cost
• S3 Inventory: generate daily/weekly CSV or ORC reports of all objects and metadata in a bucket
• S3 Storage Lens: org-wide storage analytics and activity metrics across all buckets and accounts
• Object Lock (WORM): prevent object deletion/overwrite; Governance mode (some users can override) vs Compliance mode (no one can override, including root)
• Requester Pays: requester pays for data transfer and request costs (not bucket owner) — for sharing large datasets

• SSE-S3 (default): S3-managed keys (AES-256); enabled by default on all new buckets since Jan 2023; header: x-amz-server-side-encryption: AES256
• SSE-KMS: KMS-managed keys; audit trail in CloudTrail; rate limits apply (KMS API calls); header: x-amz-server-side-encryption: aws:kms
• SSE-C: customer-provided keys; must provide key with every request; AWS does not store the key; HTTPS required
• Client-side encryption: encrypt before uploading; AWS has no knowledge of key or plaintext
• Enforce encryption in transit: bucket policy with Condition aws:SecureTransport: false → Deny

• Scenario: host static website on S3 → enable static website hosting + bucket policy for public read (or CloudFront + OAC)
• Scenario: compliance — objects cannot be deleted for 7 years → S3 Object Lock Compliance mode
• Scenario: access S3 from EC2 without internet → VPC Gateway Endpoint for S3
• Scenario: transform S3 objects on read without storing modified copies → S3 Object Lambda
• Scenario: notify Lambda every time a file is uploaded → S3 Event Notification → Lambda
• TRAP: S3 is not a file system — it's object storage; no directory structure (just key prefixes)
• TRAP: CRR and SRR require versioning enabled on BOTH source and destination buckets
• TRAP: SSE-KMS has KMS API rate limits — high-throughput S3 workloads may hit KMS throttling
• TRAP: S3 Standard-IA and One Zone-IA have minimum 30-day storage charge and minimum 128 KB object size
• TRAP: S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval is NOT instant — plan for retrieval time; use Glacier Instant Retrieval for ms access

amazon-s3-glacier.text:
• Ultra-low-cost archival storage — for data accessed rarely (once or twice per year)
• Part of the S3 storage class family — managed via S3 Lifecycle policies or directly
• Three products: S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval, S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval, S3 Glacier Deep Archive
• 11 nines (99.999999999%) durability across 3+ AZs
• Minimum storage durations: Instant/Flexible = 90 days; Deep Archive = 180 days

• Glacier Flexible Retrieval:
  - Expedited: 1–5 minutes (highest cost; Provisioned Capacity ensures availability)
  - Standard: 3–5 hours
  - Bulk: 5–12 hours (lowest cost)
• Glacier Deep Archive:
  - Standard: up to 12 hours
  - Bulk: up to 48 hours
• Glacier Instant Retrieval: millisecond retrieval — same as S3 Standard-IA speed but cheaper for quarterly access
• Vault Lock: enforce compliance controls using a WORM (Write Once Read Many) policy on a vault
  - Once locked, policy cannot be changed — even by AWS root
  - Use for regulatory compliance (SEC 17a-4, HIPAA, CJIS)
• Provisioned Capacity: purchase units to ensure Expedited retrievals are available when needed

• Scenario: archive compliance records for 10 years, lowest cost → S3 Glacier Deep Archive
• Scenario: archive medical images, need to retrieve within 5 minutes occasionally → S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval + Provisioned Capacity
• Scenario: regulatory requirement that archived records cannot be deleted or modified → Glacier Vault Lock (WORM)
• Scenario: archive data accessed a few times per year but need millisecond retrieval → S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval
• TRAP: Glacier Flexible Retrieval is NOT immediate — plan hours for retrieval; use Instant Retrieval for ms access
• TRAP: minimum storage duration charges apply even if you delete early — design lifecycle policies carefully
• TRAP: Vault Lock ≠ Object Lock — Vault Lock is for Glacier vaults via direct Glacier API; Object Lock is for S3 buckets

aws-backup.text:
• Centrally manage and automate data protection across AWS services from a single place
• Supported services: EC2 (AMIs), EBS, RDS (all engines), Aurora, DynamoDB, EFS, FSx (all types), S3, Storage Gateway volumes, DocumentDB, Neptune, VMware on AWS
• Backup Plans: define backup schedule, lifecycle (transition to cold storage, retention period), and vault
• Backup Vault: encrypted S3-backed storage for backups; apply resource-based access policies

• On-demand backups: immediate backup outside of scheduled plan
• Cross-Region backups: copy backups to another region for DR compliance
• Cross-Account backups: copy backups to another AWS account via Organizations — protects against accidental/malicious deletion in source account
• Backup Vault Lock: WORM mode on a vault — even the root user and AWS cannot delete backups; enforce minimum/maximum retention periods
• Tag-based backup policies: automatically back up all resources with specific tags across accounts
• AWS Backup Audit Manager: generate compliance reports on backup activity and policy adherence
• Restore testing: automatically test restores on a schedule and report success/failure

• Scenario: centralize backup for EC2, RDS, EFS, DynamoDB across 20 accounts → AWS Backup with Organizations
• Scenario: prevent backup deletion even by admins for compliance → Backup Vault Lock
• Scenario: automatically back up all resources tagged with Environment=Production → tag-based backup policy
• Scenario: DR requirement — keep copies of backups in another region → cross-region backup copy in Backup Plan
• TRAP: AWS Backup does NOT replace service-native backups entirely — it orchestrates and centralizes them
• TRAP: EFS and S3 backups via AWS Backup are different from EFS/S3 native replication — Backup creates restore points
• TRAP: Backup Vault Lock has a 72-hour cool-off period after creation before it becomes immutable

aws-storage-gateway.text:
• Hybrid cloud storage bridge — connects on-premises environments to AWS storage seamlessly
• Deployed as a VM appliance (VMware, Hyper-V, KVM) or hardware appliance
• On-premises apps continue using NFS, SMB, iSCSI protocols — no app changes needed
• Four gateway types: S3 File Gateway, FSx File Gateway, Volume Gateway (Stored/Cached), Tape Gateway
• All gateways use S3 as the durable backend storage (except Tape which uses S3 + Glacier)

• S3 File Gateway:
  - NFS (Linux) or SMB (Windows) access to objects stored in S3
  - Files stored as native S3 objects — accessible directly in S3 too
  - Local cache for recently accessed data (low-latency reads)
  - Supports all S3 storage classes via lifecycle policies
  - Use for: cloud-backed file shares, backup target, tiering cold data to S3
• FSx File Gateway:
  - SMB access to Amazon FSx for Windows File Server
  - Local cache for frequently accessed files
  - Integrates with Active Directory
  - Use for: Windows file shares with local cache + cloud durability
• Volume Gateway – Cached Mode:
  - Primary data stored in S3; frequently accessed data cached on-premises
  - Presented as iSCSI block device to on-prem servers
  - Low-latency for cached data; full dataset in S3
• Volume Gateway – Stored Mode:
  - Primary data stored on-premises; asynchronously backed up to S3 as EBS snapshots
  - Full low-latency access to all data locally
  - Use for: primary on-prem storage with cloud backup
• Tape Gateway:
  - Virtual Tape Library (VTL) — emulates physical tape infrastructure
  - Backup software (Veeam, Veritas, Commvault) writes to virtual tapes → stored in S3
  - Archive virtual tapes to S3 Glacier or Deep Archive
  - Use for: replace physical tape backup without changing backup software

• Scenario: on-prem app writes to NFS share, want data backed up to S3 → S3 File Gateway
• Scenario: keep most data in S3 but need fast local access for hot files → Volume Gateway Cached Mode
• Scenario: full on-prem storage with periodic backup to AWS → Volume Gateway Stored Mode
• Scenario: replace physical tape library with cloud-backed virtual tapes → Tape Gateway
• Scenario: Windows apps on-prem need SMB file share backed by FSx → FSx File Gateway
• TRAP: Storage Gateway ≠ DataSync — Gateway is ongoing hybrid access; DataSync is bulk one-time or scheduled migration
• TRAP: S3 File Gateway caches locally but writes go directly to S3 — on-prem storage is cache only (in cached mode)
• TRAP: Volume Gateway presents iSCSI block storage — File Gateway presents NFS/SMB file storage; different protocols

vpc-fundamentals.text:
• VPC (Virtual Private Cloud): logically isolated network in AWS where you launch resources
• Region-scoped: one VPC spans multiple AZs within a region
• Default VPC: pre-created in each region; has default subnets in each AZ; all public; ready to use
• Custom VPC: you define CIDR, subnets, routing, and security — best practice for production
• CIDR block: define IP range at VPC creation (e.g., 10.0.0.0/16); cannot change after creation but can add secondary CIDRs
• Max: 5 VPCs per region (soft limit), /16 to /28 CIDR size

• Subnet: a range of IPs within a VPC, tied to a single AZ
• Public subnet: has a route to an Internet Gateway — resources get public IP access
• Private subnet: no route to Internet Gateway — resources are isolated from internet
• AWS reserves 5 IPs per subnet (first 4 + last 1): network address, VPC router, DNS, reserved for future, broadcast
• Example: 10.0.0.0/24 → usable IPs: 10.0.0.1 to 10.0.0.254 (251 usable, not 256)
• Best practice: create at least 1 public + 1 private subnet per AZ for HA

internet-gateway-and-nat.text:
• Allows resources in public subnets to communicate with the internet
• Attached to a VPC (one IGW per VPC)
• Horizontally scaled, redundant, HA — no bandwidth limits
• For a subnet to be 'public': must have route 0.0.0.0/0 → IGW in its route table AND resource must have public/Elastic IP
• IGW also performs NAT for instances with public IPs

• Allows private subnet instances to initiate outbound internet connections (e.g., for OS updates) without being reachable from the internet
• Managed by AWS — HA within an AZ; for multi-AZ HA, deploy one NAT GW per AZ
• Deployed in a PUBLIC subnet; private subnet route table: 0.0.0.0/0 → NAT Gateway
• Bandwidth: starts at 5 Gbps, scales to 100 Gbps automatically
• Not free: charged per hour + per GB processed
• Supports TCP, UDP, ICMP
• NAT Gateway vs NAT Instance (legacy): NAT GW is managed, scalable, HA within AZ; NAT Instance is EC2-based, manual, not HA but cheaper and supports port forwarding/security groups

• Scenario: private EC2 needs to download patches from internet → NAT Gateway in public subnet + route in private subnet
• Scenario: multi-AZ setup — private subnets in 3 AZs need internet access → 3 NAT Gateways (one per AZ)
• TRAP: NAT Gateway is in the PUBLIC subnet — not the private subnet
• TRAP: NAT Gateway costs money — a common exam cost optimization answer is to check if NAT GW is necessary
• TRAP: NAT Gateway cannot be used for inbound internet connections — only outbound
• TRAP: one NAT GW per AZ is needed for full HA — a single NAT GW is a single point of failure if that AZ goes down

security-groups-and-nacls.text:
• Virtual firewall at the ENI/instance level
• Stateful: return traffic is automatically allowed regardless of inbound rules
• Rules: ALLOW only — no deny rules
• Default: all outbound allowed, all inbound denied (for new custom SGs)
• Can reference other security groups as source/destination — not just IP ranges
• Applies to: EC2, RDS, Lambda (in VPC), ELB, ECS tasks, ElastiCache, etc.
• Changes take effect immediately
• Limits: up to 5 SGs per ENI; up to 60 inbound + 60 outbound rules per SG

• Firewall at the subnet level — applies to all traffic entering/leaving the subnet
• Stateless: must define both inbound AND outbound rules explicitly
• Rules: ALLOW and DENY — evaluated in order by rule number (lowest first); first match wins
• Default NACL: allows ALL inbound and outbound traffic
• Custom NACL: denies everything by default — must add allow rules
• Ephemeral ports: for outbound responses, must allow port range 1024–65535 in NACL outbound rules
• Changes take some time to propagate
• One NACL per subnet; a NACL can be associated with multiple subnets

• Security Group: instance-level, stateful, allow-only, evaluated as a whole
• NACL: subnet-level, stateless, allow + deny, evaluated in order by rule number
• Use both together for defense in depth
• Common pattern: use NACLs to block specific IPs/CIDRs at subnet level (e.g., block a malicious IP); use SGs for fine-grained instance access
• TRAP: NACLs are stateless — if you allow inbound HTTP (80), you must also allow outbound ephemeral ports for the response
• TRAP: SGs cannot explicitly deny — use NACLs to block specific IPs
• TRAP: SGs apply per instance; NACLs apply per subnet — understand which layer is being tested

vpc-endpoints.text:
• VPC Endpoints allow private connectivity from your VPC to AWS services without traversing the internet
• Traffic stays within the AWS network — no Internet Gateway, NAT Gateway, VPN, or Direct Connect needed
• Two types: Gateway Endpoints and Interface Endpoints
• Benefits: improved security, reduced NAT Gateway data processing costs, lower latency

• Supported services: Amazon S3 and Amazon DynamoDB ONLY
• Free — no hourly charge or data processing fee
• Works via route table entries — add a route pointing to the gateway endpoint prefix list
• Scope: VPC-level; associated with specific route tables
• No DNS changes needed — use existing S3/DynamoDB DNS names
• Cannot be extended outside the VPC (no Direct Connect, VPN, or peering)

• Powered by AWS PrivateLink — creates an ENI with a private IP in your subnet
• Supports 100+ AWS services: EC2 API, SSM, Secrets Manager, KMS, CloudWatch, SNS, SQS, API Gateway, and more
• Charged: per hour per AZ + per GB data processed
• DNS resolution: endpoint provides private DNS names; enable private DNS to override the public DNS name
• Security: controlled by Security Groups attached to the endpoint ENI
• Can be accessed from on-premises via Direct Connect or VPN (unlike Gateway Endpoints)

• Scenario: private EC2 needs to access S3 without internet/NAT → Gateway Endpoint for S3 (free)
• Scenario: private EC2 needs to call Secrets Manager or SSM without internet → Interface Endpoint
• Scenario: on-prem servers need to access S3 privately via Direct Connect → Interface Endpoint for S3 (Gateway Endpoints don't support on-prem)
• Scenario: reduce costs — EC2 in private subnet making many S3 calls through NAT Gateway → replace NAT with S3 Gateway Endpoint
• TRAP: Gateway Endpoints = S3 + DynamoDB only; everything else uses Interface Endpoints
• TRAP: Gateway Endpoints are free; Interface Endpoints cost money — always prefer Gateway for S3/DynamoDB
• TRAP: Gateway Endpoints cannot be accessed from on-premises — must use Interface Endpoint for that use case

vpc-peering.text:
• Direct private network connection between two VPCs (same or different region, same or different account)
• Traffic stays on AWS backbone — not encrypted by default but private
• Non-transitive: A↔B and B↔C does NOT mean A↔C — need explicit peering between each pair
• CIDRs must not overlap between peered VPCs
• Both sides must accept the peering request and add route table entries

• Steps: create peering connection → accept in target VPC → add routes in BOTH VPCs' route tables → update security groups
• No bandwidth limits — behaves like same-region connectivity
• Inter-region VPC peering: supported; traffic is encrypted by default when crossing regions
• No edge-to-edge routing: cannot use a peered VPC's Internet Gateway, NAT Gateway, VPN, or Direct Connect
• Limits: 125 peering connections per VPC (use Transit Gateway for larger meshes)

• Scenario: connect Dev VPC to Prod VPC for database access → VPC Peering
• Scenario: 10 VPCs all need to communicate with each other → Transit Gateway (not 45 peering connections)
• TRAP: VPC Peering is NOT transitive — A↔B and B↔C ≠ A↔C
• TRAP: overlapping CIDR blocks prevent peering — plan IP ranges in advance
• TRAP: route tables must be updated on BOTH sides — peering alone is not enough
• TRAP: cannot peer VPCs with the same CIDR — even 10.0.0.0/16 and 10.0.0.0/16 in different accounts

vpc-flow-logs.text:
• Capture network traffic metadata for VPCs, subnets, or individual ENIs
• Does NOT capture packet content — only metadata (source IP, dest IP, ports, protocol, bytes, action ACCEPT/REJECT)
• Published to: CloudWatch Logs, Amazon S3, or Kinesis Data Firehose
• Covers: EC2 instances, ELB, RDS, ElastiCache, Redshift, NAT Gateway, Transit Gateway, etc.
• Three levels: VPC level (all traffic), Subnet level, ENI level
• Not real-time — slight delay before logs appear

• Key log fields: srcaddr, dstaddr, srcport, dstport, protocol, packets, bytes, start, end, action (ACCEPT/REJECT), log-status
• Custom format: choose which fields to include and in what order
• Use Athena to query Flow Logs stored in S3 for cost-effective analysis
• Use CloudWatch Logs Insights for real-time querying
• Use cases: troubleshoot connectivity issues, identify port scanning, audit security group rules, detect unusual traffic patterns

• Scenario: identify which IP addresses are being rejected by security groups → VPC Flow Logs (action=REJECT)
• Scenario: detect if an EC2 instance is communicating with suspicious external IPs → Flow Logs + Athena or GuardDuty
• Scenario: troubleshoot why EC2 can't connect to RDS → check Flow Logs for REJECT on port 3306
• TRAP: Flow Logs capture METADATA not packet content — for packet inspection use VPC Traffic Mirroring
• TRAP: Flow Logs do NOT capture: DNS traffic to Route 53, DHCP traffic, instance metadata (169.254.169.254), Windows license activation traffic
• TRAP: enabling Flow Logs does NOT affect network performance — it's passive metadata capture

bastion-hosts-and-site-to-site-vpn.text:
• EC2 instance in a public subnet used as a jump server to SSH/RDP into private instances
• Users SSH to bastion → SSH from bastion to private EC2
• Security: bastion SG should only allow SSH from known IPs (corporate CIDR)
• Private instances SG: only allow SSH from bastion's security group (not public internet)
• High availability: deploy bastion hosts in multiple AZs; use NLB in front of multiple bastions
• Modern alternative: AWS Systems Manager Session Manager — no bastion needed, no open port 22

• Encrypted IPSec VPN tunnel between on-premises network and AWS VPC
• Components:
  - Virtual Private Gateway (VGW): AWS side of the VPN — attached to VPC
  - Customer Gateway (CGW): on-premises side — represents your router/firewall device
• Two tunnels per connection for redundancy (active/passive or active/active with BGP)
• Bandwidth: up to 1.25 Gbps per tunnel (limited by VGW)
• Route types: static routes or dynamic (BGP — Border Gateway Protocol)
• VPN CloudHub: connect multiple branch offices to AWS using multiple Customer Gateways via single VGW
• Accelerated VPN: route VPN traffic over AWS Global Accelerator backbone for improved performance

• Scenario: secure access to private EC2 without opening port 22 → Systems Manager Session Manager
• Scenario: connect on-prem data center to AWS VPC securely and quickly (within hours) → Site-to-Site VPN
• Scenario: connect 5 branch offices to AWS → VPN CloudHub (multiple CGWs on one VGW)
• Scenario: Direct Connect went down, need failover connectivity → Site-to-Site VPN as backup
• TRAP: Site-to-Site VPN is encrypted; Direct Connect is NOT encrypted by default
• TRAP: VPN bandwidth is limited to 1.25 Gbps per tunnel — for higher bandwidth use Direct Connect
• TRAP: VPN goes over the public internet (even though encrypted) — latency can vary; Direct Connect is dedicated private line

vpc-advanced-features.text:
• Like a NAT Gateway but for IPv6 traffic
• Allows IPv6 instances in private subnets to initiate outbound internet connections
• Blocks inbound IPv6 connections from the internet
• Free — no charge (unlike NAT Gateway)
• Add route ::/0 → Egress-Only IGW in private subnet route table
• IPv4 equivalent: NAT Gateway; IPv6 equivalent: Egress-Only Internet Gateway

• Copy network traffic from ENIs to a target for inspection (IDS/IPS, packet capture tools)
• Source: ENI on EC2 instance
• Target: ENI (another EC2 running inspection tool) or NLB
• Captures actual packet content (unlike Flow Logs which only capture metadata)
• Use for: deep packet inspection, threat detection, network forensics
• Filter: capture only specific traffic (by CIDR, protocol, port)

• VPC can operate in dual-stack mode: IPv4 + IPv6 simultaneously
• IPv6 addresses are globally unique and public by default — no concept of private IPv6
• For private IPv6 subnets: use Egress-Only Internet Gateway for outbound only
• Enable IPv6 CIDR on VPC and subnets; assign IPv6 addresses to instances
• All IPv6 addresses are /128 (single address per instance)
• Route: ::/0 → IGW for public IPv6; ::/0 → Egress-Only IGW for private IPv6 outbound

• VPC DNS server (Route 53 Resolver): available at VPC CIDR base + 2 (e.g., 10.0.0.2)
• Two VPC DNS settings:
  - enableDnsHostnames: assigns public DNS hostnames to instances with public IPs
  - enableDnsSupport: enables DNS resolution via AWS DNS server (required for most features)
• Both must be enabled for Interface VPC Endpoints with private DNS to work
• Route 53 Resolver Inbound Endpoints: allow on-prem DNS to resolve AWS private hosted zone records
• Route 53 Resolver Outbound Endpoints: allow VPC DNS queries to be forwarded to on-prem DNS servers
• Resolver Rules: define which domains to forward to on-prem (forward rules) or resolve in VPC (system rules)

• Scenario: IPv6 EC2 in private subnet needs outbound internet access → Egress-Only Internet Gateway
• Scenario: need full packet capture for forensics investigation → VPC Traffic Mirroring
• Scenario: on-prem servers need to resolve private Route 53 zones → Resolver Inbound Endpoint
• Scenario: VPC resources need to resolve on-prem DNS hostnames → Resolver Outbound Endpoint + Forwarding Rule
• TRAP: IPv6 addresses are ALWAYS public — there's no private IPv6 range like RFC1918 for IPv4
• TRAP: Egress-Only IGW is for IPv6 ONLY; NAT Gateway is for IPv4 ONLY
• TRAP: enableDnsSupport must be enabled for Interface Endpoints with private DNS names to work

well-architected-framework.text:
• Operational Excellence: run and monitor systems, continually improve processes
• Security: protect data, systems, and assets; use cloud capabilities to improve security
• Reliability: recover from failures, dynamically acquire resources, mitigate disruptions
• Performance Efficiency: use computing resources efficiently; maintain efficiency as demand changes
• Cost Optimization: avoid unnecessary costs; understand spending and control where money goes
• Sustainability: minimize environmental impact of running cloud workloads

• Design principles: perform operations as code (IaC), make frequent small reversible changes, anticipate failure, learn from failure
• Key services: CloudFormation (IaC), CodePipeline (CI/CD), CloudWatch (monitoring), CloudTrail (audit), X-Ray (tracing), Config (compliance)
• Runbooks: documented procedures for routine operations
• Playbooks: documented steps for incident response
• Game Days: simulate failures to test response procedures

• Design principles: implement strong identity foundation, enable traceability, apply security at all layers, automate security, protect data in transit and at rest, minimize blast radius
• Identity: IAM least privilege, MFA, SCPs, IAM Identity Center
• Detective controls: CloudTrail, Config, GuardDuty, Security Hub, Macie
• Infrastructure protection: VPC, Security Groups, NACLs, WAF, Shield, Network Firewall
• Data protection: KMS, ACM, Secrets Manager, S3 encryption, EBS encryption
• Incident response: Lambda auto-remediation via EventBridge, runbooks in Systems Manager

• Design principles: test recovery procedures, automatically recover from failure, scale horizontally, stop guessing capacity, manage change through automation
• Foundations: service quotas management, VPC design, network topology
• Workload architecture: distributed system design, loose coupling (SQS, SNS, EventBridge)
• Change management: Auto Scaling, CloudWatch alarms
• Failure management: backups (AWS Backup), DR strategies, multi-AZ, multi-region
• Key services: Route 53 (health checks + failover), ELB, ASG, RDS Multi-AZ, Aurora Global

• Design principles: democratize advanced technologies, go global in minutes, use serverless architectures, experiment more often, consider mechanical sympathy (use the right tool)
• Selection: choose right compute (Lambda vs EC2 vs containers), database (RDS vs DynamoDB vs ElastiCache), storage (S3 vs EBS vs EFS), network (CloudFront vs Global Accelerator)
• Review: evolve architecture as AWS releases new services
• Monitoring: CloudWatch metrics, X-Ray traces to identify bottlenecks
• Tradeoffs: use ElastiCache to trade consistency for performance; use read replicas to trade write freshness for read speed

• Design principles: implement cloud financial management, adopt consumption model (pay for what you use), measure overall efficiency, stop spending on undifferentiated heavy lifting, analyze and attribute expenditure
• Expenditure awareness: AWS Budgets, Cost Explorer, Cost and Usage Report, tagging strategy
• Cost-effective resources: Reserved Instances, Savings Plans, Spot Instances, right-sizing
• Manage demand: SQS to smooth spiky workloads, Auto Scaling to match demand
• Optimize over time: review new AWS services regularly; decommission unused resources

high-availability-patterns.text:
• Core principle: spread workload across at least 2 AZs so a single AZ failure doesn't take down the app
• Compute: Auto Scaling Group spanning multiple AZs + ALB distributing traffic
• Database: RDS Multi-AZ (synchronous standby), Aurora (6 copies across 3 AZs), ElastiCache Redis with Multi-AZ
• Storage: EFS (multi-AZ by default), S3 (multi-AZ by default), EBS (single AZ — must use snapshots for cross-AZ)
• NAT: one NAT Gateway per AZ for private subnet internet access HA
• Route 53: health checks + failover routing between AZs or regions

• Active-Passive: primary region handles all traffic; secondary region is warm standby — promote on disaster
• Active-Active: both regions handle traffic simultaneously; Route 53 latency or weighted routing
• Data replication: Aurora Global Database (< 1s RPO), DynamoDB Global Tables (multi-active), S3 CRR, RDS read replicas cross-region
• DNS: Route 53 geolocation or latency-based routing to direct users to nearest region
• Infrastructure: CloudFormation StackSets to deploy identical infra in multiple regions
• Considerations: data sovereignty, latency, cost of cross-region replication

• Synchronous coupling (tightly coupled): Service A calls Service B directly — B's failure causes A's failure
• Asynchronous decoupling: use a queue or event bus between services
• SQS decoupling: producer sends to queue; consumer processes independently — handles bursts, isolates failures
• SNS fan-out: one event triggers multiple consumers in parallel (SNS → multiple SQS queues)
• EventBridge: event-driven decoupling — services emit events, consumers react without knowing each other
• Step Functions: orchestrate multi-step workflows with built-in retry, error handling, and state management
• Pattern: spike in requests → SQS queue absorbs burst → Lambda or EC2 processes at steady rate

• EC2 Auto Scaling Group (ASG): scale EC2 capacity based on demand
  - Scaling policies: Target Tracking (maintain metric at target, e.g., 60% CPU), Step Scaling (based on alarm thresholds), Scheduled (predictable patterns), Predictive (ML forecast)
  - Cooldown period: time after scaling action before another can occur (default 300s)
  - Lifecycle hooks: pause instances during launch or termination for custom actions (install software, drain connections)
  - Health checks: EC2 health (default) or ELB health checks (more accurate for web apps)
• Application Auto Scaling: scale ECS services, Lambda concurrency, DynamoDB capacity, Aurora replicas, etc.
• Scale-out vs Scale-up: horizontal (add more instances) vs vertical (bigger instance) — prefer horizontal for HA

serverless-architecture-patterns.text:
• Classic serverless web API: Client → CloudFront → API Gateway → Lambda → DynamoDB
• Benefits: no server management, auto-scaling to zero, pay per use, built-in HA
• Lambda limitations to design around: 15-min timeout, 10 GB memory, 250 MB deployment size, cold starts
• For long-running tasks: Lambda triggers ECS Fargate task or Step Functions workflow
• For large files: S3 presigned URL (client uploads directly to S3, bypassing Lambda)

• S3 trigger: file uploaded to S3 → EventBridge or S3 Notification → Lambda for processing
• Stream processing: Kinesis Data Streams → Lambda (Event Source Mapping) → DynamoDB/S3
• Queue processing: SQS → Lambda → process messages; DLQ for failed messages
• Fan-out: SNS → multiple SQS queues → multiple Lambda functions (parallel processing)
• Scheduled tasks: EventBridge Scheduler → Lambda (replace cron jobs on EC2)
• Orchestration: Step Functions coordinates multiple Lambda functions with error handling

• API per service: each microservice has its own API Gateway + Lambda or ECS service
• Service discovery: ALB path-based routing (/users → service A, /orders → service B) or API Gateway
• Communication: synchronous (API Gateway/ALB), asynchronous (SQS/SNS/EventBridge)
• Strangler Fig pattern: gradually migrate monolith to microservices — route some paths to new services while keeping old ones running
• Sidecar pattern: deploy helper container alongside main container in ECS task (logging, monitoring, proxy)
• Service mesh: AWS App Mesh for observability and traffic control between ECS/EKS microservices

• Batch ETL: S3 (raw) → Glue ETL Job → S3 (processed) → Athena/Redshift
• Real-time ingestion: Kinesis Data Streams → Lambda/Firehose → S3/OpenSearch
• Lambda architecture: batch layer (S3 + Glue + Redshift) + speed layer (Kinesis + Lambda) + serving layer (DynamoDB/ElastiCache)
• Data lake: S3 (storage) + Glue (catalog + ETL) + Athena (query) + QuickSight (visualization)
• CDC pattern: RDS → DMS → DynamoDB or Redshift (replicate DB changes in near real-time)

caching-patterns.text:
• CDN caching (CloudFront): cache static and dynamic content at edge — reduces origin load, global low latency
• DNS caching: Route 53 TTL controls how long DNS responses are cached by resolvers
• Application caching (ElastiCache): cache DB query results, session data, computed values in memory
• Database caching: RDS Read Replicas offload read queries; Aurora reader endpoints
• API caching: API Gateway cache responses — reduces Lambda invocations and backend calls
• Object caching: DAX for DynamoDB microsecond reads

• Lazy Loading (Cache-Aside): read from cache; on miss, read from DB and write to cache
  - Pros: only requested data is cached; resilient to cache failures
  - Cons: cache miss penalty (3 trips), stale data possible
• Write-Through: write to cache AND DB simultaneously on every write
  - Pros: cache always up to date, no stale reads
  - Cons: cache may have data never read (wasted memory), write latency
• Write-Behind (Write-Back): write to cache first, async write to DB later
  - Pros: fast writes
  - Cons: risk of data loss if cache fails before DB write
• TTL: set expiry time on cached items — balance freshness vs performance

• Scenario: DB getting hammered by same read queries → ElastiCache (lazy loading or write-through)
• Scenario: DynamoDB latency needs to drop from ms to microseconds → DAX
• Scenario: reduce API Gateway Lambda invocations for slow-changing data → API Gateway caching
• Scenario: serve global users with low latency static assets → CloudFront + S3
• TRAP: caching can serve stale data — design TTL and invalidation strategy carefully
• TRAP: ElastiCache Redis vs Memcached — Redis for persistence, HA, complex data; Memcached for simple horizontal scaling
• TRAP: DAX is DynamoDB-only; you cannot use DAX with any other database

cross-account-and-multi-account-patterns.text:
• Best practice: use separate AWS accounts for different environments (dev, staging, prod) and workloads
• AWS Organizations: centrally manage accounts in a hierarchy (Root → OUs → Accounts)
• Benefits: blast radius isolation, separate billing, SCPs for guardrails, consolidated billing with volume discounts
• Landing Zone: automated multi-account setup following best practices — use AWS Control Tower
• Control Tower: sets up Organizations, SCPs, IAM Identity Center, CloudTrail, Config, logging account, security account automatically

• IAM Role Cross-Account: Account A assumes role in Account B → gets temp credentials → accesses B's resources
  - Account B: create role with trust policy allowing Account A
  - Account A: user/service needs sts:AssumeRole permission
• Resource-Based Policies: grant cross-account access directly on the resource (S3, KMS, Lambda, SQS, SNS, ECR)
  - No AssumeRole needed — principal from other account accesses directly
• AWS RAM: share resources (VPC subnets, TGW, Route 53 rules) without duplicating them
• S3 cross-account: bucket policy with explicit account/user principal
• CodePipeline cross-account: pipeline in account A deploys to account B using cross-account roles

• Scenario: developer in dev account needs read-only access to prod S3 → cross-account IAM role
• Scenario: enforce no one can disable CloudTrail in any account → SCP on root OU denying cloudtrail:StopLogging
• Scenario: share a VPC subnet across 5 accounts without peering → RAM VPC subnet sharing
• Scenario: set up new accounts automatically with security baseline → AWS Control Tower
• TRAP: SCPs do NOT apply to the management account — cannot restrict root/master account with SCPs
• TRAP: cross-account role requires trust from BOTH sides — role must trust the principal account AND the principal needs AssumeRole permission
• TRAP: resource-based policies can allow cross-account without AssumeRole, but the accessing principal still needs identity-based permission if the resource requires it

disaster-recovery-concepts.text:
• RPO (Recovery Point Objective): maximum acceptable amount of data loss measured in time
  - Example: RPO = 1 hour means you can tolerate losing up to 1 hour of data
  - Lower RPO = more frequent backups/replication = higher cost
• RTO (Recovery Time Objective): maximum acceptable time to restore service after a disaster
  - Example: RTO = 4 hours means the system must be back online within 4 hours
  - Lower RTO = more infrastructure pre-provisioned = higher cost
• The tighter the RPO and RTO requirements, the more expensive the DR strategy
• Exam tip: always map the scenario's RPO/RTO requirement to the appropriate DR strategy

• Four main strategies from cheapest/slowest to most expensive/fastest:
  1. Backup & Restore: lowest cost, highest RPO/RTO (hours)
  2. Pilot Light: minimal footprint always running, scale up on disaster (minutes to hours)
  3. Warm Standby: scaled-down but fully functional version always running (minutes)
  4. Multi-Site Active-Active: full capacity in multiple regions simultaneously (near zero RPO/RTO)
• Choose based on: RPO/RTO requirements, budget, compliance, criticality of workload

backup-and-restore.text:
• Cheapest DR strategy — no running infrastructure in DR region
• RPO: hours (depends on backup frequency); RTO: hours (time to provision + restore)
• How it works: regularly backup data to S3 (or another region via CRR); on disaster, provision new infrastructure and restore data
• Tools: AWS Backup, RDS automated snapshots copied cross-region, EC2 AMIs copied cross-region, S3 CRR
• CloudFormation: store templates in S3 or CodeCommit — redeploy entire infrastructure from scratch
• Route 53: update DNS to point to new region after recovery

• S3 Cross-Region Replication: automatically replicate objects to DR bucket
• RDS: enable automated backups → copy snapshots to DR region via Lambda + CloudWatch Events
• EC2 AMIs: copy AMIs to DR region — ensures you can launch identical instances
• Glacier: archive backups cheaply — good for compliance data with relaxed RTO
• Recovery procedure: restore snapshots → launch from AMIs → configure Route 53
• Test regularly: run DR drills to validate actual RTO matches target

pilot-light.text:
• A minimal version of the environment always running in DR region — like a pilot light in a gas heater
• Core components kept running: typically just the database (RDS Multi-Region replica, Aurora Global)
• Application servers NOT running — only launched during a disaster
• RPO: minutes (continuous DB replication); RTO: minutes to hours (time to launch app servers + update DNS)
• Cost: low — only pay for database running in DR region + stored AMIs

• Database: RDS Read Replica in DR region (promote on disaster) or Aurora Global Database
• App servers: pre-create AMIs and CloudFormation templates — launch quickly on failover
• DNS: Route 53 health check + failover routing — auto-switch to DR region when primary fails
• Elastic IPs: pre-allocate in DR region if static IPs needed
• Data sync: ensure any non-DB data (S3, EFS) is replicated to DR region
• Activation steps: promote DB replica → launch app servers from AMIs → update Route 53 or ELB

warm-standby.text:
• Scaled-down but fully functional version of the production environment always running in DR region
• All components running but at minimum capacity (e.g., 1 small EC2 vs production's 10 large EC2)
• RPO: near zero (continuous replication); RTO: minutes (scale up existing infrastructure)
• Cost: moderate — running all components at reduced capacity
• On disaster: scale up ASG, promote DB, switch DNS — much faster than Pilot Light

• Compute: ASG with minimum 1 instance in DR region — scale out on failover
• Database: Aurora Global Database or RDS cross-region replica (continuously replicating)
• Load balancer: ALB running but with fewer targets — add more on failover
• Route 53: weighted routing (99% primary, 1% standby) or health check failover
• Failover steps: scale ASG to production capacity → promote DB replica → adjust Route 53 weights
• Difference from Pilot Light: app servers ARE running (just small) vs Pilot Light where they are NOT running

multi-site-active-active.text:
• Full production capacity running simultaneously in 2+ regions — most expensive, lowest RPO/RTO
• RPO: near zero; RTO: near zero — traffic automatically rerouted on failure
• Users served from nearest/healthiest region at all times (performance + resilience)
• Cost: highest — full infrastructure cost in each region

• Database: Aurora Global Database (< 1s replication, < 1 min failover) or DynamoDB Global Tables (multi-active, multi-region)
• Traffic routing: Route 53 latency-based or geolocation routing; Global Accelerator for instant failover
• Data consistency: use eventual consistency carefully — DynamoDB Global Tables uses last-write-wins
• Session management: DynamoDB or ElastiCache for shared sessions across regions
• Conflict resolution: design app to handle concurrent writes from multiple regions
• CloudFormation StackSets: deploy identical infrastructure in all active regions

• Scenario: RPO = 0, RTO = 0, no data loss tolerated → Multi-Site Active-Active (Aurora Global + DynamoDB Global Tables + Route 53)
• Scenario: RPO = 1 hour, RTO = 4 hours, tight budget → Backup & Restore
• Scenario: critical app, RPO = minutes, RTO = minutes, moderate budget → Warm Standby
• Scenario: database must always be replicated, app can be launched on demand → Pilot Light
• TRAP: RDS Read Replica cross-region must be PROMOTED to become writable — it's read-only until promoted
• TRAP: Promoting RDS Read Replica to standalone instance severs the replication link — data diverges after promotion
• TRAP: Aurora Global Database failover < 1 minute; standard RDS cross-region failover takes longer
• TRAP: DynamoDB Global Tables is active-active (both regions writable); Aurora Global is active-passive (one primary writer region)

dr-services-reference.text:
• EC2: AMIs copied cross-region; Elastic IPs per region; ASG + ELB for HA within region
• RDS: automated backups to S3 (same region); copy snapshots cross-region; read replicas cross-region; Multi-AZ for HA (same region)
• Aurora: Multi-AZ by default; up to 5 cross-region read replicas; Aurora Global (< 1s RPO, < 1min RTO)
• DynamoDB: point-in-time recovery (35 days); on-demand backups; Global Tables (multi-region active-active)
• S3: CRR (cross-region replication) for automated replication; versioning + MFA Delete for protection
• EFS: AWS Backup for cross-region backup; DataSync for replication
• Route 53: health checks + failover routing; latency-based for active-active
• ElastiCache: Redis Multi-AZ (same region); backup/restore to S3; no native cross-region replication (rebuild from DB)

• Test DR regularly — untested DR plans often fail when needed
• Chaos engineering: intentionally inject failures (terminate instances, block AZ) to validate resilience
• AWS Fault Injection Simulator (FIS): managed chaos engineering — inject EC2 stop, CPU stress, network latency, AZ failure
• DR runbooks: document step-by-step recovery procedures in Systems Manager
• RTO validation: time your actual recovery to ensure it meets the target
• Backup validation: regularly restore from backups to confirm they are valid and complete
• Tabletop exercises: walk through DR scenarios with the team without executing them

ec2-cost-optimization.text:
• On-Demand: pay per second (Linux/Windows) or per hour; no commitment; highest price; use for unpredictable workloads
• Reserved Instances (RI): 1 or 3 year commitment; up to 72% discount vs On-Demand
  - Standard RI: highest discount; cannot change instance family/OS/tenancy
  - Convertible RI: lower discount (~54%); can change instance family, OS, tenancy, scope
  - Scope: Regional (flexibility across AZs) vs Zonal (capacity reservation in specific AZ)
• Savings Plans: commitment to $ spend per hour (not specific instance); more flexible than RIs
  - Compute Savings Plans: up to 66% discount; any instance family, region, OS, tenancy; also covers Lambda and Fargate
  - EC2 Instance Savings Plans: up to 72% discount; locked to instance family in a region; more flexible than Standard RI
• Spot Instances: up to 90% discount; can be interrupted with 2-min warning; for fault-tolerant, flexible workloads
• Dedicated Host: physical server dedicated to you; for BYOL (Bring Your Own License) compliance; most expensive
• Dedicated Instance: runs on hardware dedicated to you; may share hardware with other instances from same account
• Capacity Reservations: reserve On-Demand capacity in specific AZ; no billing discount alone — combine with RIs or Savings Plans

• Max price: set your max Spot price; instance runs while spot price < max price
• Spot Instance interruption: 2-minute warning before termination; can stop or hibernate instead of terminate
• Spot Fleets: mix of Spot + On-Demand instances across multiple pools; allocation strategies:
  - lowestPrice: cheapest pool (cost optimization)
  - diversified: spread across pools (availability)
  - capacityOptimized: pool with most available capacity (fewer interruptions)
  - priceCapacityOptimized: best balance of price and capacity (recommended)
• Spot Blocks: reserve Spot Instances for 1–6 hours without interruption (being deprecated)
• Use cases: batch jobs, data analysis, image processing, CI/CD, distributed computing
• NOT suitable for: databases, critical web servers, anything that can't handle interruption

• Right-sizing: match instance type/size to actual workload requirements — most common cost waste is oversized instances
• Tools: AWS Cost Explorer Rightsizing Recommendations, AWS Compute Optimizer
• Compute Optimizer: uses ML to analyze CloudWatch metrics → recommends optimal EC2, Lambda, ECS, EBS, Auto Scaling
• Stop unused instances: use AWS Instance Scheduler to auto-stop dev/test environments nights and weekends
• Graviton processors: ARM-based EC2 instances (M7g, C7g, R7g) — up to 40% better price/performance vs x86
• Key rule: for steady-state workloads → Reserved Instances or Savings Plans; for spiky → On-Demand + Auto Scaling; for flexible batch → Spot

storage-cost-optimization.text:
• Choose the right storage class: Standard → Standard-IA → Intelligent-Tiering → Glacier → Deep Archive
• S3 Intelligent-Tiering: automatically moves objects — best for unpredictable access patterns with no retrieval fee
• Lifecycle policies: auto-transition objects to cheaper classes after N days; expire (delete) old versions and incomplete multipart uploads
• S3 Inventory: identify objects that haven't been accessed and can be tiered or deleted
• Requester Pays: when sharing large datasets externally, make requester pay for transfer + requests
• S3 Select: retrieve only needed data with SQL — reduce data transfer cost vs downloading whole objects
• Multipart upload cleanup: incomplete multipart uploads accumulate storage cost — set lifecycle rule to delete after 7 days
• S3 Storage Lens: org-wide cost insights, identify buckets with high storage cost and low access

• EBS: delete unattached volumes (common waste — EC2 terminated but EBS left behind)
• EBS: use gp3 instead of gp2 — same performance, 20% cheaper; independently tune IOPS without over-provisioning
• EBS: use st1/sc1 for throughput-heavy sequential workloads instead of expensive io1/io2
• EBS Snapshots: move old snapshots to Snapshot Archive tier (75% cheaper) if rarely needed
• EFS: enable Lifecycle Management → move infrequently accessed files to EFS-IA (85% cheaper than Standard)
• EFS: use Elastic throughput mode to avoid paying for provisioned throughput you don't use

database-cost-optimization.text:
• Reserved DB Instances: 1 or 3 year commitment for RDS; up to 69% savings for steady-state databases
• Aurora Serverless v2: auto-scales in fine-grained increments — only pay for ACUs actually used; ideal for variable DB workloads
• Stop RDS/Aurora: stop instances when not needed (dev/test) — stopped instances save compute cost; still charged for storage
• Right-size: use Performance Insights + CloudWatch to identify underutilized RDS instances
• Use Aurora instead of RDS for MySQL/PostgreSQL at scale — more storage-efficient architecture
• RDS Proxy: reduce connection overhead — allows Lambda to reuse connections; reduces cost of over-provisioning for connection handling

• Capacity modes:
  - On-Demand: pay per request — good for unpredictable traffic; more expensive per request but no idle cost
  - Provisioned + Auto Scaling: cheaper per request; set min/max capacity; best for predictable steady traffic
• DynamoDB Reserved Capacity: purchase reserved read/write capacity units for 1 or 3 years — up to 76% savings
• TTL: automatically delete expired items — reduces storage cost at no charge
• DAX: reduces DynamoDB read costs for hot-key workloads by serving from cache
• Data modeling: efficient access patterns reduce RCU/WCU consumption — single-table design avoids expensive scans
• Avoid Scan operations: use Query (targeted) instead of Scan (reads entire table) — massive cost and performance difference

network-cost-optimization.text:
• Data transfer IN to AWS: always free (from internet to EC2, S3, etc.)
• Data transfer OUT to internet: charged per GB (varies by region and service)
• Data transfer between AZs (same region): charged ~$0.01/GB each direction for EC2; free for some managed services
• Data transfer between regions: charged higher rate
• Data transfer within same AZ: free
• S3 to CloudFront: free (no charge for S3 → CloudFront origin fetch)
• CloudFront to internet: cheaper than EC2/S3 direct — use CloudFront to reduce egress costs
• PrivateLink (Interface Endpoints): charged per hour + per GB — Gateway Endpoints for S3/DynamoDB are FREE

• Use S3 Gateway Endpoints: eliminate NAT Gateway data processing costs for S3 traffic from private subnets
• CloudFront: serve content from edge — reduces origin data transfer and improves performance
• Minimize cross-AZ traffic: keep app servers and DB in same AZ where possible for latency-sensitive tiers
• NAT Gateway costs: charged per hour ($0.045) + per GB processed ($0.045) — can be significant for high-throughput apps
  - Replace with VPC Interface Endpoint for AWS service traffic where possible
  - Use S3 Gateway Endpoint to eliminate S3 traffic through NAT Gateway
• Direct Connect: for high-volume data transfer between on-prem and AWS — lower per-GB rate than internet egress
• Compression: compress data before transfer to reduce GB processed charges

cost-management-tools.text:
• AWS Cost Explorer: visualize spending by service, region, account, tag; 12-month history + 12-month forecast; rightsizing + RI/SP recommendations
• AWS Cost and Usage Report (CUR): most granular data — every resource, every hour; query with Athena
• AWS Budgets: set spending/usage/RI/SP budgets; alert before or when threshold hit; Budget Actions for automated response
• AWS Compute Optimizer: ML-based right-sizing for EC2, Lambda, ECS, EBS, ASG — identifies over-provisioned resources
• Cost Allocation Tags: tag resources (Project, Team, Environment) → activate tags → filter costs by tag in Cost Explorer and CUR
• AWS Trusted Advisor: cost optimization checks — idle resources, underutilized EC2, unassociated Elastic IPs, old snapshots

• RI Utilization report: track how much of your reserved capacity is being used
• RI Coverage report: % of On-Demand hours covered by RIs — identify where to buy more RIs
• Savings Plans recommendations: Cost Explorer suggests optimal commitment amount and estimated savings
• RI Marketplace: sell unused Standard RIs to other AWS customers
• Convertible RIs: exchange for different instance family, OS, or tenancy — useful when requirements change
• Key rule: use Savings Plans (more flexible) for new commitments; Standard RIs for specific instance families with known long-term need

• Scenario: reduce cost for steady-state production EC2 running 24/7 → Reserved Instances or Savings Plans
• Scenario: reduce cost for dev environment running 8h/day → auto-stop with Instance Scheduler (saves ~67%)
• Scenario: reduce cost for fault-tolerant batch processing → Spot Instances
• Scenario: identify which team is spending the most → Cost Allocation Tags + Cost Explorer
• Scenario: get recommendations to downsize over-provisioned EC2 → Compute Optimizer or Cost Explorer Rightsizing
• TRAP: Convertible RIs are less discounted than Standard RIs — choose Standard when instance needs are fixed
• TRAP: Savings Plans cover Lambda and Fargate (Compute SP) — RIs do not
• TRAP: unused RI capacity is wasted — monitor RI utilization and exchange/sell unused RIs
• TRAP: Capacity Reservations alone don't provide a discount — must combine with Regional RI or Savings Plan

key-numbers-and-limits.text:
• Lambda: max timeout 900s (15 min); memory 128 MB–10,240 MB; /tmp storage 512 MB–10,240 MB; deployment package 50 MB zipped / 250 MB unzipped; default concurrency 1,000/region
• EC2: max 5 Elastic IPs per region (soft limit); max 5 VPCs per region (soft limit); max 20 On-Demand instances per region (default, can increase)
• EBS gp2: max 16,000 IOPS; gp3: max 16,000 IOPS + 1,000 MB/s independently; io2 Block Express: max 256,000 IOPS
• EBS: max volume size 64 TB (io2 Block Express); max snapshot 1 PB
• EC2 User Data: max 16 KB

• S3: max object size 5 TB; single PUT max 5 GB; multipart recommended > 100 MB, required > 5 GB
• S3 Standard-IA: minimum storage 30 days, minimum object size 128 KB
• S3 Glacier Flexible: minimum storage 90 days
• S3 Glacier Deep Archive: minimum storage 180 days
• S3 bucket: unlimited objects; max 100 buckets per account (soft limit, increase to 1,000)
• EFS: max file system size 8 EB; max throughput Elastic mode: 3 GB/s read, 1 GB/s write
• EBS snapshot: incremental; first snapshot is full copy

• RDS: max 5 read replicas per instance; Multi-AZ failover ~1–2 minutes; automated backup retention 0–35 days
• Aurora: 6 copies of data across 3 AZs; up to 15 read replicas; max storage 128 TB; Aurora Global RPO < 1s, RTO < 1 min
• DynamoDB: max item size 400 KB; max 20 GSIs + 5 LSIs per table; PITR up to 35 days
• ElastiCache Redis: max 500 nodes per cluster; max 6.1 TB per node (some instance types)
• Redshift: max 128 nodes per cluster; RA3 nodes decouple compute/storage
• DynamoDB On-Demand: max 40,000 RCUs + 40,000 WCUs per table (default, can increase)

• VPC: max /16 CIDR (65,536 IPs); min /28 CIDR (16 IPs); 5 reserved IPs per subnet
• Security Groups: max 5 per ENI; max 60 inbound + 60 outbound rules per SG (default)
• NACLs: evaluated in rule number order; up to 20 rules per NACL (default)
• VPC Peering: max 125 peering connections per VPC
• Transit Gateway: max 5,000 VPC attachments; max 10,000 routes per route table
• API Gateway: default 10,000 RPS, 5,000 burst; max 29-second integration timeout
• CloudFront: max 25 behaviors per distribution; default 10 GB/s per distribution
• ALB: supports up to 100 targets per target group (default); max 100 rules per ALB listener

• SQS: max message size 256 KB (use Extended Client Library + S3 for larger); retention 1 min–14 days (default 4 days); visibility timeout 0 sec–12 hours (default 30s); max long poll wait 20s
• SQS FIFO: max 300 TPS without batching; 3,000 TPS with batching
• SNS: max 256 KB message size; max 12.5 million subscriptions per topic; max 100,000 topics
• Kinesis Data Streams: 1 MB/s write per shard; 2 MB/s read per shard; max 1,000 records/s per shard; retention 1–365 days
• EventBridge: max 300 rules per event bus; max 5 targets per rule (default)
• Step Functions Standard: max 1 year duration; Express: max 5 minutes

• IAM: max 10 managed policies per user/group/role; max 5,000 IAM users per account; max 1,000 IAM roles per account (default)
• STS temporary credentials: min 15 min, max 12 hours (36 hours for some role types)
• KMS: max 4 KB payload for Encrypt/Decrypt directly; use envelope encryption for larger data; key deletion waiting period 7–30 days
• ACM: certificates are regional except CloudFront (must be in us-east-1)
• Secrets Manager: max 65,536 bytes per secret value; max 40,000 secrets per region (default)
• GuardDuty: 30-day free trial; findings retained for 90 days

most-common-exam-traps.text:
• Lambda in VPC loses internet access — must add NAT Gateway for outbound internet + VPC for private resources
• Lambda timeout is 15 min max — cannot use for long-running jobs; use Fargate or Batch
• Increasing Lambda memory also increases CPU (proportional) — sometimes faster + cheaper overall
• SQS visibility timeout should be 6x Lambda timeout — prevents duplicate processing on slow executions
• API Gateway hard timeout is 29 seconds — Lambda must respond within 29s for sync calls
• EC2 User Data runs once on first launch only — not on every restart
• Spot Instance interruption notice is 2 minutes — not enough time for long checkpoints
• Reserved Instances apply to any instance in the family within the region (Regional RI) — even if in different AZ

• EBS volumes are AZ-locked — cannot attach to EC2 in different AZ; use snapshots to move
• EBS st1 and sc1 (HDD) cannot be boot volumes — only SSD types can
• S3 is not a file system — it's object storage; no true directory hierarchy
• S3 Standard-IA and One Zone-IA have minimum 30-day storage charge — don't use for short-lived objects
• S3 CRR and SRR require versioning on BOTH source and destination buckets
• RDS Multi-AZ is for HA/failover NOT for read scaling — use Read Replicas for reads
• RDS Read Replicas are asynchronous — slight replication lag; not suitable for zero-lag requirements
• DynamoDB Scan reads the ENTIRE table — very expensive; always use Query with partition key
• DynamoDB item size max is 400 KB — store large objects in S3 and reference them from DynamoDB
• Promoting RDS Read Replica to standalone severs the replication link permanently

• NAT Gateway is in the PUBLIC subnet — not the private subnet
• Security Groups are STATEFUL; NACLs are STATELESS — NACLs need explicit inbound + outbound rules
• Security Groups can only ALLOW — they cannot explicitly deny; use NACLs for explicit deny
• VPC Peering is NOT transitive — A↔B and B↔C does NOT give A↔C connectivity
• CNAME cannot be used at the zone apex (root domain) — use Route 53 Alias record instead
• Route 53 Alias records are FREE; CNAME records cost per query
• Geolocation routing ≠ Latency routing — Geolocation routes by WHERE you are; Latency routes by what's FASTEST
• Direct Connect is NOT encrypted by default — add VPN over Direct Connect for encryption
• CloudFront ACM certificate MUST be in us-east-1 — a very common exam trap
• Gateway VPC Endpoints (S3, DynamoDB) are FREE; Interface Endpoints cost money

• Explicit Deny ALWAYS wins in IAM — no Allow can override a Deny
• SCPs do NOT apply to the management (master) account — only to member accounts
• SCPs do NOT grant permissions — they only restrict maximum permissions
• Permission Boundaries do NOT grant permissions — they set maximum allowed
• IAM policies are evaluated as: SCP ∩ Identity Policy ∩ Resource Policy ∩ Permission Boundary
• KMS key deletion has 7–30 day waiting period and is IRREVERSIBLE — encrypted data is lost forever
• ACM public certificates cannot have their private keys exported — they live in ACM only
• Cognito User Pools = authentication (JWT tokens); Identity Pools = AWS authorization (temp credentials)
• GuardDuty detects threats but does NOT block them — must use Lambda/WAF/SGs for automated blocking
• Macie is S3-only — does not scan EBS, RDS, EFS, or DynamoDB

• Convertible RIs offer less discount than Standard RIs — pay for flexibility
• Savings Plans cover Lambda and Fargate; Reserved Instances do NOT
• Data transfer IN to AWS is free; OUT to internet is charged
• S3 to CloudFront is free; CloudFront to internet is cheaper than direct S3 egress
• One NAT Gateway per AZ for HA — a single NAT Gateway is a single point of failure
• Cross-AZ traffic within a VPC is charged (~$0.01/GB) — keep tightly coupled services in same AZ
• EFS Standard-IA has a per-retrieval fee — if files are accessed frequently, Standard is cheaper
• Aurora Global Database failover is < 1 min; DynamoDB Global Tables is active-active (no failover needed)
• Warm Standby has app servers RUNNING (scaled down); Pilot Light does NOT have app servers running

service-selection-cheatsheet.text:
• Need to run code < 15 min without managing servers → Lambda
• Need persistent long-running server, full OS control → EC2
• Need containers without managing EC2 → ECS on Fargate
• Need Kubernetes → EKS
• Need long-running batch jobs → AWS Batch
• Need simple app deployment without infrastructure knowledge → Elastic Beanstalk
• Need containers, want to manage underlying EC2 → ECS on EC2 launch type
• Need serverless containers with per-second billing → Fargate

• Need relational database (MySQL/PostgreSQL), managed → RDS
• Need best performance relational DB on AWS → Aurora
• Need serverless relational DB → Aurora Serverless v2
• Need key-value / document NoSQL, serverless, any scale → DynamoDB
• Need in-memory caching → ElastiCache (Redis for HA/persistence, Memcached for simple scaling)
• Need DynamoDB microsecond latency → DAX
• Need MongoDB-compatible managed DB → DocumentDB
• Need petabyte-scale data warehouse → Redshift
• Need search / full-text / log analytics → OpenSearch Service
• Need graph database → Amazon Neptune
• Need time series database → Amazon Timestream

• Need object storage at any scale → S3
• Need block storage for EC2 (single instance) → EBS
• Need shared file storage for Linux (multiple EC2) → EFS
• Need shared file storage for Windows (SMB) → FSx for Windows File Server
• Need high-performance parallel file system (HPC, ML) → FSx for Lustre
• Need archival at lowest cost → S3 Glacier Deep Archive
• Need hybrid on-prem + cloud file storage → Storage Gateway (S3 File Gateway)
• Need to migrate on-prem files to AWS efficiently → DataSync
• Need to transfer large amounts of data (offline, > 10 TB) → Snowball Edge
• Need to transfer petabytes offline → Snowmobile

service-selection-cheatsheet.part: When to Use What: Messaging & Integration
service-selection-cheatsheet.text:
• Need to decouple services with a queue (1:1) → SQS
• Need to fan-out one message to multiple consumers → SNS + SQS
• Need strict FIFO message ordering + exactly-once → SQS FIFO
• Need event-driven routing across services / SaaS → EventBridge
• Need real-time data streaming (sub-second) → Kinesis Data Streams
• Need to deliver streaming data to S3/Redshift/OpenSearch → Kinesis Data Firehose
• Need to orchestrate multi-step workflows with retries → Step Functions
• Need GraphQL API with real-time subscriptions → AppSync
• Need REST / HTTP / WebSocket API managed → API Gateway
• Need managed Kafka → Amazon MSK

service-selection-cheatsheet.part: When to Use What: Security & Access
service-selection-cheatsheet.text:
• Need to manage who can access AWS services (internal users) → IAM
• Need to manage who can access your app (external users) → Cognito
• Need SSO across multiple AWS accounts → IAM Identity Center
• Need to detect threats in AWS environment → GuardDuty
• Need to scan EC2/ECR/Lambda for vulnerabilities → Inspector
• Need to find sensitive data in S3 → Macie
• Need to aggregate security findings → Security Hub
• Need to investigate root cause of security incident → Detective
• Need to manage encryption keys → KMS
• Need dedicated HSM hardware → CloudHSM
• Need to store/rotate DB credentials → Secrets Manager
• Need to store config/non-sensitive parameters → SSM Parameter Store
• Need SSL/TLS certificates for AWS services → ACM
